SAP Portfolio Briefing | May 2026 · FY2026 |
YTD Consumed $39.0M YTD Budget $44.6M YTD ACV $50.7M | Proj Consumed $93.7M Proj Budget $108.3M Proj ACV $121.6M
AbbVie Inc
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.4M
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD ACV: $1.9M
Projected Consumed: $3.3M
Projected Budget: $4.5M
Projected ACV: $4.6M
EA Insights and Actions
AbbVie has fully dormant BTP entitlements (~$82K Technology Foundation, dual-zero Public and Database Cloud agreements) sitting underneath active SAC BI, SAC Planning, Concur, Ariba, and Traceability Hub workloads — the integration, data, and AI fabric meant to unify these domains is contracted but missing, meaning every L1 area is operating as an isolated point solution rather than a connected pharma enterprise platform.
The compounding zero-state of Integration Suite, Datasphere, and Joule Studio means AbbVie's agentic AI and clean-core extensibility ambitions are architecturally blocked at the foundation — SAC BI plateaus at ~$30K/month, SAC Planning is stuck at 57%, and Batch Release Hub cannot close the loop with Traceability Hub, because none of them have the brokered integration layer they were designed to consume.
AbbVie's GxP compliance spine is half-built — Traceability Hub runs serialization at a flat $40K plateau (with an unresolved -$40K May reversal) while Batch Release Hub sits at $0, leaving the quality-release-to-distribution chain broken on paper-based batch disposition and exposing the pharma manufacturing core to regulatory and recall risk that the contracted architecture was sized to eliminate.
AbbVie's highest-consuming domain — Finance & Spend at 115–125% on the Concur stack and 109% on Ariba Buying — is simultaneously its most architecturally exposed, with Ariba SLP decaying to 81%, SSO dormant across the entire identity surface, and no BTP fabric beneath FP&A, meaning the LoB delivering the most contract value is running on weakening supplier governance, fragmented identity, and a capped planning model.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with AbbVie's platform sponsor to activate Integration Suite as the single foundational workload that unblocks SAC BI expansion, SAC Planning unification, Joule-led analytics, and the Batch Release Hub–Traceability Hub closed loop in one sequenced roadmap.
Launch a cross-domain BTP activation governance cadence with named technical owners for Integration Suite, Datasphere, Joule Studio, and SAP Build, tying each pillar to a specific downstream LoB use case (FP&A, analytics, batch release, citizen extensions) so dormant entitlement converts to measurable burn.
Commission a GxP compliance architecture workshop to stand up Batch Release Hub on AbbVie's quality release process, integrate it with Traceability Hub, and root-cause the May -$40,034 reversal before year-end attainment is restated.
Run a Finance & Spend architectural true-up covering Concur capacity ceilings, Ariba SLP supplier governance restoration, and SSO activation across the highest-traffic Concur and Ariba applications to protect the LoB carrying AbbVie's largest realized contract value.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
48.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$160K
YTD Budget$332K
YTD ACV: $334K
Projected Consumed: $385K · Projected Budget: $839K · Projected ACV: $802K
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Data & AI foundation is architecturally inverted — SAC BI is consuming analytics at a capped ~$30K/month plateau while both BTP Enterprise Agreements (Public and Database Cloud) sit at $0, meaning dashboards are being served without the underlying integration, extension, and data platform that BTP is designed to provide, leaving analytics disconnected from governed enterprise data flows.
The dual-zero BTP posture across Public and Database Cloud agreements signals that AbbVie has contracted the agentic AI, automation, and Datasphere capabilities meant to feed SAC BI with trusted, contextualized data — but none of those pipelines exist, which is why SAC adoption has stalled at a narrow user base rather than expanding into AI-driven analytics with business context.
SAC BI's flat $384K projection against a $798K budget reflects a consumption ceiling driven by use-case scarcity, not licensing gaps — the LoB has reporting visibility for an established analyst population but no architectural mechanism (BTP Integration Suite, Datasphere) to onboard net-new domains or operationalize Joule-led analytics.
With ~$40K of combined BTP entitlement unconsumed and ~$413K of SAC BI value at risk, the Data & AI line of business is on track to forfeit over half of its contracted analytics platform value, blocking the intended outcome of unified analytics, planning, and AI grounded in SAP business context.
Stand up a joint BTP + SAC BI architecture workshop with AbbVie's Data & AI leadership to define one foundational data product on BTP (Datasphere or Integration Suite) that directly feeds a net-new SAC BI dashboard, breaking both the $0 BTP consumption and the SAC plateau with a single use case.
Activate the first production workload on each BTP Enterprise Agreement (Public and Database Cloud) tied to AbbVie's existing analytics pipeline so the entitlement becomes the integration and data backbone for SAC BI rather than remaining a dormant platform contract.
Audit SAC BI provisioned versus active licenses and pair the dormant user population with a guided enablement track focused on planning and Joule-driven analytics to lift monthly consumption toward the $66K budget line.
Build a sequenced Data & AI consumption ramp that links BTP service enablement (Integration Suite, Build, Datasphere) to specific SAC BI expansion scenarios, converting idle platform entitlement into measurable analytics outcomes for AbbVie's LoBs.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
19.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$109
YTD Budget$547
YTD ACV: $405
Projected Consumed: $261 · Projected Budget: $84K · Projected ACV: $971
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's entire Technology Foundation is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — BTP Enterprise Agreement shows $0 consumption across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and core Technology Foundation against a combined $82,808 annual budget, meaning the platform meant to power agentic AI, integration, and extensibility across SAP and non-SAP estates is not yet a functioning foundation for any LoB workload.
The Integration Suite dormancy is the most consequential gap — with $28,986 unconsumed, AbbVie has no activated SAP-native integration backbone, which blocks every downstream extension, automation, and AI use case that depends on brokered process and data flows across the SAP landscape.
SAP Build is a stalled pilot rather than a scaled capability — $108 of $1,242 consumed with five consecutive zero-consumption months after Jan/Feb indicates the low-code extensibility layer was lit up for a proof point but never operationalized for citizen developers or process automation, leaving business users without a sanctioned path to extend SAP processes.
Joule Studio sitting at $0 against $42,701 means the agentic AI development capability of BTP is completely unrealized, so AbbVie is paying for AI-context tooling while having no integration fabric (Integration Suite also dormant) for those agents to act on — the AI and integration gaps compound each other.
Convene a BTP activation workshop anchored on a single named integration workload in Integration Suite, since this is the architectural prerequisite that unblocks Joule Studio, SAP Build, and the broader Technology Foundation entitlement.
Re-baseline SAP Build with 2–3 concrete extension or workflow use cases tied to live SAP processes, converting the dormant low-code entitlement into recurring citizen-developer activity rather than a one-time pilot.
Escalate the $82,808 aggregate BTP non-start to AbbVie's executive platform sponsor and secure a named technical owner per BTP pillar (Integration, Build, Joule Studio) to break the zero-consumption pattern at the foundation level.
Stand up a BTP consumption governance cadence that tracks workload activation milestones against monthly budget, ensuring the architectural intent of BTP as the unifying extension and AI fabric is enforced through measurable burn.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
97.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.1M
YTD Budget$1.1M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.6M · Projected Budget: $2.7M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Travel & Expense stack (Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting, TripLink, Direct Travel, Company Bill, Conn/Stat/Web Extract, Preferred Success, WalkMe) is uniformly running 115–125% of plan, which architecturally confirms Concur is the embedded system-of-record for AbbVie's T&E lifecycle from capture through audit to reporting — the LoB is realizing full-stack value, but every contract ceiling is now the binding constraint rather than user adoption.
The Procure-to-Pay process is architecturally lopsided — Ariba Buying & Invoicing is over-consuming at 109% while Ariba SLP is decaying from 106% to 81% attainment, meaning AbbVie is transacting heavily against suppliers whose qualification, segmentation, and performance governance is weakening, eroding the upstream supplier-quality foundation that Buying & Invoicing depends on.
The Financial Planning & Analysis domain is operating at half-capacity with SAC Planning stuck at 57% utilization and BTP Enterprise Agreement fully dormant — architecturally, this means AbbVie has a planning UI without the extensibility, integration, and data fabric BTP is meant to provide, capping SAC at standalone finance plans rather than enabling unified workforce, capex, and operational planning.
Single Sign-on remains fully dormant against the Concur and Ariba estate that is driving real consumption, leaving AbbVie's most heavily-used Finance and Spend applications without the consolidated identity layer SSO is architected to deliver — a security and user-experience gap that grows with every additional active Concur and Ariba user.
Consolidate the Concur portfolio into a single capacity true-up covering Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting, TripLink, Direct Travel, Company Bill, Preferred Success, and WalkMe to right-size ceilings against the demonstrated 115–125% run-rate before contract throttling disrupts T&E operations.
Re-engage AbbVie procurement on Ariba SLP supplier onboarding, segmentation, and performance review cadences to restore upstream supplier governance and protect the integrity of the Ariba Buying & Invoicing transaction flow.
Activate the BTP Enterprise Agreement against a named FP&A use case (SAC data integration, planning extensions, or external data ingestion) so that SAC Planning can break its $35K/month consumption ceiling by unifying financial and operational planning models.
Launch an SSO activation initiative scoped to AbbVie's highest-traffic Concur and Ariba applications, converting the dormant LPR364 entitlement into a measurable authentication consolidation layer across the Finance and Spend estate.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
32.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$122K
YTD Budget$379K
YTD ACV: $269K
Projected Consumed: $292K · Projected Budget: $867K · Projected ACV: $645K
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Make & Deliver compliance architecture is half-wired — SAP Traceability Hub is running serialization at a fixed $40K monthly footprint while Batch Release Hub sits at $0 consumed, meaning the LoB can track product movement post-release but is still executing the GxP batch disposition decision itself on legacy paper-based processes, breaking the closed-loop quality-to-traceability chain SAP intended.
Traceability Hub adoption is architecturally narrow rather than enterprise-wide — flat $39.8K–$41.7K consumption against a rising budget curve indicates AbbVie is using serialization for a fixed slice of SKUs or trade lanes, leaving brand protection, recall streamlining, and multi-component traceability capabilities under-leveraged across the broader pharma portfolio.
The May -$40,034 reversal on Traceability Hub is a material data integrity event that distorts the LoB's true serialization run-rate, and until root-caused it masks whether the underlying compliance workload is stable, declining, or contractually disputed.
With $0 consumed on Batch Release Hub against a $480 annual budget, the electronic batch record and release decision dashboard capabilities are contracted but architecturally dormant, meaning AbbVie is paying for digitized quality release while order fulfillment lead time and compliance cost reductions remain unrealized.
Launch a Batch Release Hub activation program with AbbVie's quality release organization to move the core batch disposition workflow off paper and onto the electronic release decision dashboard, unlocking the GxP digitization the contract was sized for.
Open a data integrity investigation on the May -$40,034 Traceability Hub posting to determine whether it is a reversal, credit, or failed transaction, and restate true May consumption before any year-end attainment projection.
Drive a Traceability Hub scope-expansion workstream targeting additional product lines, markets, and regulatory reporting flows to lift consumption off the $40K plateau and convert the platform from a narrow serialization tool into the enterprise traceability backbone.
Architect the Batch Release Hub–Traceability Hub integration so released batches flow directly into the serialization and track-and-trace layer, closing the quality-to-distribution loop and giving AbbVie a single compliance spine across Make & Deliver.
Abbott Laboratories
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.5M
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD ACV: $2.1M
Projected Consumed: $3.7M
Projected Budget: $4.3M
Projected ACV: $5.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Abbott's enterprise architecture is consistently top-heavy across every L1 — SAC BI overshoots while HANA Cloud lags, Cloud for Customer underperforms while Incentive Management runs at 99.8%, Preferred Success Ariba overconsumes while transactional Ariba modules sit at 22–55%, and SAP Build flatlines while Integration Suite runs hot — revealing a customer that buys consumption and advisory layers aggressively but fails to operationalize the foundational data, transactional, and extensibility layers underneath, which structurally caps the ROI on a roughly $3.5M+ annual SAP commitment.
The integration backbone is load-bearing for a fabric that does not yet exist — Integration Suite is at 107–113% attainment but HANA Cloud sits at 65%, Data Intelligence Cloud is narrowly scoped, and SAP Build is dormant, meaning Abbott is paying to move data and events across systems without a unified data foundation to land them in or low-code surfaces to act on them, blocking the agentic AI and closed-loop decisioning outcomes BTP was contracted to deliver.
Every L1 domain shows the same closed-loop break — CRM signals don't feed Incentive Management, Supplier Risk doesn't guard Sourcing and Buying, IBP planning outputs have no confirmed downstream execution link, and SAC Planning is siloed to finance — indicating Abbott has bought integrated SAP suites but is operating them as disconnected point tools, undermining the unified front-to-back process visibility expected of a global life sciences manufacturer navigating supply resilience and compliance pressures.
Against ~$865K remaining IBP burn, a $99K SAC Planning YTD gap, a $63K HANA shortfall, and a $70K Cloud for Customer shortfall, Abbott's YTD consumption pattern shows the meter is running on platforms whose strategic modules (agentic AI, unified planning, supplier risk, citizen development, S&OP response) remain dormant — converting what should be capability investments into pure budget drawdown with no architectural compounding.
Convene a cross-LoB enterprise architecture review anchored on HANA Cloud and Integration Suite as the shared foundation, mapping each L1 domain's dormant modules (Supplier Risk, IBP response, SAC Planning workforce/capex, Cloud for Customer territory/case, Joule) to the data and integration dependencies they require.
Launch a BTP extensibility activation program pairing SAP Build use cases with the existing Integration Suite flows, seeding 5–8 business-technologist apps that consume CRM, procurement, and planning APIs to convert dormant low-code capacity into front-end value across all L1 domains.
Run a portfolio-level true-forward conversation that rebalances over-consuming consumption layers (SAC BI, Preferred Success Ariba, Integration Suite) against underperforming foundations (HANA Cloud, Ariba transactional, Cloud for Customer, SAP Build), tying any uplift commitments to activation milestones rather than absorbing overages as standalone spend.
Establish enterprise-wide adoption telemetry across IBP modules, Cloud for Customer workflows, Ariba source-to-pay stages, and HANA Cloud workloads so Abbott measures architectural completeness by business decisions enabled rather than by metered dollars consumed.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
74.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$66K
YTD Budget$89K
YTD ACV: $113K
Projected Consumed: $159K · Projected Budget: $219K · Projected ACV: $272K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI stack is architecturally inverted at Abbott — HANA Cloud (the foundational data fabric for AI and analytics) is running at 65% attainment with a $63K projected annual shortfall while SAC BI (the consumption layer that sits on top of it) is overshooting by 15%, meaning the LoB is generating analytical demand faster than it is landing mission-critical data into the platform that should feed it.
Data Intelligence Cloud is operationalized but narrowly scoped — on-plan 99.1% attainment with workload-driven swings indicates a fixed set of pipelines is in production, yet the static footprint suggests Abbott has not extended DI Cloud as the integration backbone feeding HANA Cloud, which explains why HANA's contracted capacity remains dormant.
SAC BI's 112% Q1 burn against a flat HANA Cloud base signals report and model proliferation on a thin data foundation — the LoB is getting visualization value but risks decisions made on a narrow slice of mission-critical data rather than the full business data fabric the architecture was contracted to deliver.
The agentic AI and unified planning capabilities embedded in HANA Cloud and SAC (Joule, business data fabric, financial+operational planning) are effectively dormant — adoption is concentrated in classic BI visualization and steady-state integration, leaving the AI-grounded decisioning outcomes the Data & AI investment was sized for unrealized.
Convene a HANA Cloud workload expansion review with Abbott's data platform team to migrate additional data products and analytics workloads onto the existing instance, closing the $27.7K YTD gap and anchoring SAC BI consumption on the contracted data fabric.
Re-scope Data Intelligence Cloud pipelines to land net-new sources into HANA Cloud, converting DI Cloud's stable run-rate into a feeder for the underutilized database tier rather than an isolated integration layer.
Run an SAC BI consumption and modeling review to identify the users and stories driving the projected $4,153 overage, and reconcile whether report proliferation is being served by HANA Cloud or by siloed datasets bypassing the architecture.
Open a true-forward conversation that rebalances the Data & AI contract — uplift SAC BI capacity to match adoption while replanning HANA Cloud against actual workload, and position Joule and agentic AI use cases to activate the dormant AI-grounded capabilities.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$137K
YTD Budget$129K
YTD ACV: $135K
Projected Consumed: $328K · Projected Budget: $286K · Projected ACV: $323K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally lopsided — Integration Suite is running hot at 107–113% attainment as the connective tissue for active workloads, while SAP Build is flatlined at ~40% attainment on a single static app, meaning Abbott is scaling integrations to systems and data but not building the low-code experiences and workflows that would let business users act on them.
SAP Build's locked $112–$144 monthly band is the signature of a deployed-but-dormant citizen development capability — the platform is provisioned and one workload is live, but the Center of Excellence motion, template library, and business-technologist pipeline that convert Build into a recurring extension layer for core SAP processes are absent, leaving Abbott paying for a low-code factory that is producing nothing new.
Integration Suite's sustained $44K projected overrun against a $282K budget signals it has become load-bearing for Abbott's API and agent-ready data fabric, but without a usage decomposition the LoB cannot distinguish strategic net-new integrations from inefficient flow designs — creating both a true-up risk and an architectural blind spot on what the integration backbone is actually carrying.
The Build–Integration Suite pairing is the intended extensibility pattern for BTP — Integration Suite exposes the APIs and Build consumes them into apps and workflows — and Abbott is only exercising one half, which caps the ROI on the integration spend because there are no new front-end experiences pulling value from the integrations being scaled.
Run a joint SAP Build and Integration Suite architecture review with Abbott to map which active integration flows lack a consuming app or workflow, and seed 3–5 Build use cases that monetize the integration backbone already in production.
Stand up an SAP Build Center of Excellence track covering developer onboarding, a reusable template and connector library, and governance to convert the current static workload into a recurring pipeline of business-technologist apps.
Execute an Integration Suite usage decomposition to classify message volume by interface, business process, and design efficiency, separating net-new integration demand from remediable flow inefficiencies before the true-up conversation.
Initiate a capacity right-sizing discussion for Integration Suite using the consistent 107–113% attainment as the baseline, and bundle it with a Build expansion commit so the contract reflects the full extensibility architecture rather than just the integration layer.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
59.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$198K
YTD Budget$332K
YTD ACV: $384K
Projected Consumed: $476K · Projected Budget: $799K · Projected ACV: $923K
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Customer Experience architecture is bifurcated — SAP Incentive Management is operationally embedded at near-perfect 99.8% attainment through April, proving the back-office sales compensation engine is mature, while Cloud for Customer's front-office CRM layer is stuck at ~60% attainment, meaning sellers are being paid accurately on deals the system has limited visibility into pipeline-wise.
Cloud for Customer is contracted as the system of engagement for sales and service but is being consumed as a partial CRM — the steady climb from 53.6% to 62.7% followed by May's regression to 60.6% indicates Abbott has activated core record-keeping but has not operationalized the territory, activity, and case management workflows that justify the $13,800/month budget, leaving a projected $70,500 annual shortfall.
The -$51,754 May posting in SAP Incentive Management is a billing anomaly rather than an adoption signal, but if left unreconciled it will distort the $632,962 annual consumption view and mask the otherwise stable commission calculation run-rate that underpins Abbott's sales performance governance.
The Customer Experience LoB has an architectural feedback loop gap — incentive payouts are calculated with precision but the CRM signals that should drive selling behavior (the stated capability of Incentive Management) are under-adopted in Cloud for Customer, weakening the closed-loop value of pairing these two products.
Run a dormant-seat and workflow audit on Cloud for Customer to identify inactive sales and service users and activate pipeline, territory, activity planning, and case management capabilities to close the $5,456 monthly gap.
Reconcile the -$51,754 May entry in SAP Incentive Management with billing operations to confirm whether it is a credit memo, reversal, or misposting before it contaminates the annual $632,962 consumption baseline.
Establish a Cloud for Customer to SAP Incentive Management integration review to ensure CRM opportunity and booking data feeds commission calculations, closing the loop between selling behavior and incentive payout that both products are architected to deliver.
Confirm SAP Incentive Management plan complexity, payee counts, and calculation cycles remain stable to lock in the ~$52K/month Jan–Apr run-rate across the remaining fiscal periods.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
77.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$516K
YTD Budget$670K
YTD ACV: $883K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.6M · Projected ACV: $2.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Finance and Spend Management architecture shows an inverted adoption curve — Preferred Success Ariba is consumed 20% over plan while the underlying Ariba transactional and risk modules sit at 55–22% attainment, meaning advisory services are being pulled to troubleshoot a procurement stack that isn't operating at contracted scale.
The source-to-pay process is architecturally contracted but operationally fragmented — Ariba Sourcing is flat-to-declining, Buying and Invoicing has plateaued below run rate, and Supplier Risk is barely active, so Abbott is executing procurement without the upstream risk signals and downstream invoice automation that justify the integrated Ariba investment.
Ariba Supplier Risk at 22% YTD attainment indicates the risk-monitoring layer is effectively dormant, leaving Abbott's sourcing and buying decisions unguarded by the financial, ESG, and geopolitical feeds the architecture was designed to embed into procurement workflows.
SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is running as a single-purpose financial planning tool rather than the unified analytics-and-planning fabric it was contracted to be — the May drop to 63.1% attainment alongside a $99K YTD gap signals workforce, capex, and operational planning scenarios remain unactivated, blocking the LoB's ability to connect financial outcomes to operational drivers.
Reframe the Preferred Success Ariba overconsumption as evidence of adoption friction and redirect entitlement toward activating dormant Supplier Risk monitoring and expanding Ariba Buying transaction coverage rather than absorbing the $71K overage as standalone advisory.
Launch a source-to-pay reactivation play that ties Ariba Sourcing event expansion to Buying and Invoicing supplier onboarding and Supplier Risk coverage, treating the three modules as one integrated process rather than separate consumption lines.
Run an SAC Planning use-case expansion workshop with Abbott FP&A to extend beyond financial planning into workforce and capex models, converting the $99K YTD gap into unified planning scenarios that exploit the contracted architecture.
Conduct an entitlement true-up review for Preferred Success Ariba against the $263K ceiling while auditing which engagements are driving 120%+ attainment, so Abbott concentrates advisory capacity on workstreams that lift transactional module adoption.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$618K
YTD Budget$618K
YTD ACV: $618K
Projected Consumed: $1.5M · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $1.5M
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Supply Chain planning architecture rests entirely on a single pillar — IBP is the only contracted capability covering demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response, meaning the LoB has no architectural fallback or complementary planning surface if IBP adoption stalls.
The perfectly flat $123,616 monthly consumption pattern is a commercial artifact, not an adoption signal — it tells us the contract is being drawn down on schedule but reveals nothing about whether planners are actually exercising demand sensing, multilevel supply planning, or collaborative S&OP, leaving the business outcome (forward-looking supply decisions) unverified.
With $618,080 consumed YTD against a $1,483,398 annual commitment, Abbott is on a linear burn that assumes uninterrupted planning cadence for the remaining $865,318 — any architectural underuse of IBP's AI-powered forecasting or response planning modules will convert into a back-half budget gap rather than a capability gap, because the meter runs regardless of planner engagement.
IBP's architectural value is in unifying demand, inventory, and supply into one collaborative process, but without telemetry confirming cross-module usage, Abbott risks operating IBP as a demand forecasting tool only — collapsing a strategic S&OP platform into a tactical forecasting utility and forfeiting the inventory optimization and supply response outcomes the investment was sized for.
Instrument IBP module-level telemetry (S&OP, demand, inventory, response) and active planner counts to distinguish metered consumption from genuine planning activity.
Engage Abbott's planning leadership to commit the remaining $865,318 to specific high-value use cases — scenario planning, control tower visibility, and inventory optimization — that exercise the full IBP architecture rather than a single module.
Establish a planning-cycle adoption baseline (forecast runs, S&OP reviews, supply response scenarios per month) so IBP value is measured by decisions enabled, not dollars consumed.
Assess whether complementary Supply Chain capabilities (e.g., Business Network for supply visibility, Ariba for supply collaboration) are needed to close the architectural gap between IBP planning outputs and downstream execution.
Border States Industries, Inc.
Wholesale distribution
YTD Consumed: $410K
YTD Budget: $454K
YTD ACV: $399K
Projected Consumed: $984K
Projected Budget: $1.1M
Projected ACV: $957K
EA Insights and Actions
Border States is running BTP (CPEA) as the undifferentiated runtime for Data & AI, Joule Studio, and Integration Suite simultaneously — with Joule at 208% and Data & AI at 113.7% attainment against a combined ~$318K envelope, the platform has silently become the enterprise's automation and intelligence backbone without service-level attribution, meaning the customer cannot tell whether their BTP spend is funding strategic AI differentiation or incidental sprawl across three converging domains.
The customer has assembled architecturally complete stacks in HXM (Performance→Compensation→Career Development) and Finance (ExpenseIt→Expense→Audit→Pay→Reporting), yet both show broken internal chains — Compensation at $0 and Concur Expense collapsing to $507 in May — revealing a consistent enterprise pattern of contracting end-to-end process suites but operating them as disconnected modules, which blocks the closed-loop talent and spend governance outcomes that justified the suite-level investment.
Commerce Cloud trending to ~32% attainment (~$90K unused of $131,635) while HXM Onboarding sits at 48% and Career Development at 42% reveals a common architectural failure mode — the customer contracts for engagement and experience platforms (buyer-facing and employee-facing) but does not surround them with the upstream/downstream flows needed to activate them, leaving ~$130K+ of FY26 experience-layer capacity dormant against a distributor's core competitive need for digital channel and workforce productivity growth.
Joule Studio's 2x burn rate compounding on top of a production Integration Suite indicates AI agents are now invoking integrated process flows across LoBs, yet the talent, commerce, and finance domains where those agents should deliver value show no corresponding AI-driven adoption signals — meaning BTP-side AI investment is racing ahead of the LoB-side readiness to consume it, creating a widening gap between platform capability and business outcome realization.
Convene a cross-domain CPEA architecture and FinOps review that decomposes BTP consumption by service (HANA Cloud, Datasphere, AI Core, Joule Studio, Integration Suite) and attributes each to the sponsoring LoB use case in HXM, CX, and Finance before the $134K remaining Data & AI budget and Joule overrun force a reactive commercial true-up.
Run an enterprise integration coherence workshop focused on the broken internal chains — Performance→Compensation, Recruiting→Onboarding, and Concur Expense→Pay/Audit/Reporting — to diagnose whether the failures are configuration, data pipeline, or adoption issues, since the same disconnection pattern is recurring across multiple architecturally complete suites.
Position a Commerce Cloud activation roadmap surrounded by CX ecosystem products (Marketing, Sales, Service, CDP) and tied to Joule-driven buyer experiences, converting the ~$90K dormant FY26 commerce capacity and the underused Joule investment into a single B2B digital channel growth play aligned to Border States' distributor business model.
Establish an enterprise architecture governance forum with Border States covering BTP service attribution, suite-level integration health, and value-realization KPIs — replacing the current module-by-module consumption conversation with a portfolio view that ties the ~$700K+ FY26 SAP footprint to measurable business outcomes.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
93.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$81K
YTD Budget$87K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $195K · Projected Budget: $216K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States' Data & AI footprint in BAIP rests on a single architectural pillar — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement — which is operating as the consumption envelope for all BTP-based data and AI workloads, meaning every analytics, integration, or AI service the LoB activates draws against this one credit pool rather than discrete entitlements.
The May attainment of 113.7% following April's 101.7% on a $215,815 annual ceiling indicates the LoB has crossed from steady-state into expansion mode, suggesting net-new Data & AI workloads (likely AI Core, HANA Cloud, or Datasphere services) are being onboarded without a corresponding consumption governance model in place.
With $134,371 of unconsumed budget against a rising monthly burn, the architectural risk is not under-adoption but uncontrolled adoption — the enterprise agreement is delivering value but lacks the service-level visibility needed to attribute spend back to specific Data & AI capabilities and business outcomes.
Because the CPEA is a fungible credit construct, sustained over-attainment without a service decomposition view leaves the LoB unable to distinguish whether growth is coming from strategic AI initiatives or from incidental platform sprawl, blocking informed reinvestment decisions.
Decompose the CPEA consumption by underlying BTP service to identify which Data & AI capabilities (HANA Cloud, Datasphere, SAC, AI Core, Integration Suite) are driving the May overage and map each to a sponsoring business use case.
Establish a rolling consumption forecast against the remaining $134,371 budget and the $215,815 annual ceiling, with alert thresholds tied to service-level burn rates rather than aggregate attainment.
Engage Border States Data & AI stakeholders to confirm whether expanding workloads represent planned strategic adoption warranting a CPEA top-up or true-up, versus unmanaged growth requiring workload optimization.
Introduce tagging and cost-center attribution on BTP services consumed under the CPEA so future adoption signals reflect business capability uptake, not just aggregate platform spend.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
139.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$58K
YTD Budget$42K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $139K · Projected Budget: $102K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the foundational consumption vehicle for both Joule Studio (AI/extensibility) and Integration Suite (middleware), and both workloads have crossed from pilot into steady-state production — meaning the Technology Foundation is actively underpinning live business processes rather than sitting in discovery, but the contract was sized for a discovery-phase footprint.
Joule Studio is burning at ~208% of plan while Integration Suite holds a flat 117% at ~$7.2K/month, indicating two structurally different adoption modes on the same BTP contract: AI/agent workloads are scaling non-linearly as new use cases land, while integration is a stable production backbone — a single commercial true-up will not address both unless the underlying drivers are separated.
The combined trajectory ($50,974 Joule + projected Integration Suite overrun against a blended ~$102K annual envelope) signals that BTP has become the de facto runtime for cross-LoB automation and integration at Border States, yet there is no evidence of FinOps tagging or service-level attribution to tie consumption back to the business capabilities it enables.
Joule Studio's 2x burn rate alongside a productionized Integration Suite suggests AI agents are being built on top of integrated process flows — an architecturally correct pattern, but one that will compound BTP consumption unevenly as each new Joule scenario inherits Integration Suite calls downstream.
Trigger a consolidated CPEA commercial review that right-sizes capacity separately for Joule Studio's growth-curve consumption and Integration Suite's steady-state run rate, rather than treating BTP as a single undifferentiated pool.
Run a BTP workload audit across both Joule Studio and Integration Suite to attribute consumption to specific business capabilities and identify whether the 2x Joule burn reflects expanding use-case adoption or inefficient agent/runtime configuration.
Establish service-level cost tagging and FinOps governance on the CPEA so that future Joule Studio scenarios can be costed against the Integration Suite flows they invoke, preventing compounding overruns as AI adoption scales.
Validate with the LoB which production processes now depend on Integration Suite's $7.2K/month steady-state footprint to ensure contract entitlement protects against service disruption before remaining capacity is exhausted.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
32.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$17K
YTD Budget$54K
YTD ACV: $94K
Projected Consumed: $42K · Projected Budget: $132K · Projected ACV: $225K
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States' Customer Experience architecture is effectively a single-product, single-workload Commerce Cloud deployment — the flat $3,378–$3,554 monthly consumption band against a $10,800 budget indicates the storefront is live but functioning as a static catalog rather than a unified commerce engine spanning B2B, B2C, and B2B2C journeys.
The intended Commerce Cloud value drivers — multi-channel sales growth, personalization, and AI-led innovation — are architecturally contracted but operationally dormant, leaving the LoB unable to convert its digital commerce investment into incremental revenue or differentiated buyer experiences as ~$90K of FY2026 capacity trends toward unused.
Commerce Cloud is operating in isolation without visible CX ecosystem reinforcement (marketing, sales, service, or customer data), which architecturally explains the stalled adoption: there is no upstream demand generation or downstream service continuity feeding the storefront, so traffic and SKU expansion have no engine behind them.
The ~32% projected attainment against a $131,635 annual budget is less a consumption problem than an architectural one — Border States contracted for enterprise commerce scale but is running entry-level commerce scope, meaning the platform's TCO advantage and time-to-value capabilities are inverted.
Run a Commerce Cloud capability audit to map which B2B features, catalogs, storefronts, and personalization services are provisioned but inactive, and tie each dormant capability to a specific Border States revenue or buyer-experience outcome.
Define a phased storefront expansion roadmap that activates additional B2B sites, SKU ranges, or buyer segments to convert the $15,172 May ACV gap into measurable digital channel growth.
Position complementary CX domain products (Marketing, Sales, Service, or CDP) to surround Commerce Cloud with demand and retention flows, since the storefront cannot scale adoption on its own.
Establish a Commerce Cloud value realization review with the Customer Experience LoB owner to reframe the conversation from consumption attainment to digital commerce KPIs such as order volume, conversion, and B2B account penetration.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
116.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$67K
YTD Budget$57K
YTD ACV: $68K
Projected Consumed: $160K · Projected Budget: $137K · Projected ACV: $162K
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States has architecturally assembled a complete Concur Travel & Expense control stack — capture (ExpenseIt), submission (Expense), audit (Intelligent Audit), payment (Expense Pay), and reporting — and four of the five components are running 120%+ hot, confirming T&E is a production-grade, embedded business process rather than a pilot.
The May collapse in Concur Expense ($507 vs ~$7,100/month run rate) against steady Expense Pay, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting consumption is architecturally incoherent — the downstream audit, payment, and reporting layers cannot stay active without upstream expense submissions, signaling either a data pipeline break, a policy/config change, or a reporting lag that puts the integrity of the entire T&E chain at risk.
Three of five Concur modules (ExpenseIt +37%, Intelligent Audit +27%, Reporting Services +33%) are on a structural overrun trajectory totaling ~$19.5K above plan, indicating the contract was sized for a smaller T&E footprint than the LoB now operates — this is a sizing failure, not a usage problem, and unmanaged it will trigger mid-year ceiling exhaustion.
Expense Pay's disciplined 97% attainment alongside over-consuming capture, audit, and reporting modules shows reimbursement throughput is stable while the surrounding control and visibility layers are scaling faster — the LoB is investing in tighter governance and analytics on top of a steady payment base, which is the right maturity vector but needs commercial alignment.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense cliff by reconciling expense report submission counts against the still-active Expense Pay, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting volumes to isolate whether the break is in user behavior, integration, or month-close timing.
Initiate a consolidated true-up across Concur ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services to right-size contracted capacity against the demonstrated ~130% attainment baseline before ceiling exhaustion forces a reactive commercial conversation.
Re-baseline the FY2026 Concur Expense budget with the customer's finance owner, since Jan–Apr ran 115–136% over plan and the original $5,400/month allocation clearly underestimated steady-state submission volume.
Position a Concur Invoice and Concur Travel expansion conversation anchored on the proven adoption maturity of the existing T&E stack, extending Border States' spend governance architecture from employee expense into supplier invoicing and pre-trip booking.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD Budget$214K
YTD ACV: $237K
Projected Consumed: $448K · Projected Budget: $517K · Projected ACV: $570K
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States has architecturally assembled a near-complete HXM suite — core HR, payroll, onboarding, recruiting, performance, and career development are all contracted — but the talent layer is operating as disconnected point tools rather than an integrated talent system, since Performance feeds Compensation which sits at $0 consumption, breaking the pay-for-performance loop the architecture was designed to deliver.
Employee Central and EC Payroll are running at 100% and ~98% attainment respectively, confirming the HR system of record and gross-to-net engine are fully operationalized — this foundational data spine is healthy and ready to feed downstream talent modules that are currently under-leveraging it.
The talent acquisition-to-productivity chain is structurally leaking value: Recruiting Management is stuck at 88% with a projected $10,915 shortfall and Onboarding at only 48% attainment with a $16,080 gap, meaning candidates are being sourced and hired but the integrated hire-to-productive-employee journey (cross-boarding, offboarding, internal transitions) is bypassing the platform for a majority of events.
Career Development at 42% attainment combined with Compensation at 0% and Performance leaking 5–6% monthly reveals a dormant growth-and-rewards layer — the skills, goals, and pay-decision data that should flow from Performance into Career Development and Compensation is architecturally wired but operationally idle, leaving managers without the AI-driven talent decisioning the suite was contracted to enable.
Activate SF Compensation with a minimum-viable comp cycle (one plan, one population) sourced from Employee Central salary data and Performance ratings to close the broken pay-for-performance integration and recover the full $550 contracted value.
Run a unified Onboarding and Recruiting workflow audit to identify which hiring cohorts, cross-boards, and internal transitions are bypassing the SuccessFactors hire-to-productive journey, routing them back into the platform to close the combined ~$27,000 projected shortfall.
Launch a Career Development and Performance integration play that activates Goal Management, Calibration, and Mentoring against the existing Employee Central population to lift Career Development above its 42% ceiling and close the Performance 5–6% attainment gap.
Convene an executive business review with Border States HR leadership to reframe the FY26 unused runway across Onboarding, Career Development, Recruiting, and Compensation as an integrated talent-suite activation roadmap rather than module-by-module consumption recovery.
CHS Inc.
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $1.6M
YTD Budget: $2.1M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $3.9M
Projected Budget: $5.2M
Projected ACV: $5.4M
EA Insights and Actions
CHS has contracted a $2.8M+ enterprise SAP stack where every domain shows the same architectural pattern — strong system-of-record consumption (Employee Central 96%, Database Cloud 89%, IBP batch runs, Integration Suite 115%) paired with dormant intelligence layers (SAC Planning 8%, Signavio 31%, Career/Learning 33%/65%, BTP Other $0, IBP scenario modeling idle), meaning CHS is paying enterprise-platform prices to run SAP as a transactional backbone rather than as the integrated decision-and-transformation fabric the contracts assume.
The two platform capabilities that should connect every domain — BTP extensibility ($5,960 Tech Foundation Other at $0 consumption, CPEA in Finance at $0) and Signavio process governance (30.8% attainment against a 5x ACV expansion to $46,854) — are both dormant, which means HCM talent flows, Finance spend-to-plan, and Supply Chain S&OP are each being configured against tribal knowledge with no shared extension substrate, guaranteeing that every cross-domain integration CHS builds today becomes core-embedded technical debt at the next S/4 upgrade.
Integration Suite is the only platform layer scaling (15% hot, $23,917 overage) while SAP Build declines, SSO collapses to 21%, and Fieldglass Contingent/Services trend to 39%/24% — CHS is overbuilding integration pipes between systems whose user-facing, identity, and external-workforce endpoints are simultaneously eroding, so the integration investment is wiring together a shrinking active surface area rather than expanding the digital cooperative.
Across an ~$8M+ annual contracted portfolio, the consistent signal is that advisory and success capacity (Preferred Success 119%, Integration Suite 115%) is being consumed faster than the modules it should accelerate (Talent, Planning, Signavio, IBP scenario) — CHS is buying enablement against a stack whose adoption is contracting, projecting a multi-hundred-thousand dollar shortfall that is structural, not seasonal.
Convene a cross-domain Enterprise Architecture review that maps Signavio process models as the governance spine connecting HCM talent flows, Finance spend-to-plan, and Supply Chain S&OP, converting the dormant $63K Signavio commitment into the single process baseline every downstream SAP transformation is configured against.
Run a BTP activation roadmap session that consolidates the stranded CPEA entitlement in Finance, the $5,960 Tech Foundation Other line, and the declining SAP Build footprint into one prioritized extension backlog tied to the highest-value cross-domain use cases (SAC Planning extensions, IAG-SSO consolidation, hire-to-skills flows).
Launch an identity and external-workforce integration workshop pairing SSO, Cloud IAG, and Fieldglass Contingent/Services to halt the parallel decline and restore the governed-identity-to-governed-spend chain into Ariba and S/4HANA.
Redirect Preferred Success and Integration Suite advisory overage capacity toward activating the dormant intelligence layers — SAC Planning, Signavio modeling, IBP scenario planning, and the SuccessFactors talent triad — under a single enterprise adoption scorecard governed at the CHS executive level rather than per-domain.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
110.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$321K
YTD Budget$290K
YTD ACV: $96K
Projected Consumed: $770K · Projected Budget: $773K · Projected ACV: $230K
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's Data & AI footprint is architecturally lean — only Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC and Database Cloud) and SAC BI are active, meaning the LoB has data storage, runtime, and visualization layers in place but no visible planning, data integration, or AI/ML services consuming the platform, which suggests SAC BI dashboards are the primary monetized outcome of the entire BAIP investment.
SAC BI is trending to a ~10% overage ($247K projected vs $224K budget) while BDC Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is trending $28K under — the visualization tier is being consumed faster than the underlying platform, indicating analytics demand is outpacing the data foundation and risks SAC dashboards relying on narrow or stale datasets rather than a broadening BDC-hosted data estate.
Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is operationalized with steady ~89% YTD attainment and a small projected overage, confirming a persistent production workload — but with no catalog-described analytics, planning, or AI services layered on top, the database is functioning as infrastructure rather than as part of an integrated Data & AI value chain.
The Q1 BDC consumption spike (158% in Jan, ~110% Feb–Mar) followed by normalization mirrors SAC BI's identical Q1 overconsumption pattern, indicating the two are architecturally coupled — SAC workloads are driving BDC compute — and validates that analytics adoption is the dominant demand signal shaping platform consumption.
Map active BDC Public service consumption to specific SAC BI data models and use cases to confirm the $484K platform spend is directly enabling sanctioned analytics outcomes rather than drifting into unmanaged workloads.
Initiate a SAC BI true-up conversation to convert the projected $22,578 overage into expanded entitlement, and tie the expansion to identified high-value report and user cohorts driving the Q1 spike.
Build an expansion plan for the BDC Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement that absorbs the $27,905 projected shortfall by introducing adjacent Data & AI services (data integration, planning, or Joule-based AI) that extend SAC BI from reporting into forward-looking decision support.
Validate that Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement dips in Feb and Apr reflect workload seasonality rather than service disruption, and scope a small capacity true-up to cover the projected $1,400 overage before it breaches ceiling.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$112K
YTD Budget$105K
YTD ACV: $1K
Projected Consumed: $268K · Projected Budget: $248K · Projected ACV: $3K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation at CHS is architecturally split-brained — Integration Suite is running 15% hot ($23,917 projected overage) while the broader BTP entitlement under 'Technology Foundation Other' sits at $0 consumption, meaning CHS is overbuilding integration pipes without activating the extension, data, and platform services those integrations are meant to feed.
SAP Build adoption has plateaued and is now declining (May at 92.5% attainment after three months above 107%), indicating the low-code layer that should be turning integrated data into business-user-facing apps and workflows is losing momentum — CHS is moving data between systems but not converting it into new digital experiences or automations.
Joule Studio consumption is tracking near-plan with a modest $2,225 projected overage, signaling that AI/agent tooling is being adopted at a steady operational cadence — this is the healthiest signal in the foundation and the natural anchor for expanding Build and dormant BTP services around it.
The combined picture shows CHS treating BTP as an integration backbone rather than a full innovation platform — Integration Suite is scaling, Joule Studio is steady, but Build is fading and the general BTP entitlement is stranded, leaving the LoB without the application and extensibility layer that justifies the foundation investment.
Initiate a capacity and workload review on the Integration Suite Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to right-size against the $23,917 projected overage and apply consumption governance on the services driving the persistent 8–35% monthly overrun.
Launch a BTP activation workshop against the dormant $5,960 'Technology Foundation Other' entitlement to land a first extension, data, or platform workload and convert the $4,394 stranded YTD budget into live consumption.
Run a Build value and use-case review with the CHS citizen-developer community to reverse the Mar–May consumption decline by targeting app and automation scenarios that sit on top of the already-scaling Integration Suite flows.
Use the steady Joule Studio adoption pattern as a reference architecture to bundle Build, Integration Suite, and dormant BTP services into a unified AI-plus-extension roadmap that pulls the underutilized capabilities into active LoB outcomes.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
30.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$30K
YTD ACV: $94K
Projected Consumed: $22K · Projected Budget: $73K · Projected ACV: $225K
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's Business Transformation Management architecture rests on a single pillar — Signavio Process Management — and that pillar is dormant at 30.8% YTD attainment, meaning the LoB has the capability to model, govern, and continuously improve end-to-end processes but is not actually using it to drive transformation outcomes.
The 5x ACV expansion to $46,854 in May against collapsing $993 consumption signals a commercial commitment that has outpaced operational readiness — CHS has bought transformation capacity without the process ownership model, modeler enablement, or governance forum needed to convert licenses into a living process repository.
Without an active process modeling layer, every downstream SAP investment at CHS (S/4HANA, Ariba, SuccessFactors, etc.) is being configured against tribal knowledge rather than a governed process baseline, blocking the LoB's ability to measure transformation impact or standardize operations across the cooperative.
Stand up a Signavio Center of Excellence at CHS with named process owners accountable for publishing a Level 1–3 process baseline covering core grain, energy, and food value chains to anchor the $63,769 of unconsumed contract value to concrete modeling deliverables.
Reconcile the May ACV expansion to $46,854 against actual provisioned modelers and reviewers, and right-size or redeploy idle seats toward business process owners tied to active S/4 and Ariba transformation initiatives.
Establish a process governance cadence that links Signavio-published models to change requests in downstream SAP systems, ensuring the platform becomes the system of record for how CHS operates rather than a parallel documentation tool.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
34.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$200K
YTD Budget$578K
YTD ACV: $902K
Projected Consumed: $479K · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $2.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's Finance & Spend Management architecture is broadly contracted but operationally fragmented — external workforce (Fieldglass Contingent + Services), planning (SAC Planning), and platform (CPEA) all show declining or zero consumption, meaning the LoB has licensed an integrated spend-to-plan stack but is operating each pillar in isolated pilot mode rather than as a connected source-to-pay-to-plan process.
The contingent labor governance layer is structurally broken — both Fieldglass Contingent Workforce (39% projected attainment) and Services Procurement (24% projected attainment) are declining in parallel, which means external workforce and SOW spend is increasingly being executed outside the VMS, eroding the spend visibility bridge into Ariba and S/4HANA that justified the Fieldglass investment.
Identity governance is deployed as a static control rather than an expanding compliance fabric — Cloud IAG runs at flat ~$24K monthly while SSO is collapsing toward 21% attainment, indicating CHS has baseline access certification running but the authentication consolidation layer underneath it is being abandoned, leaving a widening gap between governed identities and the systems users actually log into.
The FP&A modernization path is stalled at both the application and platform layers — SAC Planning sits at 8% attainment with declining usage while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is at $0 consumption, meaning CHS has neither activated the planning models nor the extensibility platform needed to unify financial and operational planning, locking finance into legacy planning cycles for FY2026.
Run a unified external workforce diagnostic across Fieldglass Contingent Workforce and Services Procurement with CHS procurement and HR to identify which labor categories and SOWs have reverted off-platform, and re-anchor them through the Ariba/S4 integration to restore end-to-end spend visibility.
Activate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by identifying the first SAC Planning or IAG extension workload it should host, converting the stranded platform budget into the integration substrate the rest of the Finance stack depends on.
Launch an SAC Planning use-case expansion engagement targeting workforce planning and financial close scenarios tied to FY2026, moving the tool out of pilot footprint and into the production planning cycles it was licensed to support.
Conduct a joint SSO and Cloud IAG coverage review to align authenticated applications with governed identities, expanding IAG into access analysis and PAM use cases while reversing the SSO application onboarding stall.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
88.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$275K
YTD Budget$311K
YTD ACV: $357K
Projected Consumed: $659K · Projected Budget: $753K · Projected ACV: $857K
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS Inc.'s HCM foundation is healthy but the talent layer built on top is hollow — Employee Central is maturing at 96.5% attainment as the system of record, yet Career and Talent Development (33%), Learning (65%), and Performance and Goals (77%) are all under-consumed and trending flat or down, meaning the core HR data is in place but the skills-development-performance loop that justifies the SuccessFactors talent suite is not closing.
The hire-to-develop journey is architecturally wired but operationally broken — Recruiting Management is stable at ~94%, Onboarding lags at 66%, and Career/Learning sit at 33%/65%, so CHS is sourcing candidates but failing to convert them through onboarding into active learners and career-pathed employees, leaving the integrated talent funnel leaking at every downstream stage.
Compensation and Performance are eroding in lockstep — both modules show five straight months of declining attainment (Comp 84%→79%, P&G 81%→77%), and since SF Compensation architecturally depends on Performance data to drive merit and bonus decisions, the disengagement from performance reviews is directly starving the pay-for-performance process CHS contracted for.
Preferred Success is the only HCM line running hot at 119% while every talent module under-consumes — the customer is buying advisory capacity faster than they are activating the modules that advisory is meant to accelerate, indicating success services are being consumed on run-the-business support rather than driving adoption expansion across the dormant talent stack.
Redirect Preferred Success SFSF engagements toward a targeted activation program for Career and Talent Development, Learning, and Performance and Goals, converting the $24K projected overrun into measurable adoption lift on the three weakest talent modules.
Tie SF Performance and Goals re-activation to the SF Compensation merit cycle so that goal completion and review data feed comp worksheets, restoring the architectural dependency that justifies both modules and recovering the combined ~$26K projected shortfall.
Launch a hire-to-skills journey play that routes every Recruiting Management requisition through Onboarding into a Career Development worksheet and Learning curriculum, closing the funnel leakage and lifting the three under-consumed downstream modules in one integrated motion.
Establish an HCM adoption scorecard with CHS HR leadership that tracks active-user coverage across Employee Central, talent, and learning modules so the $99K of remaining EC budget and the $70K+ of dormant talent capacity are governed as one connected portfolio rather than seven independent SKUs.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
88.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$692K
YTD Budget$785K
YTD ACV: $817K
Projected Consumed: $1.7M · Projected Budget: $1.9M · Projected ACV: $2.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is deployed as a scheduled batch planning engine rather than a collaborative S&OP platform — the rigid two-tier monthly consumption pattern shows CHS is running periodic planning jobs but not engaging planners across demand sensing, inventory optimization, and supply response, leaving the unified planning process architecturally present but functionally narrow.
The $93,357 YTD gap and projected $253,523 annual shortfall against the $1.91M budget signal that licensed IBP capacity — likely planner seats and scenario modeling entitlement — is sitting idle, meaning CHS is paying for enterprise-wide planning collaboration while only one module or business unit is actively consuming it.
Without expansion beyond batch runs, CHS forfeits IBP's AI-powered demand prediction and multilevel supply planning value, leaving the supply chain reactive to monthly cycles rather than responsive to real-time demand and supply signals across the commercial network.
Audit IBP entitlement against active planner seats and module activation to identify which capabilities (demand sensing, inventory optimization, response planning) are licensed but dormant, then prioritize enablement in the underutilized modules.
Extend IBP adoption into additional CHS business units beyond the current batch-running group to convert the recurring $15,713 monthly ACV gap into active multi-LoB planning consumption.
Shift IBP from scheduled batch execution toward continuous planner-driven scenario modeling and collaborative S&OP cycles to activate the AI forecasting and response planning capabilities that justify the full contract value.
CLEVELAND-CLIFFS INC.
Mining
YTD Consumed: $119K
YTD Budget: $162K
YTD ACV: $215K
Projected Consumed: $285K
Projected Budget: $392K
Projected ACV: $516K
EA Insights and Actions
Cleveland-Cliffs has assembled a four-pillar SAP footprint (SAC, Integration Suite, C4C, IBP) where every pillar independently shows 17–64% YTD attainment, revealing an enterprise pattern of strategic licensing without operational activation — collectively ~$106K of a ~$392K annual contract is trending unconsumed, signaling the LoB is funding an architectural vision it has not yet operationalized into business outcomes.
The Integration Suite operating as a single-interface utility is the structural root cause behind the dormancy in IBP, SAC, and C4C — without a governed integration fabric, IBP planning cannot close the loop to execution, SAC cannot become an embedded analytics layer across finance/operations, and C4C cannot synchronize a 360° customer view with ERP, leaving each domain stranded as an island.
For a steel manufacturer whose competitive levers are demand-supply synchronization, customer responsiveness, and margin visibility, the architecture is missing the connective tissue — no Ariba/TM execution behind IBP, no Service/CPQ behind C4C, and no Joule/planning activation on SAC — meaning Cleveland-Cliffs' SAP estate currently supports departmental tools rather than the integrated mill-to-customer digital thread the industry requires.
The uniform pattern of one-module-per-domain adoption (SAC=BI only, IBP=demand only, C4C=SFA only, Integration Suite=one iFlow) indicates an enterprise architecture governance gap where solutions are procured at suite level but deployed at point-solution level, structurally blocking the cross-domain compounding value that justified the SAP standardization in the first place.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review with the Cleveland-Cliffs CIO that maps IBP → Integration Suite → S/4/Ariba execution and SAC → Integration Suite → operational data sources, treating Integration Suite activation as the prerequisite unlock for IBP and SAC ROI rather than a parallel workstream.
Establish an enterprise consumption and attainment governance forum that tracks YTD attainment across all four L1 domains as a single architectural health metric, targeting the ~$106K at-risk contract value through coordinated activation rather than domain-by-domain remediation.
Sponsor a mill-to-customer digital thread workshop that sequences the missing adjacent capabilities (Ariba for IBP closure, Service Cloud/CPQ for C4C, Joule/Planning for SAC) into a prioritized roadmap aligned to steel-industry margin and responsiveness objectives.
Mandate Integration Suite as the standard integration layer for any new initiative across Data & AI, CX, and SCM domains, with iFlow onboarding KPIs owned by the integration COE to convert the dormant platform into the architectural backbone its contract assumes.

Solution Insights and Actions
SAP Analytics Cloud BI is the sole Data & AI capability in scope for Cleveland-Cliffs, and its episodic April-only consumption pattern shows the platform is being treated as a project tool rather than the embedded analytics fabric it is architected to be — leaving the LoB without a trusted, unified decision-making layer across finance and operations.
With no evidence of planning or Joule-driven AI workflows being exercised on top of SAC, the unified analytics-and-planning value proposition is architecturally dormant — Cleveland-Cliffs has reporting potential but is not converting it into forward-looking financial and operational planning outcomes.
Projected $580 consumption against a $3,442 annual budget (17% YTD attainment, ~$2,862 unused capacity) confirms SAC BI has not been operationalized as the standard BI layer, signaling a content and persona-activation gap rather than a licensing or capacity constraint.
Anchor SAC BI to a recurring high-value business process (operations or finance month-end) by deploying a governed story/model set tied to named business owners, converting one-time April-style usage into sustained monthly reporting cadence.
Extend SAC beyond BI into integrated planning use cases to activate the unified analytics-and-planning architecture, so Cleveland-Cliffs gains forward-looking decision support rather than retrospective dashboards.
Run an enablement and content-activation review to diagnose the post-April collapse to $20 monthly consumption, addressing user adoption, data model readiness, and Joule-assisted analytics exposure.
Solution Area

19.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$133
YTD Budget$698
YTD ACV: $1K
Projected Consumed: $320 · Projected Budget: $2K · Projected ACV: $3K
Solution Insights and Actions
SAP Integration Suite is architecturally positioned as the unified backbone for AI-ready APIs, EDI, and S/4 connectivity at Cleveland-Cliffs, but the flat $26–$27/month consumption signature indicates only a single steady-state interface is live — meaning the Technology Foundation is contracted as a strategic integration hub yet operating as a point-to-point utility, blocking the LoB from realizing the AI agent enablement and process governance the platform was bought to deliver.
With projected annual consumption of ~$320 against a $1,706 budget (under 19% attainment and a $1,386 gap), the Integration Suite tenant is effectively dormant capacity — Cleveland-Cliffs is paying for enterprise-grade API management, prebuilt content, and AI integration patterns while the manufacturing LoB continues to rely on legacy middleware, leaving downstream S/4, Ariba, and analytics investments without a modern, governed integration fabric.
The absence of expanding iFlow volume signals that no migration roadmap from legacy PI/PO or custom interfaces has been activated, which architecturally means every new digital initiative at Cleveland-Cliffs (AI agents, supplier connectivity, plant data) will either bypass the strategic platform or be built on deprecated integration tech — compounding technical debt rather than retiring it.
Run an Integration Suite use-case discovery workshop with Cleveland-Cliffs' integration and enterprise architecture teams to identify 3–5 high-value iFlows spanning API management, EDI with steel customers/suppliers, and S/4 connectivity that move the platform from single-scenario to enterprise backbone.
Establish a legacy PI/PO and point-to-point interface inventory and build a phased migration plan onto Integration Suite, directly targeting the $1,386 unconsumed contract value and retiring parallel middleware costs.
Position Integration Suite as the mandated API layer for any AI agent or Joule-related initiative at Cleveland-Cliffs, ensuring new AI and automation use cases drive consumption growth rather than circumventing the governed platform.
Engage the Cleveland-Cliffs CIO and integration COE to define iFlow consumption KPIs and an onboarding governance model so that attainment becomes a tracked architectural metric rather than a procurement leftover.
