Projected Consumed: $5K · Projected Budget: $20K · Projected ACV: $41K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cloud for Customer is the sole deployed pillar of the Customer Experience stack at Cleveland-Cliffs, meaning the LoB's entire sales and service engagement architecture rests on a single front-office system with no surrounding CX ecosystem (marketing, commerce, or service cloud) to amplify its value.
The flat $294–$521 monthly consumption pattern indicates C4C is operating as a system-of-record for a small static user group rather than a system-of-engagement driving pipeline and service outcomes — the account, opportunity, and activity management capabilities that justify the platform are effectively dormant for the broader sales organization.
At 22.3% YTD attainment with $15,464 of the $20,295 annual budget at risk, the architectural intent of C4C as the unified customer engagement layer is not being realized — the LoB is paying for a 360° customer view it isn't operationalizing, leaving sales reps likely reliant on spreadsheets or ERP-side workflows for customer interactions.
Run a license utilization audit on Cloud for Customer to separate provisioned-but-inactive users from active users, then re-onboard dormant licenses against the core account, opportunity, and activity management workflows.
Establish a C4C adoption sprint anchored on a defined sales process (lead-to-opportunity-to-quote) so the platform delivers measurable pipeline and conversion outcomes rather than serving as a passive contact repository.
Evaluate whether the Customer Experience footprint should be expanded with adjacent CX capabilities (Service Cloud, CPQ) or rationalized down to match actual usage, ending the budget bleed from an under-leveraged single-product deployment.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
77.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$117K
YTD Budget$151K
YTD ACV: $194K
Projected Consumed: $280K · Projected Budget: $366K · Projected ACV: $466K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cleveland-Cliffs' Supply Chain Management architecture rests on a single pillar — IBP — with no adjacent execution or response capabilities (no Ariba, no Transportation Management, no EWM signals), meaning planning outputs have no closed-loop path into procurement or logistics execution, which structurally caps the ROI of the IBP investment.
IBP itself is architecturally underutilized as a unified S&OP platform: the rigid $19,419/month flat-line consumption with a one-time March catch-up indicates only one planning module (likely demand or basic S&OP) is live, while inventory optimization, response/supply planning, and AI-driven demand sensing capabilities remain dormant despite being contracted.
With May YTD attainment at 63.9% and a projected $86,840 of the $366,478 annual contract at risk of going unconsumed, the LoB is paying for enterprise-grade collaborative planning but operating it as a departmental forecasting tool — a scope mismatch that will undermine renewal economics.
Activate a second IBP planning process — specifically inventory optimization or response & supply planning — to extend value beyond the single dormant-pattern use case and consume the contracted capacity.
Initiate an architectural review to connect IBP outputs to a downstream execution layer (Ariba for supply collaboration or S/4 procurement), since planning without execution integration is the root cause of the flat consumption ceiling.
Run a planner-seat utilization audit to identify dormant licenses and re-onboard users into multilevel supply planning and collaborative S&OP workflows rather than single-scenario demand review.
Cardinal Health Inc.
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.0M
YTD Budget: $1.4M
YTD ACV: $1.7M
Projected Consumed: $2.5M
Projected Budget: $3.5M
Projected ACV: $4.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's three active L1 domains (BAIP, Customer Experience, Finance & Spend) each show a different failure mode — BAIP is sporadic, CX is dormant at 7% attainment, and Spend is over-saturated on execution but starved on governance — revealing an enterprise lacking a coherent platform operating model where investment intent is not matched by activation discipline, which blocks Cardinal Health from realizing the connected commerce-to-cash-to-spend value chain its contracts already pay for.
SAP Build sits idle as the extensibility fabric while Ariba SLP, Contracts, and Commerce Cloud all have unrealized capabilities that would naturally consume BTP integrations and low-code workflows — this disconnection means Cardinal Health is buying extensibility on one side and unmet automation needs on the other without bridging them, leaving citizen development and supplier/commerce process orchestration as parallel underperformers.
The supplier master gap in Ariba SLP (13.1%) combined with a single dormant Commerce Cloud storefront exposes a missing trusted-data backbone across both buy-side and sell-side domains, blocking Cardinal Health's healthcare-distribution mandate of end-to-end traceability from supplier qualification through customer fulfillment.
Against a combined enterprise footprint where Concur is over-consuming, CX is ~$889K unrealized, and BAIP is ~$227K short, Cardinal Health's net portfolio is materially under-attained even as specific ceilings breach — signaling an account where true-up conversations must be paired with reallocation, not just expansion, to protect renewal economics.
Convene a cross-domain architecture review that maps SAP Build extensibility opportunities directly onto the dormant capabilities in Commerce Cloud, Ariba SLP, and Ariba Contracts to create a single activation backlog rather than three disconnected adoption plans.
Run an enterprise master data and integration workshop focused on establishing the supplier and customer data backbone across Ariba SLP and Commerce Cloud, anchored on BTP Integration Suite as the connective layer.
Lead a portfolio-level commercial review with Cardinal Health that pairs the Concur and Ariba execution-layer true-ups with a reallocation proposal for the unrealized Commerce Cloud and SAP Build envelopes to rebalance the contract to actual architectural intent.
Establish a Cardinal Health SAP Center of Excellence charter spanning BAIP, Spend, and CX domains to institutionalize a unified adoption, extensibility, and governance operating model across the SAP estate.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
68.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$242
YTD Budget$354
YTD ACV: $642
Projected Consumed: $580 · Projected Budget: $807 · Projected ACV: $2K
Solution Insights and Actions
SAP Build is architecturally positioned as Cardinal Health's low-code extensibility layer for citizen development, workflow automation, and SAP process extensions — but the inconsistent consumption pattern (only March hit plan, May collapsed to 44%) indicates the platform is being used as a one-off project tool rather than an institutionalized developer practice, leaving the BTP extensibility capability dormant between bursts.
With $241 consumed against $354 YTD budget (68%) and a projected $227 annual shortfall, the foundation layer that should be enabling fit-to-standard SAP extensions and process automation is under-leveraged, meaning business-led app development and workflow modernization use cases are likely being solved outside SAP or not at all.
The absence of a sustained maker community signals that Joule Studio and SAP Build's generative development capabilities are not embedded in Cardinal Health's app dev operating model, blocking the LoB from scaling AI-assisted extensions and process automation across the SAP estate.
Run a SAP Build use-case discovery workshop with Cardinal Health's app dev and automation leads to identify 3-5 net-new citizen developer projects that absorb the $227 budget gap and anchor recurring consumption.
Audit active SAP Build users and projects to determine whether the March spike was a one-time build effort, and onboard a second wave of makers with a governed Center of Excellence to sustain monthly consumption above $80.
Establish a SAP Build extensibility roadmap tied to specific S/4HANA and BTP process extension opportunities so the platform shifts from isolated project bursts to a steady developer practice.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
8.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$29K
YTD Budget$360K
YTD ACV: $659K
Projected Consumed: $69K · Projected Budget: $982K · Projected ACV: $1.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's Customer Experience architecture is effectively single-threaded on Commerce Cloud with only one low-volume storefront live — the platform is contracted as an enterprise B2B/B2C/B2B2C engine spanning catalog, pricing, promotions, and order management, but the flat $7K monthly run rate signals that catalog breadth, segment coverage, and channel reach are all dormant, leaving the LoB unable to grow sales across every channel as intended.
The unified commerce promise is structurally broken because no complementary CX capabilities (personalization, service, or omnichannel order orchestration) appear alongside Commerce Cloud — Cardinal Health is running a transactional storefront in isolation rather than a connected customer experience, which blocks the seamless B2B buyer journey the architecture was designed to deliver.
With FY26 trending to ~$93K against a $982K budget (7% attainment, ~$889K unrealized), the consumption pattern confirms the Commerce investment is sized for an enterprise-scale rollout that never materialized, meaning the LoB is paying for elasticity, AI innovation, and multi-site scale it is not architecturally exercising.
Run a Commerce Cloud capability activation workshop with Cardinal Health's digital commerce owner to map dormant B2B catalogs, punchout integrations, locales, and customer segments against the existing license footprint.
Define a phased storefront rollout plan that sequences additional sites and buyer segments onto Commerce Cloud to convert the ~$889K of unrealized ACV into active channel revenue.
Architect Commerce Cloud into the broader Customer Experience stack by scoping personalization, service, and order management extensions so the LoB moves from a standalone storefront to a unified commerce experience.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
97.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.0M
YTD Budget$1.0M
YTD ACV: $1.1M
Projected Consumed: $2.4M · Projected Budget: $2.5M · Projected ACV: $2.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Concur T&E stack is architecturally complete and operationally saturated — Expense, ExpenseIt, Expense Pay, Direct Travel, Analytics, Reporting, Connectors, and User Support are all running at or above 100% ACV ceiling, meaning Cardinal Health has fully embedded the travel-and-expense lifecycle but every component is structurally undersized for actual demand and faces mid-year throttling.
The source-to-contract spine is broken at the governance layer — Ariba Sourcing and Buying & Invoicing are over-attaining while Ariba Contracts sits at 71.7% and SLP at 13.1%, so Cardinal Health is awarding spend and paying invoices without the contract lifecycle or supplier qualification controls that convert sourcing events into enforceable, compliant supply agreements.
Ariba SLP's $408 on/off transaction pattern confirms supplier onboarding still lives outside the platform, which starves Sourcing, Contracts, and Buying of a trusted supplier master and blocks the preferred-supplier, risk, and performance signals the rest of the Ariba architecture is designed to consume.
Preferred Success entitlements for both Ariba and Concur are fully absorbed and over-pacing budget, signaling the LoB is actively leaning on SAP-led adoption guidance — a leading indicator that consumption growth across the Spend stack will continue and the contract envelope needs to be resized, not just trued-up line by line.
Consolidate a single Finance & Spend true-up covering Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Expense Pay, Direct Travel, Analytics, Reporting Services, Connectors/Extracts, User Support Desk, and SAP Digital Payments to right-size ACV against a combined projected overrun exceeding $70K rather than negotiating each ceiling breach in isolation.
Stand up an Ariba SLP activation program to migrate supplier qualification, segmentation, and performance scorecards onto the platform, establishing the supplier master of record that Sourcing, Contracts, and Buying & Invoicing depend on for closed-loop spend governance.
Launch a Contracts adoption push centered on templates, clause library, and third-party paper ingestion so that the sourcing events already being executed in Ariba Sourcing flow into managed agreements, closing the governance gap between award and PO.
Commission a Concur User Support Desk ticket-driver analysis and route recurring issues into Preferred Success Concur enablement to deflect demand, converting structural support overrun into self-service adoption rather than pure capacity expansion.
Cargill, Incorporated
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $4.1M
YTD Budget: $4.1M
YTD ACV: $4.0M
Projected Consumed: $9.7M
Projected Budget: $9.8M
Projected ACV: $9.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Cargill is running production-scale Data & AI on BTP (BDC + Database Cloud pacing to ~$152K vs ~$114K commitment) and an integration-heavy BTP backbone (Integration Suite at 107%, Joule at 118%), yet the Ariba Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 and SAP Build is regressing to 69% — meaning the same BTP fabric that is over-consumed for data and integration is dormant for procurement extensibility and citizen development, fracturing the clean-core story across Finance, CX, and Supply Chain.
Across a ~$6M+ enterprise run-rate the customer is simultaneously overrunning the strategic, differentiating workloads (BDC +36%, Commerce +5%, IBP +7.5%, Concur stack +30%, Joule +18%) while under-consuming the governance and orchestration layers (Ariba Sourcing 38%, Contracts 45%, SIPM 83%, Build 70%, Cloud Platform EA 0%) — a structural pattern where Cargill is paying for execution capacity without the upstream controls, signaling a portfolio sized for transactions rather than outcomes.
Every L1 domain is anchored on a single product — Commerce Cloud for CX, IBP for Supply Chain, Ariba Buying for procurement, BDC for Data — with no adjacent pillars activated, meaning Cargill has built a federation of point solutions rather than a connected enterprise: Commerce transactions cannot flow into Sales/Service/CDC, IBP plans cannot be governed by sourced contracts, and BDC insights have no Build/Joule consumption surface to reach the business.
The combined ~$45K BDC, ~$14K Commerce, ~$298K IBP, and ~30% Concur overruns are not isolated true-up events but evidence that FY26 commercial envelopes were sized against a prior, narrower architectural footprint — leaving Cargill structurally exposed to ceiling breaches in the very domains (data, planning, commerce, T&E) that underpin a global agri-food operator's resilience and margin agenda.
Convene a cross-domain BTP architecture review that consolidates BDC, Database Cloud, Integration Suite, Joule Studio, Build, and the dormant Ariba Cloud Platform EA under a single platform governance model with workload attribution and clean-core extensibility guardrails.
Re-baseline the FY27 commercial envelope across BDC, Commerce Cloud, IBP, and the Concur stack as a consolidated true-up tied to a documented enterprise architecture roadmap, converting reactive overruns into a planned expansion contract.
Run a connected-enterprise workshop that maps the integration chains Commerce→Sales/CDC, Ariba Sourcing→Contracts→Buying→S/4, and IBP→BDC→Joule, exposing the missing pillars and integration flows that block Cargill's closed-loop supply-to-customer story.
Activate the dormant Ariba Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement and SAP Build with a named procurement-to-S/4 integration and a planner-facing extension app, re-anchoring the under-consumed governance layers to concrete LoB use cases already in production.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
135.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$63K
YTD Budget$47K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $152K · Projected Budget: $114K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's Business Data Cloud foundation on BTP Enterprise Agreement is in runaway expansion — Public BDC consumption is pacing to $140,722 against a $103,239 ceiling (36% overrun) while Database Cloud is pacing to $11,582 against $10,567 (10% overrun), signaling that Data & AI workloads have moved from experimentation into production scale faster than the commercial envelope was sized to absorb.
The parallel overrun across both Public BDC and Database Cloud meters indicates Cargill is operating an integrated data fabric — persistence (Database Cloud) and platform services (Public BDC) are scaling in lockstep, which is architecturally healthy but means a ceiling breach on either meter will stall the entire Data & AI value chain, not just one component.
Three consecutive months above 100% attainment on Database Cloud combined with accelerating month-over-month growth on Public BDC (peaking at 149.8% in May) confirms this is steady-state production consumption, not ramp noise — the LoB is now operationally dependent on BTP for Data & AI outcomes and a service disruption from ceiling breach would directly impact business continuity.
Initiate a consolidated BTP true-up covering both Public BDC and Database Cloud meters on LPR868, right-sizing the combined ~$114K annual commitment against the ~$152K projected consumption to eliminate the ~$45K unfunded exposure.
Conduct a workload attribution review across the Data & AI estate to map which BDC services and database workloads are driving the sustained overage, separating strategic expansion from unoptimized consumption before resizing the FY27 commitment.
Establish consumption governance and alerting on the BTP Enterprise Agreement so that Data & AI workload growth is visible to the LoB owner ahead of ceiling breach rather than discovered in arrears.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
107.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$87K
YTD Budget$81K
YTD ACV: $6K
Projected Consumed: $209K · Projected Budget: $195K · Projected ACV: $15K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's BTP foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Integration Suite is the dominant, mature workload driving $61K YTD consumption at 107% attainment while the unlabeled 'Technology Foundation Other' BTP allocation sits at $0, indicating the platform is being used almost exclusively as an integration backbone rather than a broad extensibility and innovation fabric.
The low-code extensibility layer is actively regressing — SAP Build consumption has declined three consecutive months to 69.6% attainment, meaning the citizen-developer channel intended to extend Integration Suite flows and Joule experiences into business-facing apps is stalling just as the integration backbone scales, leaving integrations without a downstream consumption surface.
Joule Studio's BTP footprint is running hot at 118% attainment with volatile March/April spikes, signaling that AI/agent workloads are being provisioned without consumption governance — a pattern that will erode the economic case for scaling Joule across Cargill before the capability is proven.
The Technology Foundation is consuming capacity faster than it is converting to business capability — combined BTP overruns on Integration Suite and Joule Studio coexist with a dormant Build pipeline and an unactivated BTP allocation, meaning Cargill is paying for platform headroom that is not translating into new LoB-facing applications or governed AI use cases.
Establish a unified BTP consumption governance model spanning Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and the dormant Technology Foundation allocation, with service-level attribution and guardrails to convert the $6K–$10K projected overruns into predictable, workload-aligned spend.
Pair the SAP Build re-engagement workshop with Integration Suite owners to identify 2–3 integration flows that need a Build front-end or workflow, reconnecting the low-code layer to the integration investments already in production.
Activate the dormant Technology Foundation BTP entitlement by anchoring it to a specific Joule or Build extensibility use case, redirecting the $418 of elapsed unconsumed budget into a named capability rather than a generic run-rate recovery.
Run a Joule Studio workload review to classify the consumption spikes as either sanctioned agent pilots or uncontrolled experimentation, and formalize a Joule adoption roadmap before unmanaged burn forces a reactive true-up conversation.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
106.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$128K
YTD Budget$121K
YTD ACV: $131K
Projected Consumed: $308K · Projected Budget: $294K · Projected ACV: $314K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's Customer Experience footprint is architecturally singular — SAP Commerce Cloud is the only CX capability in play, meaning the storefront is running without adjacent CX pillars (Sales, Service, Marketing, CDC) that would normally close the loop from lead capture to post-sale engagement, leaving the B2B commerce channel as a transactional silo rather than a connected customer experience.
The flat $25,042 consumption in Apr–May signals Commerce Cloud has reached steady-state adoption on a narrow set of use cases, which architecturally means capabilities like AI-driven personalization, omnichannel unification, and insight generation are licensed but likely dormant — Cargill is paying for a platform designed to grow sales across every channel while operating it as a single-channel order engine.
Projected $308,315 consumption against a $294,018 annual budget at the 5/12 reporting mark indicates the contract envelope was scoped for the prior commerce footprint, not the current run-rate, creating a governance exposure where B2B transaction growth will outpace contractual capacity before the CX architecture has been expanded to monetize that growth elsewhere.
Map Cargill's active Commerce Cloud use cases against the platform's personalization, unified commerce, and AI capabilities to identify dormant functions that should be activated to justify the consumption trajectory.
Open a CX architecture conversation positioning SAP Emarsys, Sales Cloud, or Customer Data Cloud as the missing layers that would convert Commerce Cloud's transaction data into retention and cross-sell outcomes for Cargill's B2B buyers.
Scope a true-up and capacity expansion path on Commerce Cloud tied explicitly to Cargill's B2B commerce roadmap to convert the $14,297 projected overrun into a planned expansion rather than an unbudgeted overage.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD Budget$2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.2M
Projected Consumed: $4.8M · Projected Budget: $5.3M · Projected ACV: $5.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's Source-to-Pay architecture is fundamentally unbalanced — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is the only procurement product running near plan ($1.6M consumed YTD) while Sourcing (38%), Contracts (45%), Catalog (75%), and SIPM (~83%) all lag, meaning the LoB is paying invoices at scale but without the upstream sourcing events, contractual governance, catalog compliance, or supplier performance discipline that justify the platform investment.
The Travel and Expense stack is the inverse problem — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, and Company Bill Statement are all consistently consuming 124–142% of plan, indicating T&E is a mission-critical, fully embedded capability whose contract envelope was structurally undersized rather than an adoption risk.
Ariba Contracts' 45% attainment with a declining May trajectory means negotiated savings from Sourcing events have no enforcement layer downstream, so even the limited sourcing volume Cargill is running is leaking value into Buying and Invoicing without contractual price, term, or compliance controls flowing through.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sitting at $0 consumption signals the extension and integration fabric intended to connect Ariba, Concur, and S/4HANA is dormant, leaving Finance and Spend Management as a set of point solutions rather than a composable platform — this is the root architectural constraint behind the shallow, single-team usage patterns visible across Sourcing, SIPM, and Catalog.
Re-sequence the procurement enablement plan to lead with Ariba Sourcing and Contracts adoption (category manager onboarding, event pipeline build, contract migration into the clause library) so that downstream Buying and Invoicing volume is governed by negotiated agreements rather than ad-hoc PO flow.
Open a consolidated true-up conversation across Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, and Company Bill Statement to right-size the T&E contract envelope against the demonstrated ~130% run rate and convert the overage into a renewal baseline.
Activate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement with a first workload tied to a concrete Finance and Spend Management integration use case (Ariba-to-S/4 catalog sync, Concur-to-GL posting, or supplier master federation) to unblock the platform layer that the rest of the portfolio depends on.
Run a catalog and SIPM coverage audit to identify which spend categories and supplier segments are bypassing Ariba Catalog and scorecarding, since the flat consumption baselines indicate a single-team footprint rather than enterprise-wide procurement compliance.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
107.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.8M
YTD Budget$1.7M
YTD ACV: $1.7M
Projected Consumed: $4.3M · Projected Budget: $4.0M · Projected ACV: $4.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's supply chain planning architecture is anchored solely on IBP, meaning demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response are all converging on a single platform — adoption is real but the LoB has no architectural redundancy if IBP planning workloads spike unpredictably as they did in Q1.
The Q1 overage followed by exact-to-budget Apr-May consumption suggests IBP is being used reactively for scenario-heavy planning runs rather than as a steady-state S&OP rhythm, indicating the collaborative unified planning capability is not yet operating as a disciplined monthly cadence.
With YTD consumption at 107.5% of plan ($1.77M of $1.65M) and seven months remaining against a $3.97M annual ceiling, the LoB is architecturally healthy on capability adoption but financially exposed to a ~$298K breach if scenario-driven spikes resurface.
Establish an IBP workload governance model with Cargill that separates baseline S&OP cycles from ad-hoc scenario simulations, with consumption guardrails tied to each planning persona (demand, supply, inventory, response).
Validate whether Apr-May exact-to-budget consumption reflects genuine steady-state planning or artificial throttling of legitimate planner activity, and rebalance the forward plan against the remaining $2.31M budget.
Confirm AI-powered demand prediction and multilevel supply planning capabilities are actively driving the consumption rather than manual forecast overrides, to ensure the LoB is realizing the intended architectural value of IBP.
Conagra Brands, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $533K
YTD Budget: $548K
YTD ACV: $481K
Projected Consumed: $1.3M
Projected Budget: $1.5M
Projected ACV: $1.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Integration Suite is structurally over-consumed across both BAIP (175% YTD) and Finance (SSO at 136%) while every paired platform service — Database Cloud, Technology Foundation Other, Cloud Platform EA in Finance, and SAC BI/Planning — is dormant or declining, revealing that Conagra is using SAP almost exclusively as integration plumbing for its existing estate rather than as the governed extension and decisioning platform the EA was sized to deliver.
The decision layer is collapsing in parallel across domains — SAC BI at 41%, SAC Planning declining four straight months, Ariba Spend Analysis flatlined to $0, and Profitability & Performance Management spiking episodically — meaning Conagra has built the data and transaction pipes but has no coherent analytics consumption surface, blocking the integrated finance, supply chain, and category strategy outcomes a CPG operator needs to defend margin against commodity and trade volatility.
Dormant foundational entitlements span every L1 — Database Cloud (~$10.5K unused), Technology Foundation Other ($7.1K at $0), Cloud Platform EA in Finance ($0 across five months), and Ariba Supplier Risk (~$17.5K unused) — indicating Conagra is paying for the governance, risk, and extension fabric of its SAP estate but operating without it, so over-consumed integration and AI workloads are running outside the controls the architecture was designed to enforce.
Total contracted envelope is being simultaneously overrun on runtime/integration (BTP Public +91%, Integration Suite +75%, SSO +36%, PaPM +257% in May) and underrun on intelligence and foundation tiers, producing a projected ~$80K+ commercial exposure on consumable surfaces while a comparable value of strategic entitlement decays unused — a net-negative architectural ROI despite gross contract health.
Commission a single cross-domain BTP architecture review covering Integration Suite, Database Cloud, Technology Foundation Other, and the Finance Cloud Platform EA to rebalance over-consumed integration workloads onto dormant foundational and data-tier entitlements under unified governance.
Stand up a named-use-case adoption program that connects SAC BI, SAC Planning, Profitability & Performance Management, and Ariba Spend Analysis into a single closed-loop finance and supply chain decisioning scenario, reversing the simultaneous decline of Conagra's analytics consumption layer.
Embed dormant governance entitlements — Ariba Supplier Risk into SLP and Sourcing gates, and Technology Foundation Other controls over Integration Suite flows — to convert standalone monitoring and unused platform services into the integrated risk and compliance fabric the architecture intends.
Initiate a consolidated commercial true-up and rightsizing conversation that addresses the ~$80K+ overrun on Public BTP, Integration Suite, and SSO while repositioning the unused Database Cloud, Technology Foundation Other, Supplier Risk, and Finance Cloud Platform EA capacity toward net-new Joule and finance extension use cases.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
162.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$110K
YTD Budget$68K
YTD ACV: $2K
Projected Consumed: $264K · Projected Budget: $234K · Projected ACV: $5K
Solution Insights and Actions
Conagra's BAIP Data & AI footprint is architecturally lopsided — the BTP runtime (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, Public) is consuming at 191% of plan while the analytics consumption layer (SAC BI) sits at 41% attainment, meaning the LoB is paying for raw platform capacity it cannot translate into decision-ready insights for finance and supply chain users.
The Database Cloud instance of Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is in steady-state at ~30% attainment with a projected $10,560 unused, indicating the data foundation is provisioned but not being activated for new analytical or AI workloads — a dormant data tier directly explains why SAC BI engagement is narrow and drifting downward.
SAC BI's declining burn (March $151 → May $101) against a healthy BTP Public runtime signals that planning, Joule-driven analytics, and unified financial/operational planning capabilities contracted in the catalog are unrealized, leaving Conagra with infrastructure cost growth but no corresponding business intelligence outcome.
The combination of overrunning Public BTP consumption and underused Database Cloud entitlement points to unmanaged compute-heavy workloads bypassing the governed data layer, creating both a $43K commercial exposure and an architectural risk that AI/analytics use cases are being built outside the intended Data & AI reference architecture.
Commission a joint BTP and SAC BI architecture review to map which Public-tier services are driving the 91% overrun and reroute eligible analytics, integration, and AI workloads onto the underutilized Database Cloud entitlement to rebalance consumption within contract.
Stand up a named-use-case adoption plan for SAC BI anchored on Conagra finance and supply chain planning scenarios, activating unified planning and Joule capabilities to convert dormant analytics entitlement into measurable LoB decisions.
Establish consumption governance across both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement instances with workload tagging and monthly attainment thresholds so the LoB can detect runaway Public-tier growth and stalled Database Cloud activation before they compound.
Initiate a commercial conversation to rightsize the Public BTP envelope to cover the projected $43,062 gap while repositioning unused Database Cloud capacity toward net-new Data & AI workloads identified in the architecture review.

Projected Consumed: $156K · Projected Budget: $143K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Conagra's BTP Technology Foundation is bifurcated — Integration Suite is the dominant, over-consumed workload (175% YTD attainment, $59.7K against $34.1K budget) while Joule Studio is just crossing activation and Technology Foundation Other remains fully dormant, indicating the LoB is leaning hard on integration plumbing without building out the AI orchestration and broader platform services the EA was sized to deliver.
Integration Suite's structural overrun ($25.6K cumulative, projected $35.9K annual overshoot) signals that Conagra is using BTP primarily as an integration backbone for its SAP estate, but the absence of paired platform services means integrations are being built without governed extension, automation, or AI surfaces — the LoB is wiring systems together without architecting differentiated business capability on top.
Joule Studio's 10x ramp from $227 to $2,512 shows the LoB has begun experimenting with AI agent development, but at a $12.7K projected run rate against $28.7K budget it remains a proof-of-concept footprint rather than a production capability, leaving Conagra's generative AI strategy on BTP under-realized.
The $0 consumption on Technology Foundation Other against a $7.1K commitment means foundational BTP services (likely runtime, security, or governance tooling) are contractually available but operationally absent, creating an architectural gap where Integration Suite workloads run without the platform-level controls the EA was designed to provide.
Initiate a consumption governance and true-up conversation on Integration Suite to address the projected $35.9K overrun, identifying the top-burn integration flows and determining whether to rightsize, commercially expand, or impose workload controls.
Convert Joule Studio's May momentum into a production roadmap by working with Conagra's platform owners to identify 2-3 concrete AI agent use cases that consume the remaining $15.9K of contracted Joule capacity.
Run an activation workshop on the dormant Technology Foundation Other entitlement to identify which foundational BTP services Conagra should stand up to govern the already over-consumed Integration Suite footprint.
Conduct a unified BTP architecture review with Conagra to rebalance the EA across integration, AI, and foundational services so the platform delivers a coherent capability stack rather than a single over-leveraged integration workload.

YTD Budget$422K
YTD ACV: $479K
Projected Consumed: $860K · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $1.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Conagra's Source-to-Pay spine is architecturally fragmented — Ariba Sourcing and SLP are running near plan while Ariba Spend Analysis has flatlined to $0 since April, severing the closed-loop where spend visibility feeds sourcing pipeline and supplier segmentation, leaving procurement executing events without data-driven category strategy.
The profitability and planning stack is operating in opposite directions — Profitability and Performance Management spiked to 357% in May after three dormant months while SAC Planning has declined four straight months to 88.6%, indicating finance is running episodic profitability models disconnected from the continuous integrated planning loop SAC was meant to anchor.
Supplier risk intelligence is structurally underutilized at 68–75% attainment with ~$17.5K of ceiling projected to go unused, meaning Conagra owns the risk-monitoring layer but is not embedding it into SLP qualification or sourcing award decisions — the architectural integration that turns Ariba Supplier Risk into preventative governance is absent.
Identity and platform foundations are inverted — Single Sign-on is overconsuming at 136% indicating real workload pull on the authentication layer, while Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 across five months, signaling users are accessing SAP estate at scale but the extension and integration platform meant to host new finance workloads has never been activated.
Diagnose the Ariba Spend Analysis stoppage by tracing whether the data feed from S/4HANA and Ariba Buying broke, the analytics owner departed, or category dashboards were decommissioned, and restore the spend visibility input that drives Sourcing and SLP category strategy.
Reconcile Profitability and Performance Management usage with SAC Planning by defining which profitability models feed planning drivers, replacing the May burst pattern with recurring allocation cycles that also reverse SAC's four-month consumption decline.
Embed Ariba Supplier Risk signals into SLP qualification gates and Sourcing award workflows for top supplier tiers, converting flat standalone monitoring into integrated procurement decisioning and lifting the ~30% utilization gap.
Launch a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop tied to a concrete finance extension use case — such as profitability data integration or SSO-enabled custom apps — to unlock the dormant platform before backloaded burn becomes unrecoverable.
Coop Regions Of Organic Producer
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $119K
YTD Budget: $127K
YTD ACV: $135K
Projected Consumed: $286K
Projected Budget: $306K
Projected ACV: $325K
EA Insights and Actions
Coop's entire enterprise architecture is a collection of four single-product islands — IBP for supply chain, SAC Planning for finance, Datasphere+SAC BI for analytics, Integration Suite for technology — with no transactional ERP backbone visible and no cross-domain data flow, meaning the cooperative is running planning, analytics, and integration capabilities that were architected to orbit a core they don't appear to have, capping every L1 outcome at standalone-tool value rather than connected enterprise value.
Integration Suite is the only over-consumed asset in the entire estate (10–23% monthly overage) while every adjacent extensibility runtime — Joule Studio CPEA, BTP CPEA, SAP Build at 64% — sits dormant, revealing an enterprise using SAP as point-to-point pipes rather than a platform, which structurally blocks the AI, agentic, and clean-core extension patterns that would let IBP, SAC Planning, and Datasphere talk to each other as a connected decisioning fabric.
Datasphere is provisioned as the semantic layer for the whole enterprise yet IBP's $56,495 of planning data and SAC Planning's $18,157 of financial models are not visibly flowing into it, meaning the cooperative is paying ~$45K annually for a unified data foundation while its two highest-consuming planning workloads remain semantically disconnected — the exact closed-loop producer-to-finance planning story organic cooperatives need for harvest-aligned margin management is architecturally unreachable.
Across a combined contracted footprint dominated by IBP ($135,593 ACV, 42% consumed) and SAC Planning ($46,097 ACV, ~39% consumed), more than half of committed value is unrealized YTD while consumption patterns (flat linear IBP burn, late-cycle SAC spike) indicate narrow single-use-case deployments, putting renewal defensibility at risk precisely because the architectural breadth that justifies the contract values has never been activated.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review to map the data and process flows that should connect IBP demand/supply plans, SAC financial plans, Datasphere semantic models, and SAC BI consumption, and identify the missing ERP-of-record dependency that currently prevents any closed-loop producer-to-finance scenario.
Launch a BTP activation program that stands up the dormant Joule Studio and BTP CPEA tenants with a first workload extending IBP or SAC Planning, breaking the integration-only usage pattern and creating the extensibility layer required for AI and agentic scenarios across all four L1 domains.
Run a single joint use-case workshop that defines one organic-producer outcome (e.g., harvest-aligned demand-to-cash planning) spanning IBP, SAC Planning, Datasphere, and SAC BI, converting four isolated workloads into one connected architectural story that simultaneously closes the SAC BI ($1,830), Datasphere ($5,934), SAC Planning ($27,940), and IBP ($79,098) consumption gaps.
Build a contract-realization scorecard quantifying activated versus dormant modules within IBP and SAC Planning against their ACV, and use it to drive a depth-of-capability expansion plan ahead of renewal rather than relying on flat-line consumption as evidence of value.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
70.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$18K
YTD Budget$26K
YTD ACV: $33K
Projected Consumed: $44K · Projected Budget: $64K · Projected ACV: $80K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI stack for Coop is architecturally coherent but operationally narrow — Datasphere is provisioned as the semantic data layer feeding SAC BI, yet both are stuck at ~70% and ~35% attainment respectively, meaning the unified data foundation is running but the consumption layer that turns it into business decisions is barely lit up.
SAC BI's flat ~$200/month consumption against a $2,814 YTD budget indicates the visualization and planning capability is deployed as static reporting only, leaving Coop's organic producer operations without the trusted-AI decisioning, unified financial/operational planning, and Joule-driven analytics the platform is architected to deliver.
Datasphere's tight $3,218–$3,721 monthly band against a rising $4,776 budget and a $5,934 YTD shortfall shows the semantic layer is hosting a single static workload — federated access, knowledge graph, and AI-ready modeling capabilities are paid for but not extended to new domains like supplier or producer analytics.
The downstream consumption gap in SAC BI directly caps the upstream value of Datasphere — without new stories and models consuming the semantic layer, the ~$1,400/month Datasphere budget gap cannot be closed by tuning alone because there is no analytical demand pulling new data products through.
Run a joint Datasphere–SAC BI use-case expansion workshop to define a producer/supplier analytics data product in Datasphere paired with 2–3 SAC dashboards that consume it, closing both the $1,830 SAC gap and the $5,934 Datasphere gap with a single business outcome.
Audit Datasphere active spaces, replication flows, and modeled entities to confirm whether the ~30% under-attainment is driven by under-scaled compute, dormant integrations, or unonboarded business users, and remediate the specific bottleneck before adding new workloads.
Activate SAC BI's planning and Joule capabilities by onboarding net-new business users from finance and operations into at least one integrated plan tied to organic producer KPIs, converting the tool from static reporting into the unified analytics-and-planning asset it is licensed as.
Establish a semantic-layer governance pattern where every new SAC story is sourced from a Datasphere business-context model, ensuring the architectural dependency between the two products translates into compounding adoption rather than parallel underuse.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
104.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$26K
YTD Budget$25K
YTD ACV: $21K
Projected Consumed: $62K · Projected Budget: $60K · Projected ACV: $50K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is the only load-bearing capability in this foundation — SAP Integration Suite is running 10–23% over budget every month while the BTP runtime (CPEA) that should host extensions and low-code apps sits at $0 consumption, meaning Coop Regions Of Organic Producer is using SAP purely as a pipe between systems rather than as a platform to build differentiated capabilities on.
SAP Build adoption is structurally trapped at single-team scale — 64% YTD attainment driven by sporadic project bursts (April spike, May collapse) indicates citizen development exists as a tactical tool rather than an embedded LoB practice, so the architectural promise of business-led extension of SAP processes is unrealized.
Integration Suite consumption is decelerating just as new AI-era API and event-driven scenarios should be expanding the footprint — the May dip below budget after a Jan–Mar burst signals the initial interface portfolio is built and no second wave of use cases is in the pipeline, capping the LoB's ability to extend integration into agent and AI-ready API patterns.
The Joule Studio CPEA tenant being completely unactivated while the Integration Suite CPEA is over-consumed reveals a split-brain BTP estate — the LoB has runtime capacity contracted for AI and developer extensibility that no one has been enabled to consume, putting $69 YTD and $174 annual entitlement at full forfeiture risk.
Stand up the Joule Studio CPEA tenant by provisioning subaccounts, entitlements, and developer access, and pair activation with a first-workload commitment tied to an existing SAP Build or Integration Suite use case so the platform has an immediate consumer.
Open a capacity uplift and consumption review on the Integration Suite CPEA to right-size the $10,157 ceiling against demonstrated run rate while confirming the 10–23% monthly overage reflects high-value workloads rather than unoptimized flows.
Establish an SAP Build center-of-excellence template seeded from the April use case and onboard makers from at least two business units outside the current consuming team to break the single-workload adoption ceiling.
Run a joint Integration Suite and Build use-case discovery focused on API-led, event-driven, and AI-agent scenarios so the tapering integration pipeline and dormant low-code runtime are refilled from the same business backlog.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
95.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$18K
YTD Budget$19K
YTD ACV: $25K
Projected Consumed: $44K · Projected Budget: $46K · Projected ACV: $59K
Solution Insights and Actions
Coop's Finance and Spend Management architecture rests on a single deployed capability — SAC Planning — meaning the LoB has unified planning and analytics technically available but no surrounding FP&A ecosystem (no integrated financial close, spend analytics, or operational planning peers) to amplify its value, leaving planning as an isolated workload rather than a connected finance backbone.
May's 111% attainment after four sub-95% months signals the LoB has crossed from pilot-mode into active planning usage, but with only $18,157 of $46,097 ACV consumed YTD, the architectural footprint is still narrow — likely one model or cohort driving the spike — and the unified financial/operational planning capability the product is designed to deliver remains largely unrealized.
The absence of adjacent FP&A or spend management products means SAC Planning is operating without the transactional and operational data context it was architected to consume, limiting Coop to standalone financial planning rather than the cross-domain planning-with-business-context outcome the platform enables.
Identify the planning models and user cohorts behind May's $4,249 consumption and extend them into a second use case such as workforce or sales planning to broaden the active architectural footprint and close the $27,940 ACV gap.
Scope an integration path between SAC Planning and Coop's operational data sources (finance actuals, headcount, sales) to activate the unified financial-and-operational planning capability that is currently dormant in the architecture.
Position a complementary spend or analytics capability within Finance and Spend Management to give the LoB the cross-domain visibility SAC Planning is designed to act on, converting isolated planning into closed-loop decision-making.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$56K
YTD Budget$56K
YTD ACV: $56K
Projected Consumed: $136K · Projected Budget: $136K · Projected ACV: $136K
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the sole active pillar of the Supply Chain Management architecture for Coop Regions Of Organic Producer, meaning demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response are all dependent on a single platform with no complementary execution or visibility products to close the plan-to-execute loop.
The lockstep $11,299/month consumption pattern (YTD $56,495 of $135,593, 42% consumed) signals operationalized planning rhythm, but flat linear burn across five months is architecturally inconsistent with a cooperative producer's seasonal demand and harvest cycles — suggesting IBP is likely running a narrow forecasting use case rather than the full multilevel supply and S&OP collaboration it is designed to deliver.
With $79,098 (58%) of contract value still to consume and no adjacent SCM products in the estate, the renewal case rests entirely on demonstrable IBP capability depth — if consumption reflects only recurring demand forecasting, the LoB is paying for an integrated planning platform while using it as a point forecasting tool.
Run an IBP capability-depth audit with the LoB to map which modules (demand, inventory, S&OP, response) are actively configured versus licensed-but-dormant, and tie each to a producer-cooperative planning outcome such as harvest-aligned supply response or member demand collaboration.
Build the architectural case for extending IBP into supply execution and visibility by identifying where the cooperative is currently relying on spreadsheets or disconnected systems to act on IBP plans, exposing the plan-to-execute gap.
Document the planning outcomes already realized from the $56,495 consumed — forecast accuracy lift, inventory turns, S&OP cycle compression — as a quantified renewal proof point and as the baseline for justifying deeper module activation.
Corteva Agriscience LLC
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $3.0M
YTD Budget: $3.0M
YTD ACV: $3.7M
Projected Consumed: $7.3M
Projected Budget: $7.2M
Projected ACV: $9.0M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is contracted as the connective tissue across every L1 domain — Data & AI (BDC Public at $0), Finance (Cloud Platform EA at $0), and Technology Foundation (Other CPEA at $0) — yet all three foundational entitlements sit unactivated while Integration Suite and Joule Studio runtimes overshoot by 200–900%, meaning Corteva is consuming SAP's platform as a tactical integration utility rather than the strategic extension and AI fabric it was sold as, which blocks any cross-domain digital agriculture play that depends on unified data, planning, and AI.
The enterprise shows a consistent 'execution without intelligence' pattern — Ariba transacts but Spend Analysis is dormant, IBP plans at 158% but has no downstream execution consumers, Datasphere is provisioned but SAC BI sits at 0.2% — meaning Corteva is generating transactional volume across Finance and Supply Chain with no analytical or decision layer closing the loop, which directly undermines the data-driven seed/crop-protection portfolio decisions the business depends on.
IBP's runaway 158% attainment with a projected ~$987K overage, combined with Integration Suite's $230K vs $65K trajectory, signals that Corteva's two most business-critical capabilities (integrated planning and integration) are operating outside their commercial envelopes while three foundational BTP agreements sit at $0 — the contract portfolio is structurally misaligned with actual workload distribution, exposing the account to mid-year capacity exhaustion in the exact domains that drive revenue continuity.
Only the Concur/Travel & Expense cluster demonstrates healthy steady-state adoption (paired with Preferred Success at 105.6%), while every other domain — Procurement intelligence, Treasury breadth, FP&A extension, Datasphere/SAC, SAP Build — shows the same enablement vacuum, proving Corteva's architectural underperformance is not a product problem but a systemic absence of adoption services tied to the platform investments.
Convene an enterprise-level BTP activation and true-up review that consolidates the three dormant CPEA/Platform agreements (BDC Public, Finance Cloud Platform EA, Technology Foundation Other) and the two overshooting runtimes (Integration Suite, Joule Studio) into a single right-sized platform envelope bound to named cross-domain workloads.
Run a cross-domain 'closed-loop' architecture workshop that sequences Datasphere→SAC BI, Ariba transactions→Spend Analysis, and IBP plans→execution/analytics consumers as one integrated decision fabric rather than three disconnected domain fixes.
Open an immediate IBP capacity expansion and planning-continuity conversation to address the ~$987K projected overage before mid-year exhaustion halts Corteva's S&OP cycles, given no fallback planning capability exists in the estate.
Redirect the Preferred Success Ariba advisory surplus and equivalent enablement capacity toward the dormant intelligence and extensibility layers (Spend Analysis, SAC BI, SAP Build) to replicate the Concur adoption pattern across Procurement, Analytics, and low-code domains.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
28.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$12K
YTD Budget$43K
YTD ACV: $68K
Projected Consumed: $29K · Projected Budget: $105K · Projected ACV: $163K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Corteva is architecturally inverted — Datasphere is provisioned as the unified semantic layer but running at ~24% attainment with a flat single-tenant footprint, while SAC BI (its intended consumption surface) sits at 0.2% attainment, meaning the LoB has neither a populated data fabric nor the visualization layer to act on it, leaving the entire BDC Public investment dormant.
The BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is a non-started contract at $0 consumption, meaning the BTP runtime that underpins extension, integration, and AI workloads for Datasphere and SAC has never been activated — without this foundation, any downstream Datasphere expansion or SAC BI activation will hit provisioning friction.
The Database Cloud BTP agreement is running at 3x its planned ceiling while the BDC Public equivalent sits idle, indicating Corteva's actual Data & AI workloads are landing on database infrastructure rather than the contracted analytics platform — the LoB is consuming data services but bypassing the semantic and analytical layer SAP sold them.
Datasphere's flat $1,400/month run rate signals a single pilot tenant with no expansion of connected sources or modeled views, which means the 'AI-ready data' and federated knowledge graph capabilities Corteva contracted for are architecturally present but functionally untapped — blocking any downstream Joule or AI use cases.
Re-baseline the BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by activating the first BTP subaccount and binding it to the existing Datasphere tenant to unblock the analytics stack foundation.
Run a joint Datasphere–SAC BI activation workshop with Corteva's analytics team to land one end-to-end use case that flows from a modeled Datasphere view into a production SAC dashboard, establishing the semantic-to-visualization path.
Initiate a true-up and workload review on the Database Cloud LPR868 to recontract the ceiling and confirm whether the overshoot represents sanctioned Data & AI workloads that should be migrated onto the underutilized BDC Public entitlements.
Engage Corteva's BI sponsor to secure named power users and a recurring dashboard portfolio on SAC BI, converting exploratory touches into production consumption tied to Datasphere models.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
184.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD Budget$102K
YTD ACV: $145K
Projected Consumed: $448K · Projected Budget: $284K · Projected ACV: $347K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Integration Suite and its underlying BTP runtime are massively over-consumed (CPEA at 7x budget driving $230K projected vs $65K, Integration Suite at 107% YTD), proving Corteva has operationalized SAP as the integration backbone for business-critical workloads while the contract envelope underrepresents this reality.
The low-code extensibility layer is stalled — SAP Build consumption is flat at ~$953/month (26% YTD attainment) and the Joule Studio CPEA slice is also undersized, meaning Corteva is consuming BTP heavily for integration but failing to activate the citizen-developer and app-extension capabilities that would convert integrated data into LoB-facing applications.
The Technology Foundation Other CPEA allocation sits at $0 consumed across five months, indicating a contracted-but-never-activated BTP entitlement that represents pure shelfware and signals a governance gap in how subaccounts and entitlements are being provisioned against named workloads.
The Joule Studio CPEA over-run (334% attainment, $44K projected vs $13K budget) alongside flat SAP Build adoption suggests AI/agent tooling consumption is growing on the platform without a corresponding low-code surface to expose those capabilities to business users, creating an architectural imbalance between AI runtime and AI-enabled application delivery.
Initiate a consolidated BTP/CPEA true-up with Corteva that re-baselines the Integration Suite runtime ($230K trajectory), Joule Studio runtime ($44K trajectory), and Technology Foundation Other ($0) allocations into a single right-sized commercial envelope reflecting actual workload distribution.
Launch a SAP Build expansion and enablement program targeting 3–5 net-new low-code apps that consume the already-active Integration Suite APIs, converting the integration investment into LoB-visible applications and absorbing the ~$29K Build budget shortfall.
Run a workload and service-level audit across both CPEA slices driving 334%–975% attainment to confirm consumption is tied to production business value, identify optimization opportunities, and govern non-essential burn before true-up.
Convene an activation workshop for the dormant Technology Foundation Other CPEA entitlement to bind it to a named workload or formally retire it, eliminating the shelfware signal from the foundation portfolio.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
75.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.3M
YTD Budget$1.7M
YTD ACV: $2.0M
Projected Consumed: $3.1M · Projected Budget: $4.1M · Projected ACV: $4.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay architecture is fundamentally broken at the intelligence layer — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is transacting at $691K and Ariba Sourcing is running RFx events, but Ariba Spend Analysis has been dormant since January and Ariba Catalog collapsed to ~$50/month, meaning Corteva is executing procurement without category intelligence or guided buying compliance, which is why off-platform leakage is showing up as the $170K YTD gap in Buying.
The Treasury stack is architecturally complete but operationally hollow — Cash & Treasury, Multi Bank Connect, Digital Payments, and S/4HANA Customer Payments are all consuming at 42–74% of plan with flat or declining run rates, indicating the integrated cash-to-payment foundation is provisioned but only servicing a narrow set of bank connections and payment flows rather than functioning as the enterprise treasury hub it was designed to be.
The Financial Planning & Analysis pillar is regressing rather than maturing — SAC Planning dropped to 55.6% in May after three months of 86–91% attainment, and the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumption across five months, meaning the LoB has neither extended its planning models into adjacent workflows nor activated the BTP foundation needed to build planning extensions, integrations, or AI-driven scenarios.
The Travel & Expense estate is the one fully embedded capability cluster — Concur Expense, Reporting Services, Connectors, and Preferred Success are all running at or above plan, demonstrating that when SAP capability is paired with active enablement (Preferred Success at 105.6%) the LoB achieves steady-state adoption, a pattern conspicuously absent from Procurement and Treasury.
Reconstruct the P2P intelligence loop by reactivating Ariba Spend Analysis data feeds and relaunching Ariba Catalog enablement across Corteva's top indirect spend categories so that Buying and Sourcing decisions are governed by visible spend patterns and compliant channels rather than ad-hoc buyer behavior.
Convene a Treasury architecture review with Corteva to map active bank connections, payment types, and entities across Cash & Treasury, Multi Bank Connect, Digital Payments, and Customer Payments, and sequence a rollout that expands coverage to untapped subsidiaries and payment scenarios as a single integrated workstream.
Land a first BTP workload on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement — ideally a SAC Planning extension or Ariba/Concur integration — to break the $0 baseline and create the technical foundation for the planning, analytics, and procurement extensions the LoB will need to scale.
Redirect Preferred Success Ariba's queued advisory capacity (which hit the ACV ceiling in May) toward the dormant Procurement assets — Spend Analysis activation, Catalog enablement, and Sourcing category expansion — converting suppressed advisory demand into measurable adoption recovery.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
127.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.6M
YTD Budget$1.2M
YTD ACV: $1.6M
Projected Consumed: $3.7M · Projected Budget: $2.8M · Projected ACV: $3.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Corteva's Supply Chain Management architecture is anchored on a single planning pillar — IBP is the only contracted capability, meaning demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response are all converging onto one platform with no adjacent execution or visibility products to absorb load or extend value.
The accelerating consumption curve (100% to 158.1% attainment in five months) indicates IBP has moved beyond pilot or single-workload use into broad planner adoption across multiple planning horizons — the LoB is architecturally treating IBP as its system of record for integrated planning, which is the intended design outcome but is now outpacing contracted capacity.
With $1.19M remaining against a run rate above $350K/month, IBP will exhaust capacity before year end and create a planning continuity risk — because no fallback planning capability exists in the contracted estate, any throttling or service disruption directly halts S&OP, demand, and supply response cycles for Corteva.
The escalating burn pattern without corresponding signals from complementary supply chain execution or analytics products suggests IBP outputs may be siloed — plans are being generated at increasing volume but the architecture lacks contracted downstream consumers to convert those plans into executed supply, inventory, and response actions.
Open a capacity expansion conversation for IBP to close the projected $987K overage and protect planning continuity given the absence of any alternative planning capability in the estate.
Run a workload decomposition review with Corteva planning leaders to identify which IBP modules (demand, inventory, S&OP, response) are driving the 158% attainment and confirm the growth reflects strategic adoption rather than inefficient model design or unmanaged scenario sprawl.
Assess the downstream architecture gap by evaluating how IBP plans flow into execution and analytics systems, and recommend complementary SAP supply chain capabilities to ensure planning outputs translate into measurable LoB outcomes.
Establish a consumption governance cadence for IBP with monthly attainment tracking and planner-level usage attribution to prevent recurrence of mid-year capacity exhaustion.
Domino's Pizza LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $803K
YTD Budget: $866K
YTD ACV: $958K
Projected Consumed: $1.9M
Projected Budget: $2.1M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
Domino's has contracted a complete digital-extension and data-intelligence spine — HANA Cloud, Integration Suite, SAP Build, CPEA, Custom Domain — but only Integration Suite is running near plan while Build (57%), Custom Domain (48%), CPEA (76%), and HANA's surrounding BDC fabric are absent or declining, meaning data is moving between systems with no low-code apps, analytics, or branded endpoints consuming it, which structurally blocks the digital-ordering and franchisee-experience differentiation that defines competitive advantage in QSR.
The Sourcing→Contracts→Buying→Supplier Collaboration→IBP value chain — the core architecture that protects ingredient margin and supply resiliency for a 20,000-store pizza operator — is broken in three places simultaneously (Contracts at 66% and declining, Sourcing down 34%, Business Network and IBP flat-lined), meaning Domino's is transacting spend and burning planning licenses without an enforceable, forecast-driven supplier ecosystem, exposing food cost and availability to uncontracted leakage.
Concur's T&E stack is the only domain demonstrating a closed-loop architecture (capture → digitize → analyze → over-consume against contract), proving Domino's can industrialize SAP when enablement is wired to a real business process — the same pattern is conspicuously absent from Procurement, Supply Chain, and BTP, indicating the enterprise gap is not technology coverage but adoption-architecture discipline.
HANA Cloud sits as an isolated database under a contracted Data & AI ambition while BTP integration and extension layers decline in parallel, so even if Datasphere/SAC were added, there are no Build apps or Custom Domain endpoints to surface insights to store operators or franchisees — the AI-to-decision pathway is architecturally severed at both ends.
Convene a single cross-domain BTP and Business Data Cloud architecture review that binds HANA Cloud, Integration Suite, Build, CPEA, and Custom Domain to two anchor use cases — store performance analytics and franchisee operations apps — so the extension, integration, data, and exposure layers are reactivated as one stack rather than four declining products.
Launch a Source-to-Pay-to-Plan integration workshop that reconnects Ariba Sourcing→Contracts→Buying with Business Network and IBP forecast collaboration, treating the $91K Sourcing underrun, dormant Contracts, and $23K Business Network gap as one broken supplier value chain rather than four product adoption issues.
Codify the Concur closed-loop enablement pattern into a reusable adoption-architecture playbook and apply it first to Ariba Contracts authoring and IBP planner activation, where contracted capacity exists but business process wiring does not.
Run an enterprise contract-vs-consumption true-up across all L1 areas to rebalance the Concur over-consumption, the ~$200K combined underrun across Build, Custom Domain, Sourcing, and Business Network, and confirm whether HANA and IBP ratable spend reflect outcomes or shelfware before the next renewal cycle.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
75.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$41K
YTD Budget$54K
YTD ACV: $67K
Projected Consumed: $98K · Projected Budget: $133K · Projected ACV: $161K
Solution Insights and Actions
Domino's Data & AI foundation is anchored solely on HANA Cloud as the database layer, but the catalog's intended architecture extends to a full Business Data Cloud fabric (Datasphere, SAC, data products) — without those companion layers, HANA is operating as a raw compute engine rather than the agentic AI and analytics backbone it was contracted to enable.
The sustained attainment drift from 82.8% in January to ~70% in April–May indicates HANA Cloud workloads are narrowing rather than expanding, which architecturally signals the LoB is not onboarding new analytical or AI use cases onto the platform and is leaving the $35K projected annual shortfall as dormant AI/analytics capacity.
With HANA Cloud consumed in isolation, Domino's has the mission-critical data substrate in place but no visible semantic modeling or business-user analytics consumption pattern, meaning store operations, supply chain, and customer data are technically queryable but not yet curated into decision-ready data products for the LoB.
Conduct a Business Data Cloud architecture review with Domino's to map which downstream capabilities (Datasphere modeling, SAC analytics, AI data products) should sit on top of HANA Cloud to convert raw database consumption into LoB decision outcomes.
Identify candidate Data & AI workloads — store performance analytics, demand forecasting, delivery optimization — that can be migrated or extended onto HANA Cloud to absorb the $3,184 monthly unconsumed budget and reverse the attainment decline.
Validate HANA Cloud sizing, auto-scaling, and active job profiles with Domino's data engineering team to confirm whether the April–May dip reflects right-sizing efficiency or genuine workload contraction requiring use-case expansion.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$88K
YTD Budget$109K
YTD ACV: $131K
Projected Consumed: $212K · Projected Budget: $261K · Projected ACV: $314K
Solution Insights and Actions
Domino's Technology Foundation is architecturally complete for building and integrating extensions — SAP Build (low-code), Integration Suite (APIs/iFlows), Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BTP runtime), and Custom Domain (branded endpoints) are all contracted — but three of the four are in sustained decline, indicating the LoB has stabilized on a narrow set of initial extensions rather than industrializing BTP as the extension platform for digital ordering and franchisee services.
Integration Suite is the only product running near plan ($144,515 projected vs $142,596 budget) while SAP Build trails 43% short of its $113,620 ceiling — this means Domino's is moving data between systems but not building the low-code apps and workflows that would consume those integrations, leaving the integration backbone underutilized as a business capability enabler.
Custom Domain service is projected to land at 47.9% attainment with consumption shrinking as new BTP workloads fail to materialize — branded, TLS-secured endpoints for customer-facing and franchisee apps are contracted but unused, signaling that the apps Build and CPEA should be producing are not reaching production exposure.
Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement consumption has declined three consecutive months to 76.1% attainment, mirroring the Build and Custom Domain downturn — the shared BTP runtime that underpins extensions, integrations, and custom apps is contracting in lockstep, confirming the slowdown is platform-wide rather than isolated to one product.
Run a unified BTP extension roadmap review with Domino's covering SAP Build, Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, and Custom Domain together to identify the next wave of low-code apps and exposed endpoints that will reactivate the full extension stack rather than treating each product's decline in isolation.
Reposition SAP Build with Domino's developer and citizen-developer teams on concrete use cases — franchisee operations apps, store ops workflows, approval automations — to close the $49,103 gap to contract ceiling and create downstream pull on Integration Suite APIs and Custom Domain endpoints.
Audit Integration Suite iFlows to confirm whether the May dip to 92.5% reflects retired interfaces or paused projects, and anchor the next integration wave on AI-ready APIs and B2B scenarios so the $1,919 projected overage represents governed expansion tied to new Build apps.
Drive a Custom Domain activation plan targeting digital ordering, loyalty, and franchisee-facing BTP subaccounts to convert the $1,182 unused entitlement into branded production endpoints that make new extension apps customer-visible.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$347K
YTD Budget$353K
YTD ACV: $434K
Projected Consumed: $834K · Projected Budget: $852K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay architecture is fracturing at the contract layer — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is in healthy steady-state (99.96% attainment) and Ariba Sourcing still runs events, but Ariba Contracts has declined four consecutive months to 66% attainment, meaning Domino's is transacting purchases and running sourcing without the contract governance layer that converts negotiated savings into enforceable supply agreements.
Ariba Sourcing is decoupling from the procurement ecosystem it is meant to feed — a 34% consumption drop from Feb to May with $91,802 of unconsumed annual budget indicates strategic sourcing events are no longer flowing into Contracts and Buying, eroding the upstream savings pipeline even though downstream PO and invoice automation remain embedded.
The entire Concur T&E stack (Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, Preferred Success) is over-consuming budget every month since February with a combined projected overage approaching $10,000, signaling the travel and expense architecture is fully embedded in daily finance operations but contractually under-sized for actual demand.
Concur Analytics over-consumption alongside healthy Expense and ExpenseIt usage confirms the T&E insight loop is closed — spend is being captured, digitized via image recognition, and actively analyzed — giving Domino's finance a working feedback architecture that the Procurement domain currently lacks due to dormant Contracts.
Run a Contracts reactivation diagnostic with Domino's contract authoring team to restore the Sourcing-to-Contracts-to-Buying handoff and protect negotiated savings from leaking through uncontracted spend in Ariba Buying.
Rebuild the Ariba Sourcing event pipeline against the $91,802 unconsumed budget by targeting categories not yet routed through the platform, ensuring sourcing outcomes feed Contracts and downstream P2P execution.
Initiate a consolidated Concur capacity true-up covering Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, and Preferred Success to align contracted entitlements with demonstrated T&E demand rather than pushing further adoption.
Leverage the Concur stack's closed-loop adoption pattern as a template to re-enable the Procurement closed loop, replicating the enablement plays that drove Concur stickiness into Ariba Contracts authoring workflows.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
93.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$326K
YTD Budget$349K
YTD ACV: $326K
Projected Consumed: $783K · Projected Budget: $903K · Projected ACV: $783K
Solution Insights and Actions
Domino's supply chain planning stack is architecturally contracted end-to-end but behaviorally inert — both IBP and Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration show perfectly flat, ratable consumption with no usage variance, indicating the LoB has the integrated demand-to-supply-to-supplier loop on paper but is not running the S&OP-to-supplier-commit process that justifies the investment.
The Business Network collaboration layer is degrading in value as the budget curve rises from $57.5K to $63.7K while consumption holds at $55,968, producing a $23K YTD gap that signals supplier onboarding and document flow expansion have stalled — Domino's is paying for collaboration capacity it isn't using to drive resiliency or PO/ASN/invoice automation with its pizza ingredient and equipment suppliers.
IBP's flawless 100% attainment on $9,308/month masks shelfware risk on the planning platform that should be powering Domino's demand sensing and inventory optimization across its commissary and store network — without evidence of planner activity or S&OP cycle completion, the $111,692 annual spend reflects license burn rather than planning outcomes.
The architectural dependency between IBP and Business Network is unrealized — IBP is designed to push demand signals and receive supply commitments through Business Network collaboration, but the static consumption on both products indicates the forecast collaboration handshake with suppliers is not operational, leaving Domino's planning and execution disconnected.
Validate IBP adoption at Domino's by pulling planner login counts, planning area activity, and S&OP cycle completion to confirm whether the $9,308/month reflects active demand and supply planning or passive license burn.
Launch a supplier onboarding and use-case expansion wave on Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration to activate PO, ASN, and invoice automation scenarios that convert the static $55,968 baseline into growth-aligned consumption and close the $23K YTD budget gap.
Establish the IBP-to-Business Network forecast collaboration integration so demand signals generated in IBP flow to Domino's suppliers and supply commitments return into response planning, operationalizing the integrated planning-to-execution architecture.
Run a joint IBP capability review with Domino's supply chain leadership to map current usage against demand sensing, inventory optimization, and response planning modules and convert ratable consumption into measurable planning outcomes.
Eli Lilly & Company
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $7.3M
YTD Budget: $7.6M
YTD ACV: $8.4M
Projected Consumed: $17.5M
Projected Budget: $18.8M
Projected ACV: $20.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly has invested in AI experimentation (Joule Studio at 172%, Database Cloud at 136%) and procurement execution (Ariba Buying at $677K over) but the connective tissue — Integration Suite at 89% and shrinking, Process Intelligence at 17%, SAC Planning at 4%, Batch Release Hub at 2.4% — is dormant across every L1, meaning the enterprise is scaling consumption at the edges while the integration, planning, and compliance spine that turns those edges into closed-loop pharma operations is architecturally hollow.
The pattern of over-consumed advisory wrappers (Preferred Success overruns in CX +$17K, Ariba +$175K, SFSF +20%, Signavio at ceiling) paired with under-consumed core platforms (Commerce Cloud 24%, SAC Planning 4%, Batch Release Hub 2.4%, Process Intelligence 17%) shows Lilly is paying for enablement on assets that are not being adopted — success services are decoupled from platform activation outcomes across the entire estate.
Lilly's regulated-pharma compliance backbone is incomplete in two critical places simultaneously — Batch Release Hub never cut over for GxP electronic disposition and Document & Reporting Compliance is flatlined against global e-invoicing mandates — leaving a $7M+ procurement and supply chain footprint operating without the statutory and quality release layers that pharma regulators require.
