The Plan-to-Make-to-Release-to-Cash chain is architecturally fragmented — IBP plans don't flow to Batch Release Hub, Ariba Sourcing is capped while Buying overruns, Commerce Cloud runs a single channel, and Integration Suite is shrinking — meaning Lilly owns every component of an end-to-end pharma value chain but none of the integration cadence needed to operate it as one system, blocking the unified supply-to-shelf visibility the portfolio was sized to deliver.
Convene an enterprise integration architecture review across BTP Integration Suite, Joule Studio, Ariba, IBP, Batch Release Hub, and SuccessFactors to map the broken handoffs and rebuild Integration Suite as the AI-ready API fabric the rest of the estate depends on before the $241K at-risk integration capacity lapses.
Launch a portfolio-wide Preferred Success re-baselining across CX, Ariba, SFSF, and Signavio to tie all advisory entitlements to named platform activation milestones (Commerce Cloud channels, SAC Planning models, Process Intelligence connectors, Batch Release Hub site cutovers) rather than ratable accrual.
Establish a compliance activation program co-sponsored by Quality Ops, Tax, and Manufacturing IT to bring Batch Release Hub and Document & Reporting Compliance live on priority sites and jurisdictions, converting two dormant regulated-pharma capabilities into operating systems of record.
Run a coordinated commercial true-up and rebalance across the Database Cloud, SAC BI, Joule Studio, Ariba Buying, and SSO overages against the Commerce Cloud, SAC Planning, Process Intelligence, BDC Public, and Integration Suite shortfalls to right-size the enterprise envelope to the actual workload pattern rather than line-item drift.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
108.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$70K
YTD Budget$65K
YTD ACV: $14K
Projected Consumed: $168K · Projected Budget: $152K · Projected ACV: $34K
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Data & AI foundation is splitting in two opposing directions — the Database Cloud instance of Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is overburning at 136% YTD ($32.2K vs $23.8K) while the BDC Public instance has collapsed 79% since January, indicating data-tier workloads are scaling aggressively while application-tier platform services are being abandoned or migrated elsewhere.
The analytics layer is architecturally healthy but contractually undersized — SAC BI is pacing to $38.9K against a $32.4K ceiling (120%) with Q1 overruns followed by suspiciously flat 100% months, meaning Lilly has genuine BI demand feeding decision-making but governance throttling is now suppressing the very insight generation the platform was bought to enable.
The Data & AI stack shows a coherent consumption story only on the database side — Database Cloud workloads are growing in lockstep with SAC BI analytics demand, but the dormant BDC Public platform agreement suggests integration, extension, and AI/Joule workloads that should bridge raw data to analytics are not materializing, leaving Lilly with strong storage and visualization but a hollow middle layer.
Diagnose the BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement collapse with Lilly's platform owners to determine whether extension, integration, and AI workloads have migrated to the Database Cloud footprint or stalled entirely, and rebuild a use-case pipeline against the $11.8K projected shortfall.
Open a coordinated true-up on the Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement ($20.9K projected overage) and SAC BI ($6.5K projected overage) to right-size both contracts together, since the data and analytics tiers are scaling as a single workload pattern.
Run a Q1 overage root-cause review on SAC BI to confirm whether the Apr–May flat-line at exactly 100% reflects deliberate governance or suppressed business demand, and unlock Joule and planning capabilities that remain underexploited in the current consumption mix.
Map the Database Cloud consumption growth to specific Lilly data initiatives to validate whether the $6K+ monthly burn represents strategic AI/data foundation investment worth converting to committed ACV or unmanaged sprawl requiring optimization.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$196K
YTD Budget$295K
YTD ACV: $151K
Projected Consumed: $472K · Projected Budget: $708K · Projected ACV: $362K
Solution Insights and Actions
The BTP foundation is architecturally split-brained — Joule Studio workloads on the Enterprise Agreement are burning 172% of plan while Integration Suite consumption is shrinking 54% and the dedicated Integration Suite contract is flatlining at 89% attainment — meaning Eli Lilly is scaling AI agent development without proportionally scaling the API and integration fabric those agents depend on to reach SAP and non-SAP systems.
Integration Suite's architectural mandate to 'integrate AI agents, apps, and data with one solution' is being under-realized — the platform is being run as steady-state middleware with a capped iFlow footprint rather than the AI-ready API layer that Joule Studio agents will need, creating a near-term bottleneck where agent demand will outpace governed integration supply.
The declining Integration Suite consumption on the Enterprise Agreement alongside a stable but capped standalone Integration Suite tenant suggests workload fragmentation or abandonment — original use cases that justified $307K of BTP integration capacity are stalling, leaving $241K of contracted integration runway at risk while AI workloads accelerate on the same platform.
The combined BTP footprint shows a Technology Foundation that is over-consumed where AI experimentation lives and under-consumed where enterprise integration lives — without rebalancing, Lilly will hit the EA ceiling on Joule Studio before Integration Suite delivers the governed, reusable APIs needed to operationalize those AI agents at enterprise scale.
Initiate a true-up on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to lift the ceiling by approximately $42,719 and segment Joule Studio consumption from Integration Suite consumption so AI workload growth does not mask integration disengagement.
Run a joint architecture review between Lilly's AI/Joule team and the integration COE to map which Joule Studio agents require Integration Suite APIs, and convert that backlog into onboarded iFlows on the standalone Integration Suite tenant to close the $37,869 annual gap.
Launch a discovery on the declining Integration Suite consumption within the Enterprise Agreement to determine whether workloads were abandoned, migrated to the standalone tenant, or replaced by non-SAP integration tooling, and reactivate or formally retire the $241K of at-risk capacity.
Establish an API-led adoption sprint targeting B2B, event mesh, and API management use cases on Integration Suite to position the platform as the AI-consumption-optimized API layer its architecture intends, rather than a static iFlow runtime.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
48.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD Budget$96K
YTD ACV: $186K
Projected Consumed: $112K · Projected Budget: $245K · Projected ACV: $447K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Signavio stack is architecturally complete for closed-loop process transformation — Process Management captures the to-be model, Process Intelligence mines the as-is reality, and Preferred Success guides adoption — but only the modeling and advisory layers are operating at scale while the intelligence layer is barely active, leaving Lilly designing processes without evidence of how they actually run.
Process Intelligence at 17% projected attainment against a $158K budget indicates the mining capability is connected to a single narrow flow rather than the enterprise-critical pharma value chains (Order-to-Cash, Procure-to-Pay, R&D-to-Launch), which blocks the LoB from quantifying conformance, bottlenecks, and automation ROI that justify the broader Signavio investment.
Process Management's stabilizing 86% YTD attainment paired with Preferred Success hitting its ceiling three months running shows Lilly's process CoE is maturing on the design and enablement side, but without Process Intelligence telemetry feeding back into the modeled processes, the governance cadence is operating on assumptions rather than measured execution data.
Preferred Success is running with zero consumption buffer and projected to exceed ACV by ~$2,576, signaling the success services are the binding constraint on Signavio adoption — meaning any further expansion of Process Intelligence scope will hit an enablement ceiling before it hits a technical one.
Onboard Process Intelligence to Order-to-Cash and Procure-to-Pay event streams and connect the resulting conformance views back to the processes already published in Process Management to close the architectural loop between as-is mining and to-be modeling.
Run a joint value realization workshop with Lilly's process CoE to build a Process Intelligence use-case backlog (connectors, dashboards, conformance analyses) that converts the $130,933 unconsumed budget into recurring analytical workload tied to named process owners.
Re-scope Preferred Success engagement priorities around the Mar–May ceiling-hit services and reserve explicit capacity for Process Intelligence enablement, so the success layer can absorb the expansion without pushing Lilly over contracted entitlements.
Formalize a monthly process governance cadence in Process Management (review, publish, collaborate) seeded with Process Intelligence findings to sustain attainment above budget and prevent the modeling layer from drifting back toward shelfware.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
65.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$121K
YTD Budget$185K
YTD ACV: $311K
Projected Consumed: $290K · Projected Budget: $462K · Projected ACV: $747K
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Customer Experience architecture is anchored on a single product — SAP Commerce Cloud — yet it is running at roughly a quarter of contracted capacity ($12,049 of a $49,554 monthly ACV ceiling), meaning the digital commerce backbone intended to unify B2B/B2C storefronts, catalog, pricing, and order management is operating as a narrow single-channel deployment rather than the enterprise commerce platform it was sized to be.
The flat $1,456 consumption drift over five months indicates none of Commerce Cloud's differentiating capabilities — personalization, promotions, assisted service, headless APIs, or AI innovation — are being activated, leaving Eli Lilly with a transactional storefront but none of the experience, insight, or omnichannel outcomes the platform was architected to deliver.
Preferred Success is being consumed as a fixed $12,700/month subscription draw decoupled from any Commerce Cloud adoption inflection, meaning the advisory and success-planning entitlements designed to drive platform maturity are accruing on a schedule while the underlying Commerce Cloud platform they should be accelerating remains stagnant — a clear signal that success services are not wired to adoption outcomes.
With Commerce Cloud trending to ~42% attainment ($189K unrealized) and Preferred Success trending to a $17,281 overrun, the Customer Experience LoB is simultaneously over-consuming advisory capacity and under-consuming the platform that advisory is meant to expand — an inverted architecture where enablement spend outpaces the asset being enabled.
Redirect Preferred Success advisory hours toward a Commerce Cloud capability activation roadmap covering personalization, promotions, assisted service, and headless API enablement so the $12,700 monthly burn produces measurable platform adoption rather than passive accrual.
Launch a Commerce Cloud channel and catalog expansion review with Eli Lilly to identify additional B2B portals, brand storefronts, or product lines that can be onboarded to close the $37,505 monthly ACV gap and convert contracted capacity into transacting channels.
Re-baseline the Preferred Success consumption plan to tie the remaining $71,617 of entitlements to named Commerce Cloud adoption milestones (storefront launches, feature go-lives, B2B buyer onboarding) preventing the projected $17,281 overrun.
Run a Commerce Cloud feature-utilization audit to quantify which architectural capabilities are dormant and prioritize activation of AI, insight, and unified commerce features that justify the $326,566 annual platform investment.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
99.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$5.8M
YTD Budget$5.8M
YTD ACV: $6.3M
Projected Consumed: $13.8M · Projected Budget: $14.4M · Projected ACV: $15.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Finance and Spend Management architecture is procurement-heavy and planning-light — Ariba Buying & Invoicing and Ariba Sourcing are executing transactional P2P at or above contracted capacity while SAP Analytics Cloud Planning sits at 4.1% attainment, meaning the LoB is operating spend but lacks the forward-looking financial and operational planning layer that would close the loop between procurement execution and FP&A.
The Source-to-Pay process is architecturally complete but commercially misaligned — Ariba Buying & Invoicing is projected $677K over its $7.31M ceiling while Ariba Sourcing has hit a hard ACV cap and is trending $447K under budget, indicating strategic sourcing throughput is being throttled even as downstream buying accelerates, eroding the upstream savings and contract governance the sourcing investment was meant to deliver.
Statutory compliance capability is provisioned but never activated — Document & Reporting Compliance public is flatlined at $2/month against a $10.8K budget, leaving Lilly's global tax and e-invoicing mandates (a regulated pharma must-have across 90+ jurisdictions) running on country-specific workarounds rather than the centralized SAP compliance backbone.
Security and success-services entitlements are over-consuming while the analytical and compliance core sits idle — SSO is 28% over plan and Preferred Success Ariba is burning $175K above budget on a fixed schedule, signaling that Lilly is paying for access and advisory wrappers around a Finance footprint whose core planning and compliance capabilities remain dormant.
Rebalance the Finance and Spend Management commercial envelope by expanding Ariba Buying & Invoicing and SSO ceilings to match demonstrated run rates while redirecting unrealized SAP Analytics Cloud Planning and Document & Reporting Compliance value into activation programs.
Investigate the Ariba Sourcing ACV ceiling behavior to confirm whether sourcing events are being deferred or routed outside the platform, and rebuild the back-half event pipeline so strategic sourcing governs the spend that Ariba Buying & Invoicing is executing.
Launch a joint FP&A and supply chain workshop to onboard at least two new SAP Analytics Cloud Planning models — connecting procurement spend signals from Ariba into integrated financial and operational planning — to close the ~$913K unrealized planning gap.
Convene Lilly's tax and statutory reporting leads to activate Document & Reporting Compliance public on a priority jurisdiction and validate source-system connectivity, converting a dormant compliance tenant into a live e-invoicing and VAT reporting capability.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$343K
YTD Budget$349K
YTD ACV: $371K
Projected Consumed: $824K · Projected Budget: $846K · Projected ACV: $889K
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's HCM learning architecture is operating as a compliance-grade LMS stack — SF Learning and SF Validated Learning are both on healthy upward adoption curves (95–96% attainment), confirming GxP-regulated training workflows are running on-platform rather than in shadow systems, which is the core value driver for a pharma L&D estate.
The validated vs non-validated learning split is heavily weighted toward GxP content ($56K/mo vs $440/mo), meaning Eli Lilly is using SuccessFactors primarily as a regulated training system of record while general workforce development on SF Learning is materially under-scaled relative to a 30K+ employee population.
HXM Operations' steady 93.7%→95.7% attainment indicates the SuccessFactors transactional backbone (EC, payroll, org data) is stable and consolidating workforce processes on-platform, which is the prerequisite for Learning completions to flow into skills profiles and Career Development — the architectural foundation is sound.
Preferred Success SFSF is burning ratably at 118–120% of budget with no correlation to milestone activity, signaling the entitlement is accruing passively rather than being pulled against specific SuccessFactors adoption initiatives — Eli Lilly is paying for guided success but the architecture shows no evidence it is being consumed as designed.
Expand SF Learning curriculum assignments into non-GxP populations at Eli Lilly to rebalance the learning portfolio and absorb the projected $340 annual shortfall, leveraging the same activation pattern driving validated learning's upward curve.
Run a validated learning content audit to identify GxP training still delivered outside SuccessFactors and migrate it onto SF Validated Learning, closing the $43,546 ACV gap while extending the regulated system-of-record footprint.
Convert Preferred Success SFSF from passive accrual to active consumption by mapping the remaining $86,370 entitlement to specific SuccessFactors release readiness, AI feature enablement, and Learning-to-Career-Development integration workstreams.
Audit which payroll, time, and org-change transactions still route outside HXM Operations and consolidate them on-platform to close the recurring ~$45/month gap and strengthen the Employee Central data spine that Learning and Talent depend on.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
87.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$742K
YTD Budget$846K
YTD ACV: $1.1M
Projected Consumed: $1.8M · Projected Budget: $2.0M · Projected ACV: $2.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Plan-to-Make architecture is fundamentally broken at the handoff — IBP is running at a fixed $143,451/month baseline while Batch Release Hub sits at 2.4% annual attainment, meaning planning signals are being generated but the downstream quality release process that converts plans into shippable inventory is effectively dormant.
IBP is deployed as a single-process workload rather than the unified S&OP platform it is architected to be — the flat-line consumption across four of five months indicates only one planning capability (likely demand or S&OP) is active, while Response & Supply and Demand Sensing modules that would expand consumption with each new planning cycle remain unactivated.
Batch Release Hub's two consecutive months of $0 consumption against a budget that escalates to $2,953/month by December signals the GxP electronic batch release workflow never reached production cutover at Lilly's manufacturing sites, leaving the LoB exposed to continued paper-based release cycles and the compliance risk the Hub was contracted to eliminate.
The April IBP spike to $168,309 proves the platform can scale to contracted capacity, so the $100,929 YTD shortfall is an activation and adoption gap in recurring planning cadence — not a licensing or technical ceiling — meaning the gap is closeable through process operationalization rather than re-architecture.
Convene a joint Quality Operations and Manufacturing IT review to diagnose the Batch Release Hub stall, confirm site-level go-live status, and establish a batch volume ramp plan tied to Lilly's actual disposition workflows.
Run an IBP module utilization audit with Lilly's supply chain planning leads to identify which of Response & Supply, Demand Sensing, and Inventory Optimization are dormant, and scope an activation path for at least one additional planning process to break the flat-line consumption pattern.
Operationalize the planning activities that drove April's $24,858 IBP lift as a standing monthly cadence so the proven throughput becomes the new baseline rather than an outlier.
Establish a Plan-to-Release traceability review linking IBP supply plans to Batch Release Hub disposition volumes, ensuring the two products function as a connected Make & Deliver chain rather than isolated investments.
H B Fuller Company
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $449K
YTD Budget: $752K
YTD ACV: $951K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
H B Fuller has contracted a full SAP stack spanning S/4HANA Private Cloud, GROW Public Cloud, Signavio, Ariba, Concur, and BTP, yet every BTP entitlement (BDC Public, Database Cloud, CPEA 'Other', Joule Studio, S/4 Public CPEA) sits at or near zero consumption — meaning the clean-core extension and AI layer that should bind these domains is architecturally absent, forcing every cross-domain scenario (process mining feedback, contract-to-invoice automation, statutory expansion, AI agents) to either live in core or not happen at all.
The Integration Suite backbone is contracting (declining month-over-month to 64.5%) at the exact moment Ariba Contracts, Document & Reporting Compliance, Concur Expense, and Signavio Process Intelligence all show broken or stalled data flows — indicating the integration layer that should connect P2P, T&E, compliance, and process mining to S/4HANA is being decommissioned faster than new integration scenarios are onboarded, structurally undermining every end-to-end business process across the enterprise.
Signavio Process Intelligence is offline (27% attainment, May reversal) while Ariba Buying & Invoicing runs at 96% without Contracts and Concur Expense collapses against over-consuming analytics — meaning H B Fuller is executing high-volume P2P and T&E transactions with no execution-mining visibility, so process bottlenecks, leakage, and compliance failures across procurement and expense are accumulating invisibly with no governed feedback loop into transformation decisions.
Across an aggregate annual envelope exceeding ~$1.4M (Signavio ~$764K PI + PM, Preferred Success ~$253K Private + Public, BTP ~$21.4K Data & AI plus Integration/Joule/CPEA, plus Ariba/Concur/DRC), blended YTD attainment is well under 50% with success-services entitlements (Preferred Success on both ERPs) functioning as passive support rather than active enablement — meaning H B Fuller's renewal conversations will surface a portfolio where the expensive transformation and platform layers are shelfware while only transactional Ariba and Concur run hot.
Convene an enterprise architecture review that re-baselines BTP (BDC Public, Database Cloud, CPEA Other, Joule Studio, S/4 Public CPEA) as a single platform foundation and binds at least one workload from each L1 (Signavio mining feed, Ariba contracts integration, DRC country expansion, S/4 extension) to it, eliminating the current shelfware pattern.
Run a cross-domain integration workshop sequencing net-new iFlows and APIs that reconnect Signavio Process Intelligence to S/4HANA, Ariba Contracts to Buying & Invoicing, Concur Expense to its analytics layer, and DRC to additional country mandates — reversing the Integration Suite decline by tying it directly to the broken business processes in P2P, T&E, compliance, and transformation.
Re-anchor both Preferred Success entitlements (Private Cloud and Public Cloud GROW) into a unified run-state governance cadence that drives CALM, CQCs, and expert sessions against live S/4HANA operations rather than discrete projects, converting passive support spend into active adoption of the dormant BTP and Signavio layers.
Establish an executive enterprise architecture council with H B Fuller covering Transformation, Finance, Procurement, and IT to govern the full SAP portfolio as one architecture, prioritizing closure of the contract-governance gap in P2P and the mining gap in Signavio as the first two cross-domain outcomes.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
4.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$387
YTD Budget$8K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $928 · Projected Budget: $21K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
BAIP - Data & AI is architecturally provisioned on two parallel BTP entitlements — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement on BDC Public and on Database Cloud — yet the platform foundation is effectively dormant, meaning H B Fuller has contracted the substrate for data products, AI services, and analytics extensions but has no live workloads consuming it, blocking every downstream Data & AI use case the LoB intended to enable.
The Database Cloud entitlement showed a single $387 activation in May after four dormant months while BDC Public remains at absolute zero, indicating that any nascent Data & AI workload is landing on the persistence layer without a corresponding application, integration, or AI runtime tier on BDC Public — a lopsided architecture that cannot deliver end-to-end data products.
Combined YTD consumption of ~$928 against a combined annual ceiling of ~$21,400 (under 5% attainment with 7 months remaining) signals the Data & AI LoB is on track to forfeit roughly 80%+ of its platform entitlement, converting a strategic capability investment into shelfware rather than a foundation for analytics, ML, and data fabric outcomes.
Run a joint BDC Public + Database Cloud activation workshop with the Data & AI team to define a first integrated workload (e.g., a data product or AI scenario) that consumes both entitlements together rather than treating them as isolated SKUs.
Convert the May Database Cloud first-use event into a repeatable pattern by onboarding 2–3 Data & AI workloads — data pipelines, embedding stores, or SAC/Datasphere extensions — onto BDC Public to bring monthly run rate in line with the ~$950 combined monthly budget.
Establish a platform enablement plan with H B Fuller covering subaccount provisioning, entitlement assignment, and production workload migration onto LPR868 so the Data & AI LoB has a governed path from zero consumption to measurable capability adoption.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
65.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$75K
YTD Budget$115K
YTD ACV: $107K
Projected Consumed: $179K · Projected Budget: $281K · Projected ACV: $257K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally fragmented across three BTP entitlements (Joule Studio, Integration Suite, Other) all drawing on the same Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, yet only the Integration Suite slice shows meaningful workload — Joule Studio is barely activated and the 'Other' bucket sits at $0, meaning H B Fuller has contracted a broad platform but is using it as a narrow integration runtime.
The integration backbone is contracting rather than expanding — SAP Integration Suite consumption has declined every month from $13,541 to $11,652 (64.5% attainment) while its underlying CPEA capacity also dropped from a $1,782 March peak to $1,013, signaling that existing iFlows are being retired without new API, event, or AI-agent integration scenarios replacing them, which directly undermines the platform's role as the secure API layer for AI consumption.
Custom Domain service shows the classic signature of a one-time configuration entitlement (early overage Jan–Mar, tapering to $97 in May) rather than an expanding production footprint, suggesting the LoB has provisioned domains for initial BTP/SaaS endpoints but has not extended branded domain coverage as new SAP services come online.
The 'Technology Foundation Other' CPEA slice at $0 consumed against $2,273 YTD budget indicates entire categories of foundational BTP services (extensions, analytics, AI, data) remain dormant, leaving Integration Suite as an isolated capability rather than part of a composable platform — the LoB is paying for a foundation but building on only one pillar.
Run a unified BTP workload discovery across all three CPEA allocations to activate the dormant 'Other' bucket and expand Joule Studio beyond its current $1,170/month footprint, sequencing extension, analytics, and AI services that complement the existing Integration Suite investment.
Reverse the Integration Suite decline by onboarding net-new iFlows, API Management endpoints, Trading Partner Management, and prebuilt S/4HANA content tied to H B Fuller's ERP and supply chain landscape, restoring the tenant's role as the AI-ready API layer.
Validate the Custom Domain footprint against currently provisioned BTP/SaaS endpoints to confirm whether the May dip reflects decommissioning or steady-state, and align domain provisioning with the broader BTP expansion plan.
Establish a single platform-owner governance cadence at H B Fuller covering all CPEA-funded services so consumption is managed as one Technology Foundation rather than three disconnected entitlements.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
60.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$227K
YTD Budget$372K
YTD ACV: $512K
Projected Consumed: $544K · Projected Budget: $894K · Projected ACV: $1.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Signavio process transformation stack is architecturally split-brained — Process Management (design/modeling) and Process Intelligence (execution mining) are both licensed but neither is operating at scale, meaning H B Fuller has the full discover-to-improve loop contracted but is realizing neither the 'as-designed' governance nor the 'as-executed' insight that justifies the Business Transformation investment.
Process Intelligence is the load-bearing capability in this LoB at ~$764K annual envelope versus Process Management's smaller footprint, yet the May negative reversal and 27% YTD attainment indicate the mining engine that should be feeding transformation decisions is effectively offline — leaving process owners without the data signal needed to prioritize improvement work.
The 5x ACV expansion on Process Management against declining consumption signals H B Fuller has bought modeling capacity ahead of an enablement plan, so the LoB now owns the seats to scale process governance across the enterprise but is still operating in pilot mode with a handful of modelers.
Without active Process Intelligence feeding conformance and bottleneck data into Process Management models, the LoB is modeling processes in a vacuum — design decisions are being made on tribal knowledge rather than mined execution evidence, which is precisely the architectural anti-pattern Signavio is meant to eliminate.
Investigate the May -$49,543 Process Intelligence entry with billing and the process owner to confirm whether it reflects a true-up or a halted execution, and restore the steady-state mining workload before the data gap compounds.
Tie the expanded Process Management ACV to a named onboarding wave of process owners and COE modelers, with 2-3 priority end-to-end processes (order-to-cash, procure-to-pay) committed as modeling deliverables.
Establish an integrated Signavio operating cadence where Process Intelligence mining outputs (conformance, cycle time, rework) directly seed the Process Management modeling backlog, closing the discover-to-design loop the LoB is paying for.
Stand up an executive transformation review with H B Fuller's transformation lead to reframe Signavio as the single source of truth for process KPIs, anchoring both products to a measurable business outcome rather than tracking them as independent tool consumption.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$40K
YTD Budget$106K
YTD ACV: $126K
Projected Consumed: $97K · Projected Budget: $253K · Projected ACV: $304K
Solution Insights and Actions
H B Fuller's Cloud ERP Private footprint rests on a single success service entitlement, and the April consumption halving indicates the customer has architecturally decoupled Preferred Success from active S/4HANA operations — meaning the LoB is now running private cloud ERP without the advisory, CoE, and technical safeguards that were contracted to de-risk it.
With projected attainment at 38% ($97K of $253K) and YTD consumption already trending flat, the Preferred Success layer is functioning as a passive support contract rather than an active enablement engine, leaving roadmap acceleration, release adoption, and innovation uptake on S/4HANA Private Cloud unsupported.
The step-change drop in April points to a specific workstream or CoE owner disengaging rather than gradual underuse, which architecturally signals that Preferred Success entitlements were tied to a finite project rather than embedded into H B Fuller's run-state S/4HANA governance model.
Run a Preferred Success service review with H B Fuller's S/4HANA Private Cloud owners to map which advisory, CoE enablement, and technical entitlements stopped being consumed in April and re-anchor them to live workstreams.
Activate the dormant Expert Chat, Continuous Quality Checks, and Sustainment Plan entitlements against current S/4HANA release, performance, and integration priorities to close the ~$156K value gap before it undermines renewal justification.
Embed Preferred Success consumption into H B Fuller's S/4HANA Private Cloud run-state governance cadence so entitlements are driven by ongoing operations rather than discrete project demand.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
70.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$107K
YTD Budget$152K
YTD ACV: $205K
Projected Consumed: $257K · Projected Budget: $371K · Projected ACV: $492K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay architecture is fundamentally broken at H B Fuller — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running hot at 96% YTD attainment with a 6x May spike while Ariba Contracts sits dormant at $0, meaning purchases and invoices are flowing through the platform without contract governance, eliminating the compliance, savings capture, and negotiated-rate enforcement that justify the sourcing suite investment.
The Travel & Expense stack shows a structural imbalance — Concur Analytics, ExpenseIt, and Extract Services are all over-consuming (109–129%) while Concur Expense itself collapsed 62% in May, indicating reporting and receipt-capture demand persists even as the core expense submission engine that feeds them has stalled, threatening data integrity across the entire T&E reporting layer.
The statutory compliance footprint is architecturally shallow — Document & Reporting Compliance is locked at ~$315/month single-workload consumption (38% trajectory) and the companion Compliance Service sits at $0, so H B Fuller has e-document infrastructure deployed for one scenario but no expansion across the 90+ country mandates the platform was contracted to cover.
The S/4HANA Public Cloud foundation is under-enabled — Preferred Success activated two months late and is trending to 68% consumption while Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits fully dormant, meaning the GROW with SAP system of record is running without the extension platform or proactive success guidance that convert ERP deployment into continuous business value.
Convene a P2P governance review with H B Fuller procurement and legal to activate Ariba Contracts authoring and repository workflows, binding the active Ariba Buying and Invoicing transaction flow to contract-backed compliance before further invoice volume bypasses negotiated terms.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense collapse against sustained Analytics and ExpenseIt over-consumption to determine whether a policy, integration, or user-activity break is starving the expense pipeline that downstream reporting depends on.
Launch a Document & Reporting Compliance country and entity expansion workshop to onboard additional statutory reports or e-invoicing flows and concurrently activate the Compliance Service to extend case-managed compliance handling beyond the current single steady-state workload.
Run a joint Preferred Success and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation plan that maps unrealized S/4HANA success entitlements (CALM, CQCs, expert sessions) to a first BTP workload, converting dormant platform spend into measurable extension and adoption outcomes.
Hill-Rom
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $288K
YTD Budget: $378K
YTD ACV: $453K
Projected Consumed: $691K
Projected Budget: $877K
Projected ACV: $1.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's enterprise architecture shows a consistent pattern of running only the transactional 'last mile' of each domain — Commerce Cloud sustains an existing storefront while B2B/self-service sit dormant, and Ariba Buying & Invoicing pays invoices while Sourcing, Contracts, and Supplier Risk are inactive — meaning SAP is the system of execution but not the system of governance or growth, which caps the strategic ROI Hill-Rom can defend at renewal.
Across both L1 areas roughly $120K+ of contracted annual capacity ($69K Commerce Cloud + ~$50K Ariba upstream modules) is dormant while advisory and tactical consumption runs at or above ceiling, indicating Hill-Rom is over-investing in optimizing existing workloads and under-investing in activating the integrated cross-domain processes (storefront-to-order, source-to-pay) the contracts were sized for.
Neither domain shows evidence of an integration backbone connecting Commerce Cloud orders to Ariba procurement or supplier data, leaving Hill-Rom's medical-device commerce and supply governance as parallel silos — a critical architectural gap for a regulated MedTech operator whose customer trust depends on traceable supplier-to-product flows.
The Preferred Success overrun pattern in CX combined with Ariba module decline suggests advisory entitlements are being consumed reactively rather than steering an enterprise roadmap, meaning Hill-Rom lacks an architectural authority converting success hours into cross-domain capability activation.
Convene a cross-domain EA review with Hill-Rom's digital commerce and procurement leadership to map the storefront-to-supplier-to-pay value chain and identify the integration points where Commerce Cloud, Ariba Sourcing, Contracts, and Buying & Invoicing must connect.
Reposition the Preferred Success engagement model from per-domain optimization to an enterprise capability activation roadmap with outcome attribution tied to closing the ~$120K dormant-capacity gap across CX and Spend.
Commission a MedTech-specific supplier traceability and risk workshop that reactivates Ariba Supplier Risk and Contracts as the governance layer beneath both procurement and commerce, aligning the architecture to Hill-Rom's regulatory and patient-safety obligations.
Establish a single enterprise architecture scorecard that tracks contracted vs. consumed value at L1 and integration-point level so renewal conversations are framed around architectural completeness, not module-by-module attainment.

Solution Area
Customer Experience
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$215K
YTD Budget$249K
YTD ACV: $284K
Projected Consumed: $516K · Projected Budget: $566K · Projected ACV: $683K
Solution Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's Customer Experience architecture is anchored on a single channel — Commerce Cloud is running at a steady ~82% attainment with $69K of contracted capacity dormant, meaning the platform is sustaining an existing storefront workload but is not being extended into the B2B, self-service, or multi-catalog scenarios it was licensed to support.
The Commerce stack is operationally lopsided — Preferred Success is being consumed at full ceiling ($6,577/month, tracking 25% over plan) while the underlying Commerce Cloud platform is underused by 13%, indicating advisory engagement is being spent on optimizing what exists rather than activating new commerce capabilities that would unlock LoB growth.
Without an outcome attribution loop, the $78.9K projected Preferred Success draw risks becoming undifferentiated support hours rather than a lever to close the $69K Commerce Cloud activation gap — the success entitlement is architecturally positioned to drive adoption breadth but is currently reinforcing a fixed-workload pattern.
Redirect Preferred Success Commerce engagements toward a Commerce Cloud capability activation roadmap (B2B punchout, self-service portals, additional catalogs) so the overrun consumption is tied to net-new storefront or channel outcomes.
Run a joint Commerce Cloud usage audit with Hill-Rom's digital commerce team to map dormant capabilities against the $69,442 budget gap and identify which CX use cases can absorb contracted capacity.
Establish an outcome attribution log for each monthly Preferred Success engagement, linking advisory hours to evidenced Commerce Cloud adoption wins that can be defended at renewal.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
56.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$73K
YTD Budget$128K
YTD ACV: $169K
Projected Consumed: $175K · Projected Budget: $311K · Projected ACV: $405K
Solution Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's source-to-pay architecture is fundamentally broken at the governance layer — Ariba Sourcing is in active decline (78% drop Jan→May), Ariba Contracts is frozen at a single $53/month transaction, and Ariba Supplier Risk runs on a $100–$201 baseline, meaning negotiated savings have no contractual scaffolding and no risk-informed supplier decisions feeding the procurement pipeline.
Buying and Invoicing is the only operational node in the Finance and Spend stack at 80.5% attainment, but it is executing transactions without upstream sourcing events, contract terms, or risk signals — the LoB is paying invoices against suppliers and prices that bypass the strategic procurement controls the surrounding Ariba suite was purchased to enforce.
The five-month decline in Ariba Sourcing combined with flat-line Contracts and Supplier Risk consumption signals that strategic procurement has reverted to offline channels while only tactical AP automation remains in-platform, leaving roughly $50K+ of contracted Ariba value across three modules unrealized against the FY2026 plan.
February's $18,854 Buying and Invoicing spike against an otherwise flat $10.8K–$12.1K band reveals latent transactional capacity in the platform that, if paired with reactivated Sourcing and Contracts, could convert the current AP-only footprint into a closed-loop procure-to-pay process.
Reconstruct the source-to-contract-to-pay process flow with Hill-Rom procurement leadership by tying reactivated Ariba Sourcing events directly into Ariba Contracts authoring and downstream into Buying and Invoicing, restoring the integrated architecture rather than treating each module as standalone.
Commission an executive adoption recovery review for Ariba Sourcing to diagnose the five-month decline and re-commit RFx and category event volume, as Sourcing is the upstream trigger that feeds both Contracts and Supplier Risk utilization.
Launch a contract migration and supplier risk coverage workshop to move active templates and supplier tiers into Ariba Contracts and Ariba Supplier Risk respectively, converting the dormant $53 and $201 baselines into governance scope that matches Hill-Rom's invoice transaction volume.
Replicate the February consumption drivers in Ariba Buying and Invoicing — supplier onboarding, PO conversion, or invoice automation — as a category expansion play to close the $25,765 cumulative gap and broaden the active buyer base.
Ingredion Incorporated
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $441K
YTD Budget: $696K
YTD ACV: $602K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M
Projected Budget: $1.6M
Projected ACV: $1.4M
EA Insights and Actions
Ingredion has procured BTP Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements in all three L1 domains — Data & AI ($3,009), Technology Foundation ($1,938), and Finance — yet every single one sits at $0 consumption, meaning the customer has paid three times for the same architectural fabric while running SAC BI, Datasphere, SAC Planning, PaPM, and Concur as disconnected SaaS islands, blocking the AI-ready, integrated enterprise that the contract portfolio was designed to deliver.
The classic closed-loop finance architecture is broken at both ends — Ariba Buying & Invoicing has collapsed to 25.5% attainment while Spend Analysis runs at 240%, and the FP&A stack (SAC Planning at 108%, PaPM at 235%) is operating without its BTP integration layer, meaning Ingredion can see spend and model profitability but cannot close the loop from plan to commit to pay, undermining the working-capital and margin-management agenda critical to a commodity-exposed ingredients manufacturer.
Ingredion's enterprise pattern is consistent across all three L1 areas: SaaS consumption layers are over-attaining (SAC BI 111%, Datasphere 115%, Concur suite at/above plan, PaPM 235%) while every platform/integration foundation is dormant, signalling an organization that buys and uses SAP applications well but has no activated integration backbone — leaving Joule, agentic AI, and S/4HANA-connected workflows architecturally blocked despite the contracts being in place.
Total dormant BTP entitlement across domains approaches $5K annually with only 7 months of FY2026 remaining, and the parallel pattern of zero burn confirms this is a kickoff and ownership gap rather than a ramp issue — no named platform owner exists across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, or Finance, which is why three separate procurement events produced three separate non-activations.
Convene a single enterprise-level BTP activation council with one named platform owner accountable across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance, consolidating the three dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements into one architectural roadmap with named first workloads per domain (Datasphere-to-Joule, Integration Suite tenant, SAC Planning extension).
Run a cross-domain closed-loop finance architecture review that connects Ariba Spend Analysis insights to Buying & Invoicing channel recovery and links SAC Planning and PaPM to the activated BTP integration layer, restoring the plan-to-pay-to-profitability spine.
Commission an enterprise true-up and rebalancing conversation that resizes over-consuming SaaS lines (SAC BI, Datasphere, Ariba Spend Analysis, PaPM) against under-consuming platform lines, converting the current imbalance into a single Source-to-Pay plus BTP commercial alignment rather than line-by-line remediation.
Stand up an integration-first architecture workshop anchored on Integration Suite activation as the prerequisite unlocking Joule, AI agents, and S/4HANA-connected finance and supply chain workflows, framed against Ingredion's industry imperatives of margin resilience and supply chain agility.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
105.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$50K
YTD Budget$47K
YTD ACV: $46K
Projected Consumed: $119K · Projected Budget: $107K · Projected ACV: $111K
Solution Insights and Actions
Ingredion's Data & AI stack is delivering analytics consumption but missing its platform foundation — SAC BI and Datasphere are both running hot at 111-115% attainment while both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements (BDC Public and Database Cloud) sit at $0, meaning the LoB is extracting value from the semantic and visualization layers without the underlying BTP extensibility, integration, and custom data services that would let them operationalize AI-ready data into business workflows.
The Datasphere-to-SAC BI pipeline is architecturally intact and in active production — Datasphere's federated semantic layer is feeding SAC BI dashboards, evidenced by parallel overconsumption trajectories ($1,243 and $13,271 projected overages respectively), confirming Ingredion has a working unified analytics capability but one that is already constrained by contracted capacity and will throttle before year-end without intervention.
The dormant BTP entitlements ($2,318 + $691 = $3,009 annual) represent the missing connective tissue between Datasphere's trusted data layer and downstream business processes — without BTP activation, Ingredion cannot extend Joule, build custom AI agents, or integrate analytics outputs into operational systems, capping the ROI of the very SAC BI and Datasphere workloads that are outperforming.
Initiate a combined true-up and capacity expansion conversation covering both SAC BI ($13,271 projected overage) and Datasphere ($1,243 projected overage) to secure headroom for the demonstrated production demand driving the unified analytics layer.
Convene a BTP activation workshop tied directly to the Datasphere and SAC BI workloads already in production, scoping extension and integration use cases (e.g., Joule, custom data apps, AI services) that convert the $3,009 dormant Cloud Platform entitlement into the architectural bridge between trusted data and business action.
Run a Datasphere workload review with Ingredion's data team to identify which spaces, replication flows, and modeled views are driving the 112.5% monthly overage, and assess whether semantic model sprawl or replication patterns can be optimized before compounding further.
Audit the Q1 SAC BI consumption spike to identify the user groups and dashboards driving 112.5% attainment, and formalize those as reference adoption patterns to scale analytics governance across additional Ingredion business units.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$1K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $3K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the architectural foundation for Ingredion's entire BTP estate — Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and broader Technology Foundation services all depend on it — yet five consecutive months of $0 burn across all three sub-allocations confirm the platform tenant has never been activated, leaving the LoB with no integration backbone, no AI/Joule extensibility surface, and no platform services to build on.
With Joule Studio dormant, Ingredion has no path to operationalize generative AI copilots or agentic workflows on SAP data, meaning any business case tied to Joule-led productivity (finance close assistance, procurement Q&A, supply chain triage) is architecturally blocked at the foundation layer rather than at the application layer.
Integration Suite sitting at zero consumption means Ingredion is running SAP workloads without a sanctioned iPaaS layer for API management, event mesh, and cloud-to-cloud integration — either shadow integration patterns are filling the gap or downstream S/4 and LoB extensions are being deferred, both of which erode the value of every other SAP investment that assumes BTP as the integration fabric.
Across the three LPR868 allocations, roughly $1,938 of consolidated annual entitlement is fully unconsumed with only 7 months remaining, signaling this is not a ramp problem but a kickoff problem — the contract was procured without a paired technical activation plan or named first workload.
Convene a single consolidated BTP activation workshop covering Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and Technology Foundation jointly, rather than treating each as a separate dormant line, and exit with one named tenant, one named workload owner, and one first integration or extension use case.
Assign a BTP solution architect to Ingredion to stand up the Integration Suite tenant first, since it is the architectural prerequisite that unlocks downstream Joule Studio and broader platform service consumption.
Build a use-case backlog that maps each unconsumed dollar to a named LoB capability — integration flow, Joule skill, or extension app — so the contract converts from a financial commitment into an architecture roadmap.
Escalate LPR868's zero-consumption status to Ingredion's platform owner and SAP account executive jointly, framing the risk as forfeited platform capability rather than forfeited budget.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
60.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$391K
YTD Budget$647K
YTD ACV: $556K
Projected Consumed: $939K · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $1.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense suite is architecturally complete and operationally embedded — Concur Expense, Request, TripLink, ExpenseIt, Direct Travel, Reporting, and Preferred Success are all running at or above plan, meaning Ingredion has a fully functioning T&E spine where booking, capture, submission, approval, audit, and reporting reinforce each other rather than operating as point tools.
The Procure-to-Pay process is architecturally contracted but operationally fracturing — Ariba Buying and Invoicing attainment has collapsed to 25.5% while Ariba Spend Analysis runs at 240% of capacity, meaning Ingredion sees the spend but is increasingly transacting it outside the platform, eroding three-way match, compliance, and AP automation value precisely where visibility is highest.
The FP&A stack is half-deployed — SAP Analytics Cloud Planning and Profitability and Performance Management are finally ramping (108% and 235% May attainment) while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at zero, meaning the planning and profitability models are running without the BTP extensibility, integration, and data services layer they were architected to leverage for S/4HANA-connected, AI-enabled finance.
Within T&E, the audit control point is the weak link — Concur Intelligent Audit attainment regressed from 97.6% in April to 76.1% in May while Expense itself dropped to 58.2%, signalling that policy enforcement and expense submission are decoupling from the otherwise healthy booking-and-capture flow and exposing Ingredion to uncontrolled out-of-policy spend.
Launch a P2P channel recovery program on Ariba Buying and Invoicing that reroutes off-platform purchasing back through guided buying and AP automation, using Spend Analysis findings to target the categories driving the 240% analytics overrun but not the transactional flow.
Resize the Ariba Spend Analysis entitlement to reflect the demonstrated $181K run-rate and convert the conversation into a broader Source-to-Pay commercial alignment that also addresses the Buying and Invoicing underrun.
Activate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement against a finance-led use case — SAC Planning data integration or PaPM model extension — so the FP&A investments gain their intended BTP foundation rather than running as isolated SaaS islands.
Run a joint Expense and Intelligent Audit root-cause review to diagnose the May submission and audit drop, restore the April policy enforcement baseline, and protect the integrity of the otherwise healthy T&E architecture.
Kraft Heinz Foods Company
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $2.7M
YTD Budget: $3.5M
YTD ACV: $4.5M
Projected Consumed: $6.6M
Projected Budget: $8.6M
Projected ACV: $10.8M
EA Insights and Actions
Kraft Heinz has contracted a coherent intelligent-enterprise stack — BTP runtime, Integration Suite, SAC, Process Intelligence, Ariba, Business Network, and S/4HANA Finance — but every domain shows the same pattern of live transactional execution with dormant analytical and governance layers (SAC BI at $0, Database Cloud at $0, Supplier Risk at $0, Spend Analysis flatlined, Process Intelligence half-attained), meaning the LoB is paying for an insight-driven enterprise while operating a transaction-only enterprise.
Integration Suite is the connective tissue beneath Joule Studio, Ariba P2P, Business Network collaboration, and S/4HANA close cycles, yet it is simultaneously over-consuming in the EA ($11.7K overage) and decelerating on LPR499 — fragmented integration governance is the single root cause that explains why supplier collaboration runs isolated from planning, why finance close is event-driven rather than continuous, and why AI agents are scaling without an API control plane.
Joule Studio and BTP Public are both burning well above plan (433% and 1326% attainment) on experiment-sized envelopes while the sanctioned analytics and persistence layers (SAC BI $8.6K dormant, Database Cloud untouched) remain unused, indicating AI ambition is materializing as shadow workloads rather than as a governed, data-anchored agent strategy that can safely touch finance, procurement, and supply chain processes.
Against an enterprise footprint with ~$346K Ariba overrun, ~$1.19M PaPM underrun, ~$1.1M Business Network underrun, and ~$307K Process Intelligence underrun, Kraft Heinz's portfolio is structurally mis-allocated — overpaying for transactional execution while leaving the intelligence, planning, and collaboration capacity that drives CPG margin and resiliency outcomes commercially stranded.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review to map every active L1 workload onto a single integration and data backbone (Integration Suite + Database Cloud + SAC), and rebalance commercial envelopes so over-consuming transactional lines fund activation of the dormant analytics, risk, and planning layers.
Stand up an Integration Suite governance council that consolidates LPR868 and LPR499 into one integration estate and explicitly governs the API, event, and B2B flows connecting Ariba, Business Network, S/4HANA Finance, and Joule Studio so AI agents and supplier collaboration share a single sanctioned control plane.
Launch an AI-and-analytics activation program that right-sizes Joule Studio and BTP Public to demonstrated baselines, activates Database Cloud and SAC BI against a first finance or supply chain use case, and ties Process Intelligence mining outputs into that same insight surface to close the discover-to-decide loop.
Run a portfolio-level commercial true-up that addresses the Ariba Buying and Invoicing overrun alongside the PaPM, Spend Analysis, Supplier Risk, and Business Network underruns as one enterprise rebalance rather than isolated line-item conversations.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
4.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$188
YTD Budget$4K
YTD ACV: $5K
Projected Consumed: $450 · Projected Budget: $9K · Projected ACV: $12K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Kraft Heinz is architecturally bifurcated and failing on both ends — BTP Public is overconsuming at 1326% attainment while BTP Database Cloud sits at 0%, meaning the LoB is burning unplanned compute on one runtime while the contracted data persistence layer that should anchor analytics and AI workloads has never been activated.
SAP Analytics Cloud BI is contractually deployed but operationally dark with $0 consumed against a $3,058 elapsed budget, leaving Kraft Heinz with no unified analytics, planning, or Joule-driven decision surface — the BI investment exists on paper but delivers zero business intelligence to finance or supply chain users.
The Data & AI stack is architecturally incoherent for Kraft Heinz — uncontrolled BTP Public burn without a corresponding Database Cloud or SAC BI footprint indicates ad-hoc workloads are running outside the intended data-to-insight pipeline, so spend is occurring without producing the analytics outcomes the LoB contracted for.
With $187 of $305 BTP Public budget consumed in three months alongside $8,651 of dormant SAC BI budget, the LoB is over-indexed on raw platform compute and under-indexed on the analytics consumption layer that converts platform spend into business value.
Run a joint BTP consumption and architecture review with Kraft Heinz to map the Feb–Apr Public workload surge against intended Data & AI use cases and redirect uncontrolled compute toward the dormant Database Cloud and SAC BI tenants.
Launch an SAC BI activation sprint targeting one finance or supply chain dashboard to convert the $8,651 dormant analytics budget into a live decision-making capability anchored on trusted data.
Stand up a Database Cloud workload onboarding workshop to identify the first production data persistence use case and connect it to the SAC BI consumption layer so the Data & AI stack functions as an integrated pipeline.
Establish a monthly Data & AI governance cadence covering BTP Public burn, Database Cloud activation, and SAC BI offtake as a single portfolio to prevent further divergence between platform spend and analytics outcomes.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
104.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$118K
YTD Budget$112K
YTD ACV: $27K
Projected Consumed: $283K · Projected Budget: $273K · Projected ACV: $64K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally live but commercially mis-sized across every BTP envelope — Integration Suite workloads in the Enterprise Agreement track a $11,696 overage while standalone Integration Suite (LPR499) consumption is decelerating, meaning Kraft Heinz is simultaneously over-consuming integration capacity in one contract and underutilizing it in another, a sign of fragmented integration governance rather than a true demand signal.
Joule Studio has clearly gone into production on BTP (433% YTD attainment on a $130 budget with a steady ~$140/month run rate) — the AI agent foundation is being adopted faster than planned, but it was provisioned as an experiment-sized envelope, so the LoB's AI agent strategy is now operating without a commercial framework to sustain it.
The Technology Foundation Other workload on LPR868 flipped from 44–68% attainment in Q1 to 136–144% in Apr–May, indicating a net-new environment or workload went live around April without a corresponding budget adjustment — the platform is absorbing unplanned adoption that will breach the $18,892 ceiling if left ungoverned.
Integration Suite (LPR499) consumption has declined three consecutive months from $5,030 to $4,328 while the Enterprise Agreement's Integration Suite line is over-consuming — the architectural backbone for 'integrate AI agents, apps and data with one solution' is being used unevenly, which undermines the unified API governance layer that Joule Studio and downstream AI agents depend on.
Consolidate Integration Suite consumption governance across LPR868 and LPR499 into a single workload inventory so the $11,696 EA overage and the $1,135 LPR499 underrun are managed as one integration estate rather than two disconnected contracts.
Trigger a commercial right-sizing of the Joule Studio envelope on LPR868 against the demonstrated ~$140/month baseline, and tie it to a roadmap conversation on which AI agent scenarios are now in production so the budget reflects a sanctioned capability, not a shadow workload.
Run a workload attribution review on the Technology Foundation Other LPR868 line to identify what went live in April, confirm whether it is a sanctioned production workload, and rebalance the remaining $11,190 budget before two more months of 130%+ burn exhaust the annual ceiling.
Re-engage Kraft Heinz integration owners on net-new Integration Suite use cases — API management, event mesh, B2B — to reverse the LPR499 decline and ensure the integration layer keeps pace with the AI agent and BTP workloads now scaling on the same foundation.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
55.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$172K
YTD Budget$308K
YTD ACV: $425K
Projected Consumed: $413K · Projected Budget: $750K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack at Kraft Heinz is architecturally bifurcated — Process Intelligence (data-driven mining) and Process Management (model-driven documentation) are both provisioned to form a closed discover→design→monitor loop, but both are running at roughly half-attainment, meaning the LoB has the tooling to operationalize continuous process improvement yet is treating it as a project utility rather than an embedded transformation capability.
Process Intelligence is the analytical backbone of the suite and its May -$71K reversal effectively erases the mining baseline that should be feeding improvement hypotheses into Process Management — without that signal flow, the LoB loses the evidence layer that justifies which processes to model, govern, and re-engineer.
Process Management's saw-tooth usage (April 116% spike, May 55% drop) shows it is not functioning as the system of record for process documentation — process owners are modeling reactively around transformation projects rather than maintaining a living process architecture, which undermines the governance value the platform is meant to anchor.
With $307K unspent on Process Intelligence and $29K projected gap on Process Management against the annual envelope, the LoB is on track to leave nearly half of its transformation toolchain capacity unrealized — a clear signal that the architecture is contracted for enterprise-scale continuous improvement but is being consumed as point-in-time analysis.
Reconcile the May -$71,055 Process Intelligence reversal with billing and delivery to re-establish a clean consumption baseline before any forecast or renewal conversation.
Anchor a Process Intelligence adoption sprint to 2–3 high-value flows (Order-to-Cash, Procure-to-Pay) so mining findings directly feed modeling backlogs in Process Management, closing the discover-to-design loop.
Onboard a named cohort of process owners onto the Signavio Collaboration Hub with standing modeling deliverables tied to active transformation workstreams, converting episodic usage into a system-of-record cadence.
Institute a monthly process review and publishing ritual in Process Management to lock in the April-style usage peak as the steady-state rhythm and prevent project-driven drop-offs.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
87.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD Budget$2.3M
YTD ACV: $2.7M
Projected Consumed: $4.8M · Projected Budget: $5.4M · Projected ACV: $6.5M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay spine is operating with a broken governance layer — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is overconsuming toward a $346K overrun while Ariba Sourcing runs at 34.8% attainment and Ariba Supplier Risk sits at $0, meaning Kraft Heinz is executing high-volume transactional procurement without upstream strategic sourcing or supplier risk controls feeding it.
The Finance close architecture is fragmented — S/4HANA Finance Cloud shows event-driven spikes with a February blackout while Profitability and Performance Management consumption is concentrated in a single May surge, indicating Kraft Heinz has the unified ledger and profitability modeling platforms contracted but is not running them as a connected monthly close-to-margin cycle.
The compliance and identity foundation is splitting in opposite directions — Document & Reporting Compliance has ramped into steady-state statutory reporting on S/4HANA while Single Sign-on consumption is collapsing toward 12% attainment, leaving the LoB with operating compliance workloads but a deteriorating authentication layer that undermines secure access to those same finance systems.
Procurement intelligence is structurally absent — Ariba Spend Analysis has flatlined to $0 since April and Ariba Supplier Risk has never activated, so the heavy Buying and Invoicing transaction flow is running without spend visibility or risk scoring, reducing the Ariba footprint to transactional execution rather than a closed-loop source-to-pay capability.
Re-anchor the source-to-pay architecture by activating Ariba Supplier Risk and re-engaging Ariba Spend Analysis with named category analysts, so the overconsuming Ariba Buying and Invoicing flow is governed by upstream sourcing, risk, and spend visibility rather than running open-loop.
Convert Profitability and Performance Management's May spike into a recurring monthly allocations and margin-analytics cycle tied to the S/4HANA Finance close, and diagnose the February S/4HANA $0 gap to establish a predictable period-close rhythm across the finance stack.
Open a commercial true-up on Ariba Buying and Invoicing and Preferred Success Ariba to address the combined ~$493K projected overage, while redirecting the unrealized ~$1.19M on Profitability and Performance Management and ~$134K on Spend Analysis through targeted adoption sprints.
Run a Single Sign-on remediation review to identify deprovisioned integrations or rollback events, and expand SSO coverage to the finance and Ariba application set so the authentication layer matches the breadth of active LoB workloads.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
55.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$457K
YTD Budget$830K
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $1.1M · Projected Budget: $2.2M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Kraft Heinz has activated SAP Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration as the single architectural channel for buyer-supplier synchronization, but the April-May consumption swing ($75K→$143K→$125K) reveals the network is being used as a transactional tool for select events rather than as the embedded backbone for PO confirmations, ASNs, and forecast collaboration it was designed to be.
With no adjacent IBP or S/4HANA planning signals in this footprint, supplier collaboration is operating in isolation — the network's forecast collaboration capability cannot deliver supply chain resiliency outcomes because demand signals and supply commitments aren't being synchronized upstream, leaving Kraft Heinz with visibility on transactions but not on forward supply risk.
YTD attainment of 55% ($456K of $829K) and a projected $1.1M underrun against the $2.19M ceiling indicate the supplier community sized into this contract is largely transacting outside the network, meaning trading partner collaboration — the core architectural premise — is structurally incomplete for a CPG supply base of Kraft Heinz's scale.
Conduct a supplier segmentation and onboarding audit against the Business Network to identify high-volume trading partners still transacting via EDI, email, or portal workarounds, and migrate them to network-based PO collaboration, ASN, and invoicing flows.
Establish recurring forecast collaboration workflows between SAP Business Network and the demand planning function to convert episodic transaction bursts into a steady-state cadence that actually drives supply resiliency outcomes.
Define and instrument network adoption KPIs — percentage of POs confirmed on-network, ASN coverage, and invoice automation rate — so supplier collaboration is governed as an embedded process rather than a discretionary spend line.
Lancaster Colony Corporation
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $52K
YTD Budget: $85K
YTD ACV: $130K
Projected Consumed: $124K
Projected Budget: $208K
Projected ACV: $311K
EA Insights and Actions
Every foundational platform layer at Lancaster Colony — CPEA for Data & AI, BTP for Integration Suite and Joule, and CPEA for FP&A — sits at $0 consumption simultaneously, meaning the entire SAP extensibility and intelligence substrate is contracted but architecturally absent, forcing all current LoB processes onto legacy middleware and non-SAP analytics with no clean-core path forward.
Signavio Process Intelligence is the only operationally active capability in the entire portfolio yet has nothing to hand off to — Process Management governance is dormant at 20.9% and BTP Integration Suite is at zero — so mined process insights cannot be converted into governed models, automated flows, or AI-driven actions, breaking the transformation value loop at the architectural level.
Integration Suite dormancy is the single largest cross-domain blocker: without it, Data & AI workloads have no data fabric, FP&A has no S/4-to-SAC pipeline, and Signavio process improvements have no execution channel, meaning four separately contracted L1 domains are all gated on one unactivated capability.
Carrying ~$3,500 of combined unconsumed annual commitment across BAIP Data & AI ($1,880), BTP Technology Foundation ($1,966 Joule + Integration Suite), and FP&A CPEA ($186) into the final seven months signals an enterprise architecture purchased ahead of any activation plan or named platform ownership, putting the entire renewal narrative at risk regardless of individual domain merits.
Convene a single enterprise architecture activation council across Data & AI, BTP, FP&A, and Signavio sponsors to designate one accountable platform owner and sequence Integration Suite as the first cross-domain anchor capability that unblocks the other three lanes.
Define one closed-loop reference scenario — Signavio Process Intelligence finding → Process Management governed model → Integration Suite flow → S/4-to-SAC FP&A impact — to prove the contracted architecture works end-to-end before expanding workloads in any single domain.
Establish enterprise-level monthly consumption governance reconciling the ~$175/month BTP burn, ~$269/month BAIP Data & AI burn, and Signavio attainment trajectories against a single architectural roadmap rather than domain-by-domain reviews.
Run a clean-core extensibility architecture review to identify where Lancaster Colony is currently customizing in the S/4 core due to BTP dormancy and redirect those workloads onto Integration Suite and Joule Studio to convert dormant contracts into avoided technical debt.

Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BAIP Data & AI foundation for Lancaster Colony is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — both the BDC Public and Database Cloud Enterprise Agreements sit at zero consumption, meaning the LoB has no active platform substrate on which to build analytics, AI, or data workloads despite a combined $1,880 annual commitment.
Because Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the underlying capacity vehicle for downstream Data & AI services, its dual dormancy signals that no Data & AI use cases — modeling, integration, or AI-driven decisioning — are yet wired into Lancaster Colony's business processes, leaving the LoB dependent on legacy or non-SAP tooling for any current analytics outcomes.
With $0 of $718 YTD budget consumed and only 7 months remaining against a $1,880 annual ceiling, the trajectory points to near-total shelfware unless an activation event converts the contract from a financial commitment into a deployed architectural layer.
The simultaneous stall on both the BDC Public and Database Cloud entitlements indicates the gap is not workload-specific but foundational — the customer lacks an identified anchor use case and likely an assigned platform owner, which blocks any Data & AI capability from being instantiated.
Convene an executive activation review with Lancaster Colony's Data & AI sponsors to diagnose the root cause of dual zero-consumption and designate a platform owner accountable for both BDC Public and Database Cloud entitlements.
Identify and sequence 2–3 anchor Data & AI workloads — prioritizing one database-centric and one BDC-centric use case — to convert the dormant Enterprise Agreements into measurable architectural capability.
Launch a joint technical enablement and onboarding sprint to stand up the first production workload on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement and establish a consumption baseline that downstream Data & AI services can build upon.
Build a 7-month ramp plan reconciling the $137/month and $132/month required run-rates against realistic workload velocity, and escalate any structural blockers preventing platform activation.

Solution Insights and Actions
The entire Technology Foundation layer at Lancaster Colony is dormant — BTP consumption is $0 across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation Other against a combined YTD budget of $1,444, meaning the architectural substrate for extensions, integrations, and AI copilots exists on paper but delivers zero capability to any LoB downstream.
With Integration Suite at $0 consumption, Lancaster Colony has no active SAP-managed integration fabric, forcing any S/4 or line-of-business process flows to rely on legacy middleware or point-to-point connections and blocking the clean-core extensibility model the CPEA was purchased to enable.
Joule Studio sitting at $0 against a $1,966 annual plan means the generative AI and copilot extensibility roadmap is architecturally unavailable to business users, leaving Lancaster Colony unable to operationalize any of the Joule-based productivity scenarios the platform was contracted to support.
Carrying $2,373 of unconsumed BTP budget into the back half of the year across three workload categories is a structural shelfware risk that will undermine the renewal narrative unless at least one production workload lands in each capability lane.
Run a single BTP activation workshop covering Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and core platform services together, identifying one anchor workload per lane and assigning a named technical owner at Lancaster Colony for each.
Prioritize Integration Suite as the first lane to activate, since integration scenarios are prerequisites for most downstream extension and AI use cases and will unblock value capture across the other CPEA buckets.
Establish a monthly CPEA consumption review against the ~$175/month required burn rate, with workload-level attribution so dormant lanes are visible before they become renewal liabilities.
Package 2–3 Joule Studio starter scenarios tied to existing Lancaster Colony S/4 or SuccessFactors processes to convert the AI investment from contracted capacity into demonstrable business outcomes.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
62.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$52K
YTD Budget$83K
YTD ACV: $130K
Projected Consumed: $124K · Projected Budget: $202K · Projected ACV: $311K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Signavio process transformation stack is architecturally split — Process Intelligence (mining/analysis) ran at full attainment through April while Process Management (modeling/governance) sits at 20.9% YTD, meaning Lancaster Colony can see how processes actually run but has no governed to-be design layer to translate those insights into standardized, owned processes.
The May reversal on Process Intelligence (-$3,883) combined with a projected 72% annual attainment and a $45,239 shortfall signals the initial mining use case has saturated — the platform is anchored to a single process scope rather than expanded across the transformation portfolio, capping the LoB's return on the contracted analytics footprint.
Process Management ACV expanded to $31,126 yet May consumption collapsed to $529, indicating the larger entitlement was purchased ahead of an activation plan — process owners, modelers, and a collaboration hub are not yet established, so the governance layer that should operationalize Process Intelligence findings remains dormant.
Without an active Process Management discipline feeding standardized models back into Process Intelligence conformance checks, the transformation loop is broken — the LoB is generating process insights it cannot enforce, leaving improvement opportunities unconverted into measurable business outcomes.
Reconcile the May -$3,883 Process Intelligence reversal with billing and the customer admin to confirm whether it is a credit, usage correction, or rollback, and restore the steady-state consumption baseline established Jan–Apr.
Expand Process Intelligence scope beyond the initial process into a second high-value flow such as procure-to-pay or order-to-cash to lift consumption above the $13.5K monthly ceiling and close the $45,239 projected annual gap.
Stand up a Process Management activation workshop with named process owners and modelers to convert the expanded $31,126 ACV into published, governed process models tied to a priority business domain.
Link Process Management models to Process Intelligence conformance and variant analysis so mined insights drive model updates and governed changes, closing the transformation loop the two products are designed to deliver jointly.
