Solution Insights and Actions
The Financial Planning & Analysis capability contracted through CPEA is architecturally dormant — with zero consumption across five months on a $186 annual envelope, Lancaster Colony has reserved BTP capacity for FP&A extensions, integrations, or data workloads but has not activated a single use case, meaning the finance function has no platform-based augmentation to its core planning or analytics stack.
The flexible-consumption model of CPEA is being used as shelfware rather than as a capability accelerator — a non-started contract at month five signals the absence of an identified first workload (likely an S/4-to-SAC integration, a planning extension, or a finance data pipeline), which is the architectural prerequisite for CPEA to deliver any LoB value at all.
The $0 YTD against $71 cumulative budget creates a steep ~$27/month burn requirement for the remaining seven months — nearly 2x the planned monthly pace — meaning even with immediate activation, the FP&A team is unlikely to realize full-year value without parallel workloads, putting renewal credibility for the broader BTP footprint at risk.
Run a CPEA activation workshop with Lancaster Colony's finance and cloud architecture leads to identify the first FP&A-aligned BTP workload — prioritizing SAC-to-S/4 integration, planning extensions, or a finance data product — and convert the dormant contract into an active capability.
Sequence at least two production FP&A use cases onto CPEA to absorb the $186 annual envelope and establish a defensible consumption pattern ahead of renewal.
Establish a monthly CPEA consumption governance review with the finance BTP owner to track workload onboarding against the accelerated burn curve and prevent recurrence of zero-consumption months.
Land O'Lakes Inc.
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $563K
YTD Budget: $674K
YTD ACV: $836K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.6M
Projected ACV: $2.0M
EA Insights and Actions
Land O'Lakes has contracted a complete SAP stack — BTP foundation, Datasphere semantic layer, SAC analytics, Ariba/Concur/Fieldglass spend management — but the BTP and Integration Suite substrate underneath is at near-zero activation, meaning every LoB outcome above it (S2P automation, AI-ready analytics, Joule agents, clean-core extensions) is running on disconnected islands rather than the integrated enterprise architecture the ~$260K combined platform+data investment was designed to deliver.
The same integration-backbone dormancy that is starving Datasphere of S/4HANA data is also why Fieldglass sits at 1.1% while Ariba Buying overflows at 108% — without Integration Suite-mediated flows, services spend bypasses Fieldglass and lands as unstructured invoices in Ariba, demonstrating that platform inactivity is now actively distorting Procurement economics, not just blocking future AI use cases.
Across Data & AI and Finance, the architectural pattern is identical — bottom-of-stack governance layers (Datasphere, Ariba Sourcing/Spend Analysis, Fieldglass) are under-consumed while top-of-stack transaction and visualization layers (SAC, Ariba Buying/Invoicing, Concur) are over- or mis-consumed, meaning Land O'Lakes is operating an inverted architecture where execution outruns control, which is structurally incompatible with a food-and-agriculture cooperative's member-trust, traceability, and margin-discipline mandates.
Of the visible FY2026 envelope, roughly $240K in Data & AI and ~$20K in Technology Foundation entitlements are tracking to forfeiture while Concur and Ariba Buying are simultaneously breaching ceilings — the enterprise is paying twice (unused platform + overage on transactional modules) because the architectural layering that would let platform investment offload pressure from the LoB modules has never been activated.
Commission an enterprise integration architecture review that maps every active LoB flow (Ariba↔S/4, Concur↔S/4, Fieldglass↔Ariba, S/4↔Datasphere) against Integration Suite activation, and designate the first two production iFlows that simultaneously unblock Datasphere ingestion and Fieldglass services-spend routing.
Stand up a cross-domain architecture governance forum with Land O'Lakes covering BTP, Data & AI, and Spend Management with a single rule — no new dashboard, extension, or LoB integration is approved unless it lands on Datasphere semantic models and Integration Suite flows — to stop further fragmentation of the estate.
Run a consolidated commercial rebalancing covering the at-risk ~$260K platform/data underspend and the Concur + Ariba Buying overage trajectories, converting forfeiture risk into funded activation of the integration and semantic layers that the over-consuming modules structurally depend on.
Escalate to the Land O'Lakes executive sponsor a single architectural recovery narrative tying BTP/Integration Suite activation to named cooperative-critical outcomes — member analytics, grower payments traceability, contingent labor control, and clean-core readiness — rather than continuing to manage each L1 domain's gap in isolation.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
28.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$42K
YTD Budget$146K
YTD ACV: $236K
Projected Consumed: $100K · Projected Budget: $352K · Projected ACV: $567K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Land O'Lakes is architecturally assembled but functionally inert — Datasphere is provisioned as the unified semantic layer yet locked at a single low-volume workload (15% attainment), meaning the business context fabric meant to feed SAC and AI workloads is not actually unifying the data landscape.
SAC BI is consuming a semantic layer that isn't scaling — SAC consumption is declining from $5,682 to $4,891 while Datasphere sits flatlined at ~$3K/month, indicating dashboards are running on narrow or legacy data sources rather than the trusted, AI-ready Datasphere models, which explains both the SAC disengagement and the Datasphere stall.
The BTP platform commitment underpinning Data & AI is entirely dormant across both BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants (LPR868 at $0 across five months on a combined ~$9.6K ACV), meaning integration, extension, and replication services that would feed Datasphere from S/4HANA and operational systems have no runtime — the architectural plumbing for the entire Data & AI stack is missing.
Land O'Lakes has contracted a complete decision-intelligence stack (BTP → Datasphere → SAC) but is realizing only the top-of-stack visualization layer in decline, putting roughly $240K of the FY2026 Data & AI investment at risk of forfeiture and leaving planning, AI, and Joule-enabled scenarios entirely out of reach.
Activate both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements by binding the first BTP workload to a Datasphere replication flow from S/4HANA, turning the dormant platform into the ingestion path the semantic layer requires.
Run a joint Datasphere + SAC BI use-case workshop with Land O'Lakes data and analytics leads to rebuild 2–3 high-value dashboards (supply chain, finance, or member/grower analytics) on Datasphere semantic models, reversing the SAC decline and breaking the $3K Datasphere ceiling in a single motion.
Commission a consumption telemetry and tagging review across LPR868, LPR778, and LPR1064 to confirm whether zero/flat burn reflects true inactivity or unattributed BDC usage, and produce a written remediation plan tied to the ~$240K at-risk Data & AI spend.
Stand up a Data & AI architecture governance checkpoint that requires every new SAC dashboard to source from a Datasphere model, enforcing the intended layered architecture and preventing further fragmentation of the analytics estate.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
0.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$74
YTD Budget$8K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $178 · Projected Budget: $20K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and the broader BTP entitlement on LPR868 are all sitting near zero consumption, meaning Land O'Lakes has no active extensibility, integration, or AI-orchestration fabric underpinning its SAP estate.
The dormancy of Integration Suite is the most consequential gap — without an activated integration backbone, any downstream S/4, Ariba, or SuccessFactors process at Land O'Lakes is running on point-to-point or legacy middleware, blocking the clean-core, API-led architecture the BTP investment was meant to enable.
Joule Studio's $32 May uptick is the only signal of life across the foundation, but at a ~$400 annualized run rate against a $10,469 budget it represents experimentation rather than a committed AI/agent strategy, leaving Land O'Lakes without a governed path to operationalize Joule agents across LoB processes.
With all three BTP service lines showing zero or near-zero attainment on a combined ~$19.9K annual commitment and only seven months remaining, the foundation is on track for material burn-down loss and, more critically, leaves every dependent LoB roadmap (analytics, extensions, AI) without its architectural substrate.
Convene a BTP activation workshop with Land O'Lakes architecture to designate a first production workload on Integration Suite — ideally an S/4 or Ariba integration flow — to convert the dormant $6,833 entitlement into the integration backbone the rest of the estate depends on.
Convert the May Joule Studio activity into a governed pilot by scoping one LoB-anchored Joule agent use case with defined consumption targets against the $10,469 annual budget.
Establish a monthly BTP consumption governance cadence covering all three LPR868 service lines with named service owners, entitlement-level run-rate targets, and executive visibility into activation milestones.
Escalate the zero-activation status of the Technology Foundation to the Land O'Lakes executive sponsor with a consolidated recovery plan tying BTP enablement to specific downstream LoB outcomes at risk without it.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
100.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$521K
YTD Budget$520K
YTD ACV: $600K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $1.4M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay spine at Land O'Lakes is architecturally fractured — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is over-consuming at 108% (projected $82K overage) while Ariba Sourcing tracks to 58% and Spend Analysis to 41%, meaning the LoB is transacting and paying at scale without the upstream sourcing events or spend visibility that should govern what is being bought.
External services spend is operating outside the contracted control plane — Fieldglass Services Procurement is functionally dormant at 1.1% attainment, so SOW-based labor, time tracking, and milestone approvals are bypassing the platform and re-entering Ariba Buying as unstructured invoice spend, undermining contingent workforce risk and cost controls.
The Concur T&E stack shows uniformly strong adoption across Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting, and Extract Services (all 108–135% attainment) — the architecture is working as an integrated capture-audit-report loop, but the contract envelope was sized below real demand and every module is on a ceiling-breach trajectory.
The May collapse of Concur Expense to 58% attainment against an otherwise hot ExpenseIt and Intelligent Audit run rate is architecturally inconsistent — receipt capture and audit volumes do not drop while expense submission halves, indicating reports are being captured but not submitted through the platform, putting the entire downstream audit and reporting investment at risk.
Re-anchor the Procurement architecture by driving category expansion through Ariba Sourcing and Spend Analysis so that the over-consuming Buying and Invoicing layer is fed by governed sourcing events rather than ad-hoc purchasing.
Escalate Fieldglass Services Procurement activation by selecting one high-volume SOW category and routing it end-to-end through Fieldglass into Ariba and S/4HANA, establishing the missing services spend control plane.
Open a consolidated Concur commercial true-up covering Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, and Connectors/Extracts to right-size ACV against the structurally elevated T&E run rate before multiple ceilings breach simultaneously.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense submission drop by reconciling ExpenseIt capture volumes and Intelligent Audit throughput against submitted reports to confirm whether users are abandoning the submission workflow or reverting to off-platform channels.
Marathon Petroleum Company LP
Oil, gas, and energy
YTD Consumed: $767K
YTD Budget: $805K
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Projected Consumed: $1.8M
Projected Budget: $2.0M
Projected ACV: $2.4M
EA Insights and Actions
Marathon has contracted BTP capacity across three separate L1 domains — Technology Foundation ($1,832 dormant), Data & AI BDC Public (never activated), and Finance & Spend Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement ($0 consumption) — yet every single BTP entitlement is unactivated while Joule Studio runs 904% over an unrelated ceiling, meaning Marathon has paid three times for the same platform substrate and activated none of it, leaving every cross-domain integration, extension, and AI scenario architecturally homeless.
The closed-loop architecture is broken in the same pattern across three domains — Signavio (Process Management active, Process Intelligence dormant), Ariba (Buying overheated, Sourcing and Catalog starved), and Data & AI (Database Cloud at 15.8x budget, public cloud layer dormant) — revealing a systemic Marathon behavior of consuming the transactional/design tier while abandoning the measurement/governance tier, which structurally prevents the refining business from realizing savings, compliance, or insight outcomes from any of these investments.
Marathon is running business-critical workloads on commercial envelopes scoped for pilots — Database Cloud projecting $118K against a $9.2K budget and Joule Studio at $190K against $28.8K — while $34K of Preferred Success advisory and ~$250K of Process Intelligence ACV sit unconsumed, indicating a portfolio-wide procurement-to-architecture mismatch where the contracts protecting the core ERP and transformation roadmap are dormant while uncontracted AI and data workloads expand unchecked.
The S/4HANA Private Cloud core sits exposed at the center of this portfolio — Preferred Success consumption has collapsed to 38% YTD, BTP extensibility is dormant across all domains, and Integration Suite growth has stalled with $7.7K monthly headroom — meaning every customization, AI agent, and process improvement Marathon is pursuing is likely landing in the ERP core rather than on the platform, compounding technical debt directly against the next S/4HANA upgrade cycle.
Convene a single cross-domain BTP activation council that consolidates the three dormant BTP entitlements (Technology Foundation, BDC Public, Finance Cloud Platform EA) into one governed platform plan with named workloads from Data & AI, SAC Planning, and Joule Studio so the substrate underpinning all L1 domains is activated once, not contracted three times.
Commission an enterprise contract right-sizing and true-up across Database Cloud, Joule Studio, Ariba Buying & Invoicing, Process Intelligence, and Preferred Success to reconcile the pattern of pilot-scoped ceilings carrying production loads against fully-contracted services running dormant, restoring commercial visibility before further unbudgeted consumption compounds.
Run an S/4HANA Private Cloud clean-core architecture review anchoring Preferred Success advisory, Integration Suite headroom, and BTP activation to the upcoming release roadmap so extensions, AI agents, and process changes are routed onto the platform rather than the ERP core before the next upgrade window.
Establish a closed-loop governance program spanning Signavio (model-to-mine), Ariba (source-to-pay), and Data & AI (data-to-insight) that enforces activation of the measurement and governance tier in each domain as a prerequisite for further investment in the transactional tier.

Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon Petroleum's Data & AI footprint is split across two Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement instances with diametrically opposite adoption — the Database Cloud instance is running at 15.8x its YTD budget while the BDC Public instance has never been activated — indicating the LoB has consolidated all its data and AI workloads onto the database tier while the broader public cloud platform capabilities (intended for app extension, integration, and AI services) remain entirely dormant.
The Database Cloud agreement projecting ~$118K against a $9,158 annual budget is not a billing anomaly but an architectural signal that production data workloads were stood up without a sized commercial envelope, meaning Marathon is operating a business-critical data platform on a contract structure that was scoped for pilot-level usage.
The dormant BDC Public agreement leaves a structural gap in the Data & AI architecture — without the public cloud platform layer activated, the LoB cannot extend its hot database workloads with AI services, integration, or custom applications, confining the investment to raw data storage and compute rather than the intended end-to-end data-to-insight value chain.
Open a commercial true-up on the Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to right-size the ceiling against the $118K projected burn and conduct a workload review to confirm the 2098% attainment reflects high-value production use rather than unoptimized consumption.
Launch an activation kickoff for the BDC Public Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement with a named Marathon technical owner, targeting a first workload that extends the already-running Database Cloud footprint with AI or integration services.
Establish a unified Data & AI consumption governance checkpoint across both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement instances so Marathon's architecture decisions on where workloads land are visible commercially before they create another 15x overrun or another shelfware contract.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
167.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$179K
YTD Budget$107K
YTD ACV: $110K
Projected Consumed: $429K · Projected Budget: $264K · Projected ACV: $264K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation layer is architecturally fragmented at Marathon — Joule Studio is consuming at 6.6x its contracted ceiling ($190K projected vs $28.8K budget) while the general BTP entitlement sits at $0 consumption and Integration Suite plateaus post-April, meaning the platform foundation that should underpin AI agents, extensions, and integrations is being driven by a single unsanctioned workload rather than a governed multi-service adoption.
Joule Studio consumption running 904% over budget against a flat BTP enterprise agreement indicates Marathon has stood up AI agent tooling without the surrounding API governance and runtime services architecturally required to make those agents safe and useful — the LoB is generating agent activity faster than the Integration Suite foundation can expose secure, AI-ready APIs to feed them.
SAP Integration Suite is on track to fully monetize its $228K budget but the April–May softening below budget for the first time this year, coinciding with the ACV step-up to $25,775, signals integration workload growth has stalled exactly when capacity expanded — leaving $7,682 of monthly ACV headroom that should be carrying net-new AI agent and event-driven scenarios rather than legacy iFlow run-rate.
The dormant $1,832 Technology Foundation BTP entitlement against an active Joule Studio overrun reveals a procurement-architecture mismatch — Marathon contracted general-purpose BTP capacity for foundational services (extensions, data, security) but is instead spending uncontracted dollars on AI tooling, leaving core platform capabilities un-activated while AI workloads run without a governed substrate.
Convene a BTP consumption governance review with Marathon to reconcile the Joule Studio overrun against the dormant Technology Foundation entitlement, reallocating contracted capacity and right-sizing the LPR868 ceiling to reflect the actual AI-led demand profile.
Deploy BTP cost controls, consumption alerts, and service-level tagging on LPR868 to make Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation consumption separately observable before further unbudgeted AI spend compounds.
Build an Integration Suite expansion plan targeting the May ACV headroom with API management, event mesh, and AI-ready API scenarios that directly serve the Joule Studio agents already in flight, converting unused integration capacity into the governance layer the AI workload currently lacks.
Run a Technology Foundation activation workshop to identify the first production workload for the dormant $1,832 entitlement, prioritizing extension and data services tied to Marathon's existing SAP landscape so the foundation layer catches up to the AI tier already consuming above it.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
61.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$97K
YTD Budget$158K
YTD ACV: $181K
Projected Consumed: $234K · Projected Budget: $384K · Projected ACV: $434K
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally split-brained — Process Management (the modeling/design layer) is being actively used at 109% YTD attainment while Process Intelligence (the mining/measurement layer) is drifting to 60% YTD with a negative May entry, meaning the LoB is documenting target processes but not measuring how they actually run in refining operations.
The -$29,700 May reversal in Process Intelligence wiped out months of cumulative consumption and signals either a billing anomaly or a deliberate usage pullback, leaving roughly $250K of ACV unconsumed and the closed-loop transformation value proposition (model → mine → improve) effectively broken on the intelligence side.
The May ACV ceiling jump to $9,222 on Process Management against only $1,222 of actual consumption indicates a contract uplift that current modeling activity cannot absorb, exposing Marathon to a growing entitlement gap on the one Signavio capability that is genuinely working.
Together these patterns suggest Marathon is treating Signavio as a process documentation tool rather than a transformation platform — the design-time capability is landing but the runtime intelligence that would justify the larger investment and drive measurable refining process improvements is dormant.
Reconcile the -$29,700 May Process Intelligence entry with billing operations and restate the YTD baseline to clarify whether this is a credit, a data error, or a genuine usage reversal before planning recovery.
Anchor a Process Intelligence revival on a refining-relevant mining use case such as order-to-cash or procure-to-pay analysis, explicitly connected to processes already modeled in Process Management to close the architectural loop.
Expand Process Management modeling into additional Marathon process domains with net-new modelers and published artifacts to absorb the $7,999 May ACV gap created by the contract uplift.
Establish a joint consumption plan across both Signavio products so that every modeled process in Process Management has a corresponding mined process in Process Intelligence, restoring the intended design-to-insight architecture.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$23K
YTD ACV: $27K
Projected Consumed: $21K · Projected Budget: $55K · Projected ACV: $65K
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's Cloud ERP Private footprint rests on a single success-enablement service with no surrounding Private Cloud catalog signals, meaning the architectural safety net for S/4HANA Private Cloud — CIO advisory, release governance, and continuous quality validation — is the only lever available to de-risk the core ERP platform.
The April 50% consumption collapse indicates Marathon has narrowed Preferred Success to a thin set of reactive touchpoints rather than the full success architecture, leaving release planning, upgrade readiness, and continuous quality checks effectively dormant against an active S/4HANA Private Cloud workload.
At 38% YTD attainment trending to ~$20.7K of a $54.7K annual entitlement, roughly $34K of contracted advisory capacity is at risk of expiring unused — value that is architecturally tied to protecting Marathon's ERP roadmap milestones and cannot be recovered post-term.
Reengage Marathon on the dormant Preferred Success entitlements — CIO advisory, Empowerment Sessions, and Expert-Guided Implementations — and anchor each to a specific S/4HANA Private Cloud roadmap milestone to convert passive support into roadmap acceleration.
Establish a consumption recovery plan that sequences continuous quality checks and release/upgrade planning services against Marathon's upcoming S/4HANA release cycles to close the $34K entitlement gap with outcomes tied to ERP stability.
Run a Preferred Success service-catalog review with Marathon's ERP leadership to diagnose why scope contracted in April and realign the engagement model to the breadth of services contracted rather than the narrow set currently consumed.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
84.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$433K
YTD Budget$513K
YTD ACV: $688K
Projected Consumed: $1.0M · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $1.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's procure-to-pay architecture is operating with a broken upstream-to-downstream linkage — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is overconsuming at 123% YTD while Ariba Sourcing sits at 44% and Ariba Catalog at 14%, meaning transactional purchasing is scaling without the sourcing governance or catalog-driven compliance that should be feeding it, exposing the LoB to maverick spend and off-contract leakage.
The Procurement function is architecturally inverted — buyers are transacting heavily through Ariba Buying and Invoicing but bypassing the Ariba Catalog layer (declining from $5.3K to $3.4K monthly), which means negotiated pricing and preferred-supplier enforcement are eroding precisely as PO/invoice volume accelerates, undermining the savings thesis of the Ariba suite.
Financial Planning & Analysis is half-architected — SAC Planning runs a stable recurring workload at 98% budget attainment but Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is fully dormant at $0 consumption, so Marathon has planning models without the extensibility platform needed to integrate operational data, custom apps, or AI services that would unify financial and operational planning as intended.
Strategic sourcing is functionally decoupled from execution — Ariba Sourcing's flatlined $15K/month run rate against a rising plan indicates category managers are not feeding sourced agreements into the downstream Buying and Catalog layer, which explains both the catalog content decay and the uncontrolled growth in transactional buying.
Rebalance the Ariba Procurement footprint by redirecting Sourcing and Catalog enablement investment to feed the overheated Buying and Invoicing channel, prioritizing catalog content refresh and migration of offline sourcing events onto the platform to restore the closed-loop source-to-pay architecture.
Activate Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement against a defined first workload tied to SAC Planning extensibility — such as data integration, custom planning apps, or Joule-enabled analytics — to convert the dormant platform contract into a unifying layer for financial and operational planning.
Commission a contract right-sizing review across the Procurement portfolio to true up Ariba Buying and Invoicing to its demonstrated run rate while reallocating unrealized Catalog and Sourcing capacity toward supplier enablement and category migration programs.
Establish a Procurement governance council to enforce catalog-first buying and sourced-contract compliance, closing the architectural gap where transactional volume is scaling independently of upstream sourcing and catalog controls.
Medtronic, Inc.
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $2.1M
YTD Budget: $2.6M
YTD ACV: $3.2M
Projected Consumed: $4.9M
Projected Budget: $6.4M
Projected ACV: $7.7M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is the common dependency across every L1 domain — Data & AI entitlements dormant at ~1%, Integration Suite eroding to 49% with $0 consumed on new BTP integration SKUs, and Finance/Spend Management showing zero BTP consumption against $470 — meaning Medtronic has contracted for a clean-core, AI-ready extensibility fabric but is executing Commerce, Ariba, Concur, and analytics workloads through point-to-point integrations that will compound technical debt at the next S/4HANA or device-portfolio expansion event.
Medtronic's architecture is inverted across domains — advisory and AI-assist layers (Preferred Success Commerce at 100%, Joule Studio at 591–856% overrun, Concur Preferred Success overconsuming) are burning hot while the platforms they exist to accelerate (Commerce Cloud at 8.4%, Database Cloud at ~1%, Integration Suite declining, Ariba Sourcing at 55.8%) are stalled, meaning Medtronic is paying premium rates for enablement against footprints that have not scaled to absorb it.
For a regulated medical device manufacturer, the most consequential architectural gap is the broken source-to-pay-to-risk chain — Ariba Supplier Risk and Watchlist Screening sit at $0 while Buying and Invoicing run at plan and Sourcing declines, meaning PO and payment volume is flowing against unsourced agreements and unscreened third parties, directly exposing FDA/EU MDR supplier governance and sanctions compliance obligations the contract was sized to cover.
Of the combined contracted envelope across these L1s (roughly $1.05M Commerce Cloud + $239K Preferred Success Commerce + $4.7K BTP Data & AI + dormant BTP Integration/Other + active Ariba/Concur estate), the lapsing-risk concentration is in Commerce Cloud (~$907K unconsumed, ~80% at risk) and BTP entitlements (~99% unconsumed) — the two assets that together would deliver Medtronic's omnichannel device-sales and AI-extension agenda, making their joint dormancy the single largest strategic-value leak in the portfolio.
Convene a cross-domain BTP activation architecture review that pairs each dormant BTP entitlement (Data & AI, Integration Suite, Technology Foundation Other, Finance BTP) with a named workload from Commerce Cloud expansion, Ariba supplier risk integration, or Concur connector consolidation so platform consumption is driven by domain outcomes rather than service-by-service activation.
Stand up an enterprise integration coherence workshop to migrate Concur connector overages, Ariba data flows, and Commerce Cloud catalog/order integrations onto Integration Suite, reversing its three-month decline and replacing the point-to-point fabric currently absorbing every cross-domain handoff.
Launch a regulated-industry compliance architecture intervention to activate Ariba Supplier Risk and Watchlist Screening against the live supplier master and reconnect Sourcing outcomes into Buying and Contracts, closing the source-to-pay-to-risk loop before further unscreened PO volume accrues.
Rebuild the Preferred Success Commerce and Joule Studio engagements as outcome-linked plans tied to Commerce Cloud storefront launches and BTP extension use cases, converting fixed-fee advisory burn and unmanaged AI overrun into measurable platform adoption against the $907K Commerce Cloud and $4.7K Data & AI gaps.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$11
YTD Budget$3K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $26 · Projected Budget: $5K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Medtronic is architecturally contracted but operationally non-existent — both BDC Public and Database Cloud instances of the BTP Enterprise Agreement sit dormant, meaning the LoB has no active substrate for agentic AI, integration, or analytics extensions despite holding the entitlement that every downstream Data & AI use case depends on.
The single $11 May spike on the Database Cloud tenant against five months of silence indicates a technical activation event rather than genuine workload onboarding, leaving the data persistence and AI-context layer that should anchor Medtronic's analytics and ML scenarios effectively unbuilt.
With $4,753 of combined annual entitlement tracking toward ~1% attainment and the front-loaded Jan–Jun consumption window already forfeited on the BDC Public side, Medtronic is paying for an AI-ready development and data platform while executing business processes without the extension, automation, or contextual AI capabilities it was procured to deliver.
The absence of any BTP consumption signals that no SAP or non-SAP integrations, side-by-side extensions, or data products have been stood up for Data & AI — a structural gap that blocks every higher-order outcome (Joule, AI agents, SAC live data, Datasphere federation) the LoB would expect from this investment.
Convene a joint activation review with Medtronic's Data & AI technical owner across both BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants to diagnose whether the blocker is enablement, identity/landscape setup, or absence of a sponsoring use case.
Anchor activation to one production-grade Data & AI workload — a Datasphere-to-SAC integration or a Joule/AI agent extension against an existing SAP system of record — to convert dormant entitlement into a referenceable consumption pattern.
Build a prioritized BTP use-case backlog spanning integration, extension, and data scenarios sized against the $4,753 unconsumed entitlement so renewal conversations are grounded in a credible adoption trajectory rather than a 1% attainment baseline.
Establish a consumption telemetry checkpoint on the Database Cloud tenant to confirm whether the May $11 represents the start of a workload ramp or an isolated provisioning event, and escalate to a customer success intervention if no follow-through materializes.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$82K
YTD Budget$123K
YTD ACV: $159K
Projected Consumed: $197K · Projected Budget: $321K · Projected ACV: $380K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally split-brained — Integration Suite is actively eroding (three consecutive months of decline, 49% attainment) while two BTP Enterprise Agreement entitlements for Integration Suite and Technology Foundation Other sit at $0 consumed, meaning Medtronic is decommissioning legacy iFlows on the licensed platform while never activating the BTP services meant to replace them with agentic, AI-ready integration.
BTP consumption governance has broken down at the workload level — Joule Studio is burning 591%–856% of monthly budget toward a $13,642 annual overage while Integration Suite and Other BTP entitlements remain dormant, indicating AI/dev workloads are scaling unmanaged on the same contract whose integration and extension capabilities are stranded.
The low-code extension layer is project-bound rather than platform-adopted — SAP Build's volatile $30→$127→$85 pattern and projected 46% unrealized value show citizen development is episodic, leaving the architectural promise of business-user-led extensions to core SAP processes unfulfilled even as Joule Studio overruns prove appetite for AI-assisted building exists.
Medtronic's foundation is trending toward an AI-and-dev-heavy, integration-light posture — Joule Studio overconsumption alongside Integration Suite decline and dormant BTP integration entitlements means agentic capabilities are being built without the secure, governed API fabric they require to reach SAP and non-SAP systems of record.
Rebalance the BTP Enterprise Agreement by reallocating the Joule Studio overage trajectory against the $3,041 of dormant Integration Suite and Technology Foundation Other entitlements, pairing each AI use case with the integration workload it depends on.
Run a joint Integration Suite and BTP activation workshop to map the iFlows retired since January to net-new API, B2B, and event-driven replacements provisioned on the dormant LPR1153 entitlements, reversing the $51K YTD shortfall.
Institute BTP consumption governance — service-level cost alerts, quotas, and workload tagging — to contain the Joule Studio overrun and expose which Build and Integration Suite services should absorb redirected spend.
Convert the April–May SAP Build spike into a sustained citizen-developer program tied to Joule-assisted extension of existing SAP applications, closing the $219 YTD gap and anchoring low-code as a repeatable capability rather than a project artifact.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
27.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$132K
YTD Budget$475K
YTD ACV: $890K
Projected Consumed: $318K · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $2.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Medtronic's Customer Experience architecture is inverted — the Preferred Success advisory wrapper (LPR1042) is fully funded and burning at 100% of ACV while the underlying SAP Commerce Cloud platform it is meant to accelerate sits at 8.4% YTD attainment, meaning Medtronic is paying for commerce success enablement against a storefront footprint that has not progressed beyond a single workload.
The Commerce Cloud platform itself is architecturally contracted but operationally single-threaded — consumption frozen at $10,300 for two consecutive months indicates one B2B storefront or catalog was stood up and then stalled, leaving catalog management, promotions, order orchestration, and unified omnichannel experiences dormant across the rest of Medtronic's device and therapy portfolio.
The fixed-fee $19,999 monthly burn on Preferred Success Commerce is a passive financial pattern, not an adoption signal — CSM hours, expert sessions, and accelerator entitlements designed to drive storefront expansion, AI innovation activation, and TCO reduction are being recognized on the clock rather than pulled against the $907,179 of unconsumed Commerce Cloud value they exist to unlock.
With 7 months remaining and ~80% of Commerce Cloud ACV at risk of lapsing, the LoB outcome gap is concrete — Medtronic's stated goal of growing sales across every channel cannot be realized when only one channel surface is live, and the insight/transparency and AI-innovation capabilities of the platform are effectively shelfware.
Convene a joint Commerce Cloud activation review with Medtronic's digital commerce owners to diagnose the post-January plateau and identify the next 2–3 storefronts, B2B customer segments, or geographies that can be sequenced onto the existing platform footprint.
Redirect unused Preferred Success Commerce entitlements (CSM hours, expert workshops, accelerators) explicitly toward Commerce Cloud storefront expansion, catalog onboarding, and AI feature enablement so advisory consumption produces measurable platform adoption rather than passive monthly recognition.
Map the $99,995 of Preferred Success spend already consumed to concrete deliverables received and surface any lapsing entitlements, then rebuild the remaining $139,990 engagement plan around Commerce Cloud adoption milestones rather than calendar-based delivery.
Establish a Commerce Cloud consumption ramp plan tied to storefront, catalog, and channel launch milestones that lifts monthly burn above the $79,626 budget pace and converts contracted capacity into omnichannel revenue outcomes for Medtronic's device businesses.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
91.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.8M
YTD Budget$2.0M
YTD ACV: $2.1M
Projected Consumed: $4.4M · Projected Budget: $4.9M · Projected ACV: $5.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Medtronic's Finance and Spend Management architecture is procurement-heavy but governance-light — Ariba Buying, Buying & Invoicing, and Sourcing are all transacting at scale while Ariba Supplier Risk sits at $0 and Watchlist Screening has never activated, meaning purchases and payments are flowing without embedded supplier risk monitoring or sanctions/denied-party controls, exposing a regulated medical device manufacturer to compliance and third-party risk it has already paid to mitigate.
The sourcing-to-buy value chain is structurally unbalanced — Ariba Sourcing is trending to 55.8% annual attainment with declining RFx activity while downstream Ariba Buying and Buying & Invoicing run near plan, indicating Medtronic is executing POs and invoices against legacy or unsourced agreements, eroding the negotiated savings and contract governance the sourcing investment was meant to deliver.
The Concur estate shows healthy operational adoption but is hitting its ceiling — Expense, ExpenseIt, Drive, Triplink, Reporting, User Support, and Preferred Success Concur are all overconsuming against budget while Company Bill Statement remains flat at ~12% of plan, meaning travel and out-of-pocket expense capture is mature but corporate card reconciliation — the highest-volume AP integration point — is effectively dormant and forcing manual close activity.
BTP has zero consumption five months into the year against a $470 annual commitment, meaning the extensibility, integration, and AI fabric intended to connect Ariba, Concur, and S/4HANA is absent — every Concur connector overage and every Ariba data gap is being absorbed point-to-point rather than through the platform Medtronic has already contracted for.
Convene a compliance architecture review with Medtronic trade, procurement, and supplier governance leads to activate Watchlist Screening and Ariba Supplier Risk against the existing supplier master, closing the regulated-industry control gap before further PO volume flows through unscreened third parties.
Rebuild the sourcing pipeline with Medtronic's category management organization to lift Ariba Sourcing event throughput and reconnect awarded outcomes into Ariba Buying and Contracts, restoring the source-to-pay governance the downstream procurement spend depends on.
Launch a Concur Company Bill Statement card-program expansion targeting unmapped corporate card BINs, entities, and regions to bring card feed coverage in line with the broader Concur footprint and offload manual reconciliation effort from AP.
Stand up a BTP activation workshop anchored on Integration Suite to consolidate the Concur Connector and Extract Services overruns and Ariba data flows onto the contracted platform, converting the dormant BTP entitlement into the integration backbone for Finance and Spend Management.
Molson Coors Beverage Company
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $1.9M
YTD Budget: $2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $4.5M
Projected Budget: $5.3M
Projected ACV: $5.5M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP Enterprise Agreement sits at 0% consumption against ~$15,071 combined commitment (Data & AI plus Technology Foundation) while Ariba and SuccessFactors modules simultaneously underperform 25–35% below plan — Molson Coors has contracted the integration and extensibility substrate that both SaaS stacks depend on, but its dormancy means every cross-domain workflow (sourcing-to-contract, performance-to-pay, Ariba-to-S/4) is running disconnected, blocking the closed-loop automation the enterprise license was sized to deliver.
Preferred Success is over-consuming at 119% in both Ariba and SuccessFactors while the underlying functional modules underrun and BTP is inert — Molson Coors is burning premium advisory capacity to compensate for the absence of an integration and extension fabric, converting what should be transformation spend into recurring operational support and capping the ceiling on adoption maturity across Finance and HR.
The two flagship value chains — sourcing-to-contract in Ariba (Contracts at 24% vs Sourcing at 73%) and performance-to-compensation in SuccessFactors (flat Performance, declining Compensation) — are both broken at the same architectural seam: the handoff between transactional capture and downstream decisioning, indicating Molson Coors has deployed systems of record without activating the workflow integrations that turn data into financial and talent outcomes.
Spend Analysis attainment is decaying from 98% to 75% and Career & Talent Development sits at 69% with a $66K shortfall while BTP and Joule Studio are unactivated — the analytical and AI layers that should drive category strategy and skills-based workforce planning for a CPG brewer competing on margin and labor agility are absent, leaving Molson Coors with descriptive reporting instead of the agentic, context-aware decision support its license stack already pays for.
Convene a cross-domain architecture review that binds BTP Integration Suite activation to two named flagship integrations — Ariba-to-S/4HANA for procure-to-pay and SuccessFactors Performance-to-Compensation for pay-for-performance — making BTP consumption a prerequisite outcome of Finance and HCM adoption rather than a parallel track.
Reframe the Preferred Success over-burn in both Ariba and SuccessFactors as a single enterprise enablement program governed by EA, redirecting advisory hours toward closing the Contracts, Spend Analysis, Compensation, and Career Development activation gaps instead of absorbing repeat operational tickets.
Assign a single named BTP platform owner accountable for converting the $15K+ dormant entitlement into sequenced workloads across Data & AI, Integration Suite, and Joule Studio, with monthly consumption governance tied to specific Finance and HCM use cases.
Run a value-chain integration workshop focused on the two broken handoffs — Sourcing-to-Contracts and Performance-to-Compensation — to activate the workflow, calibration, and contract-award automation that turns Molson Coors' systems of record into systems of outcome.

Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BAIP Data & AI footprint at Molson Coors is in a pre-adoption state — both BTP Enterprise Agreements (Public and Database Cloud) show zero consumption against ~$4,973 combined commitment, meaning the foundational platform for agentic AI, integration, and data services is contracted but architecturally inert, blocking every downstream Data & AI use case the LoB intended to build.
Because BTP is the substrate for Integration Suite, Build, and AI capabilities, its dormancy means Molson Coors has no operational path to context-aware AI or process integration on SAP — the Data & AI investment thesis is paused at the platform layer before any business capability can be activated.
The split between BDC Public and Database Cloud commitments signals an intended dual-pattern architecture (application extension plus persistent data services), but with neither side consuming, the LoB cannot validate whether its Data & AI roadmap requires both footprints or is over-provisioned against actual demand.
Run a joint BTP activation workshop with Molson Coors platform and data owners to land a first production workload — prioritizing an Integration Suite or SAP-data extension scenario that exercises both BDC Public and Database Cloud entitlements simultaneously.
Stand up a Data & AI enablement track on Build, Integration Suite, and Joule/agentic AI services so the LoB has trained practitioners ready to consume the $4,973 commitment against named use cases rather than letting the entitlement lapse.
Establish a consumption governance cadence that maps each remaining month of budget to a specific Data & AI capability owner and use case, converting the unconsumed runway into a sequenced activation plan rather than an undifferentiated catch-up.

Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BTP Enterprise Agreement is architecturally dormant across all three workload streams (Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation), meaning Molson Coors has contracted for the full agentic AI, integration, and extensibility foundation but has activated none of it — the LoB has no platform substrate to integrate SAP and non-SAP processes, extend applications, or operationalize AI use cases.
The Integration Suite zero-consumption signal is the most architecturally consequential gap, because without it Molson Coors has no governed iPaaS layer to connect SAP core to its broader brewing, distribution, and commercial ecosystem — every downstream BTP extension and Joule scenario is blocked until integration is live.
With Joule Studio unactivated, the AI-with-context capability that would let Molson Coors ground agents in its own business data is absent, leaving the LoB unable to convert its SAP investment into the agentic productivity outcomes the Enterprise Agreement was sized to deliver.
The combined $10,098 annual commitment sitting at 0% consumption with the majority of the fiscal year remaining indicates this is a governance and ownership gap, not a demand gap — no platform owner has been assigned to convert entitlement into workload, which is the root architectural risk.
Assign a named BTP platform owner at Molson Coors accountable for subaccount provisioning, entitlement allocation, and first-workload activation across all three streams of LPR1153.
Anchor Integration Suite activation to a specific SAP-to-non-SAP integration scenario (such as S/4 to trade promotion, logistics, or manufacturing systems) so the iPaaS foundation begins carrying real process traffic.
Scope a Joule Studio pilot tied to a concrete business persona at Molson Coors (finance close, supply planner, or procurement) to convert the AI entitlement into a contextual agent use case rather than shelfware.
Establish a BTP consumption governance cadence with Molson Coors that tracks workload onboarding against the $10,098 ceiling and escalates persistent zero-consumption streams before forfeiture risk compounds.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
82.2%
YTD Budget Attainment

YTD Budget$1.3M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.6M · Projected Budget: $3.2M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay backbone is architecturally assembled but operationally throttled — Ariba Buying, Buying & Invoicing, Contracts, and Sourcing are all live yet each runs 25–35% below plan, indicating Molson Coors has stood up the full source-to-pay stack but is executing only a fraction of indirect spend through it, leaving negotiated savings and compliance leakage on the table.
Contract governance is the weakest link in the S2P chain — Ariba Contracts is tracking to ~24% of annual budget while Sourcing runs at ~73%, meaning sourcing events are being executed without flowing into a managed contract repository, which breaks the sourcing-to-savings realization loop that Contracts is architecturally designed to close.
Spend intelligence is decaying just as procurement execution needs it most — Ariba Spend Analysis attainment has fallen from 98% to 75% over three months while Buying, Contracts, and Sourcing all show under-utilization, leaving the LoB without the category insight needed to redirect maverick spend back onto the platforms they are already paying for.
The success and innovation layer is inverted — Preferred Success Ariba is over-consuming at 119% while the BTP Enterprise Agreement sits at 0% and every Ariba module under-runs, meaning Molson Coors is buying advisory capacity faster than it is operationalizing the platforms that advice is meant to accelerate, and has no extension fabric to close the gap.
Use the Preferred Success Ariba over-burn as the delivery vehicle to drive a coordinated S2P adoption program that lifts Buying, Buying & Invoicing, Contracts, and Sourcing onto a single category-expansion roadmap rather than treating each module's gap in isolation.
Anchor the dormant BTP Enterprise Agreement to a procurement-domain use case — Ariba-to-S/4HANA integration, supplier data harmonization, or invoice exception automation — converting unused BTP capacity into the connective tissue that the under-performing Ariba stack needs.
Mandate that all Ariba Sourcing awards terminate in an Ariba Contracts workspace to close the sourcing-to-contract handoff gap and convert the flat $9–10K Contracts run rate into volume tied to actual sourcing throughput.
Re-energize Ariba Spend Analysis with new analytical use cases (tail spend, supplier risk, off-catalog leakage) and feed its outputs directly into Buying catalog and Sourcing event pipelines so analytics drives execution rather than decaying in isolation.
