Solution Insights and Actions
Molson Coors' HCM foundation is architecturally sound but adoption-stalled — Employee Central, Performance & Goals, Learning, and Career Development all show flat, plateaued consumption against rising budgets, indicating the SuccessFactors suite is deployed as a system of record but not activated as a connected talent ecosystem where EC data drives goals, goals feed compensation, and skills route learning recommendations.
The talent-to-pay value chain is broken at the decision layer — Performance & Goals consumption is flat while Compensation attainment is declining month-over-month, meaning performance data is being captured but not flowing into merit, bonus, and equity planning, defeating the architectural purpose of pay-for-performance that these two modules are designed to deliver together.
Career and Talent Development is the most under-activated growth capability at 69% attainment with a projected $66K shortfall — the skills-driven career pathing, mentoring, and development planning layer that should connect Learning completions to Performance outcomes is dormant, leaving Molson Coors with compliance training but no talent mobility or skills-based workforce planning.
Preferred Success is consumption-capped at 119% while every functional module underruns — the LoB is exhausting its premium advisory entitlement trying to drive adoption that isn't materializing in the underlying products, signaling the support model is absorbing demand that should be resolved through enablement and configuration changes inside EC, Performance, and Compensation.
Re-sequence the SuccessFactors adoption program around the performance-to-compensation handoff by activating dormant calibration, goal-cascade, and compensation worksheet workflows so Performance & Goals data drives the merit and bonus cycle as architected.
Launch a Career Worksheet, Development Plan, and mentoring activation initiative tied to Learning curricula and EC position data to convert the $66K Career and Talent Development shortfall into a functioning skills and mobility layer.
Expand Preferred Success SFSF entitlement to match the demonstrated $31,969/month consumption ceiling and redirect those engagements toward closing the EC self-service, Performance adoption, and Compensation activation gaps rather than absorbing repeat operational demand.
Run an entitlement-vs-active-user reconciliation across EC, Performance & Goals, Recruiting, and Learning to identify which manager and HRBP populations are licensed but not transacting, then drive a unified self-service enablement push to lift the consistent sub-budget run rate across the suite.
Mondelez Global LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $1.9M
YTD Budget: $2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $4.5M
Projected Budget: $5.0M
Projected ACV: $5.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Mondelez has contracted a ~$3M+ SAP estate engineered as an integrated digital-core-plus-BTP fabric, but every L1 domain shows the same pattern — the consumption layer (SAC BI, SAC Planning, Process Intelligence, IBP, Integration Suite) is running hot while the foundation layer (Business Data Cloud, BTP EA, Process Management, Integrated Product Development, Joule Studio) sits near $0, meaning the enterprise is paying for outcomes it cannot architecturally deliver because the substrates those outcomes depend on are dormant.
The integration backbone is the only live platform capability — Integration Suite at 124% — yet BTP extension, data, and AI services are flatlined, which means Mondelez is scaling point-to-point connectivity into a CPG operating model (NPI, S&OP, FP&A, P2P) without the governance, extension, or agentic layers to industrialize it, setting up integration sprawl and clean-core erosion at the next S/4HANA milestone.
The CPG-critical innovation-to-plan-to-finance loop is architecturally broken at three sequential handoffs — Integrated Product Development ($0) does not feed IBP, IBP is not closed-loop with SAC Planning (finance-only scope), and neither is fed by Business Data Cloud data products — leaving Mondelez unable to stress-test NPI launches, supply scenarios, and financial targets as a single enterprise plan, which is the core value thesis of its SAP investment.
With roughly $1M+ of contracted capability (BDC $14K, BTP EA ~$67K idle, Process Management ~$518K of the Signavio stack, IPD $4.6K, SAC Planning ~$120K under-run) projected to go unconsumed against overage pressure in SAC BI and Integration Suite, Mondelez is simultaneously over-paying for visible tools and forfeiting the foundational capabilities that would make those tools strategic — a classic 'tools without platform' posture that compounds technical debt while suppressing ROI.
Convene a cross-domain Enterprise Architecture review with Mondelez's CIO, Chief Data Officer, and LoB owners to map the dormant foundation layers (BDC, BTP EA, Process Management, IPD, Joule Studio) to the overheating consumption layers and re-sequence activation so foundations precede further consumption-tier expansion.
Stand up a closed-loop NPI-to-S&OP-to-FP&A architecture workstream that binds Integrated Product Development, IBP, and SAC Planning through BDC data products, directly addressing the severed innovation-to-plan-to-finance chain that is core to Mondelez's CPG operating model.
Commission a BTP platform activation plan that converts idle BTP EA and Joule Studio headroom into governed extensions and AI agents riding on the already-hot Integration Suite, protecting clean-core posture and turning integration spend into composable business capability rather than point-to-point sprawl.
Launch a contract-and-consumption true-up across the portfolio to reconcile the Process Intelligence -$113K reversal, right-size SAC BI and Integration Suite overage trajectories, and redirect the unconsumed ~$1M of entitlement into a single funded architecture roadmap rather than fragmented domain conversations.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
96.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$276K
YTD Budget$285K
YTD ACV: $322K
Projected Consumed: $663K · Projected Budget: $637K · Projected ACV: $774K
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Data & AI stack is architecturally inverted — the analytics consumption layer (SAC BI) is running hot toward overage while the underlying Business Data Cloud foundation sits at $0, meaning the LoB is visualizing data without the mission-critical data products and business data fabric that BDC was contracted to provide.
The agentic AI and unified data product strategy is contractually staged but operationally dormant — BDC's untouched $14,288 budget means Datasphere-anchored data products and Joule-driven analytics have no substrate, leaving SAC BI to operate as a standalone BI tool rather than the AI-infused decision layer the architecture intends.
SAC BI's Apr–May plateau at ~$44K mirroring the ACV step-down signals adoption has hit the license ceiling rather than the capability ceiling — without BDC data products feeding new domains (finance planning, supply chain), the LoB cannot unlock the unified analytics-and-planning capability it is paying for.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement front-loaded burn followed by a two-month flatline indicates the database/platform foundation was used for a one-off project rather than as a persistent runtime for BDC and SAC workloads, severing the compute layer from the analytics and data fabric layers above it.
Convene a Business Data Cloud activation workshop with Mondelez Data & AI owners to scope initial data products (finance, supply chain) that bridge BDC into existing SAC BI dashboards and convert the dormant $14,288 into a phased consumption ramp.
Run a SAC BI overage and value-expansion review to right-size the projected $41,880 overshoot while redirecting growth toward BDC-fed planning and Joule use cases rather than additional standalone reporting.
Re-engage Mondelez technical owners on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to diagnose the April–May collapse and align the remaining budget to workloads that underpin BDC and SAC, restoring the platform's role as the persistent compute layer for Data & AI.
Establish a Data & AI architecture roadmap that sequences BDC data product go-lives ahead of SAC BI expansion and platform workload onboarding, so consumption across all three contracts reinforces a single business data fabric rather than operating as disconnected line items.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
108.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$201K
YTD Budget$185K
YTD ACV: $224K
Projected Consumed: $481K · Projected Budget: $403K · Projected ACV: $536K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is the only load-bearing capability in Mondelez's Technology Foundation — SAP Integration Suite is running 24% hot while the broader BTP Enterprise Agreement has flatlined, meaning the LoB is consuming iPaaS connectivity but has abandoned the extension, data, and AI services that turn integration into composable business capability.
Joule Studio adoption is effectively dormant under the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (cumulative $19.6K vs. $87K projected, May at -$467), signaling that the AI agent authoring capability contracted to ride on top of Integration Suite's AI-ready APIs is not being built — the LoB has the pipes but no agents consuming them.
The 37% projected overrun on Integration Suite against a 46% under-run on the BTP EA reveals an architectural imbalance where Mondelez is scaling point-to-point integration without the surrounding platform services (extension, governance, AI) needed to avoid integration sprawl and runtime cost inflation.
Re-baseline the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by scoping Joule Studio agent use cases that consume the existing Integration Suite APIs, converting the $67K of idle BTP headroom into AI-driven LoB workflows rather than forfeited value.
Open a true-up conversation on SAP Integration Suite to align contracted ACV with the $434K projected run-rate, and pair it with an iFlow consolidation review to validate whether the 124% attainment reflects genuine business demand or unoptimized message patterns.
Commission a Technology Foundation architecture review to map which BTP services (integration, extension, data, AI) are active versus dormant, ensuring the platform investment delivers a composable foundation rather than a single-purpose integration tool.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
20.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$60K
YTD Budget$291K
YTD ACV: $250K
Projected Consumed: $144K · Projected Budget: $662K · Projected ACV: $601K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack at Mondelez is architecturally incomplete — Process Management (the modeling and governance layer where target processes are designed and owned) is effectively dormant at 8.2% YTD attainment while Process Intelligence (the mining and measurement layer) carries the spend, meaning the LoB is measuring processes it has never formally modeled or governed, which undermines any transformation roadmap.
Process Intelligence shows a structurally suspicious consumption pattern — three months of identical $56,732 clips followed by a $0 month and a -$113,176 reversal — indicating a billing or contractual event rather than usage decline, and until reconciled the 23.4% projected attainment cannot be read as a true adoption signal.
Combined entitlement of ~$662K with only ~$144K projected consumption means roughly $518K of contracted transformation capability will go unused this year, leaving Mondelez paying for a closed-loop process improvement capability (model → mine → improve) while operating only fragments of it.
The Jan–Feb trickle of Process Management activity followed by three consecutive zero months is the signature of an unscoped pilot with no named process owners, which blocks the LoB from converting Process Intelligence findings into governed process changes.
Reconcile the May -$113,176 Process Intelligence entry with billing and contracts to confirm whether it is a credit, true-up, or data error before framing any adoption narrative with Mondelez.
Re-anchor the Signavio investment around 2–3 named end-to-end processes (O2C, P2P, R2R) with assigned process owners modeling in Process Management and corresponding mining scopes activated in Process Intelligence, so the two products operate as a single closed loop.
Run a joint Process Management and Process Intelligence enablement workshop to expand the active modeler base and bind mined insights to modeled target states, directly addressing the dormant governance layer.
Build a quarterly process-scope expansion plan for Process Intelligence to onboard additional in-scope value streams and convert the ~$448K unconsumed entitlement into measurable transformation outcomes.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$499K
YTD Budget$578K
YTD ACV: $661K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.3M · Projected ACV: $1.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez has contracted SAP Analytics Cloud Planning as its unified financial and operational planning backbone, but the flat $97K–$102K monthly consumption pattern indicates the platform is functioning as a single-purpose finance plan engine rather than the cross-domain planning fabric it was architected to be — workforce, capex, and driver-based scenarios that would unify FP&A with operational planning remain dormant.
The persistent 12–16% attainment shortfall and projected $120K annual under-consumption reveal a capability-utilization gap, not a licensing gap: the same users are re-running the same plans, meaning the AI-assisted decisioning, Joule-driven analytics, and scenario modeling capabilities that justify the SAC Planning investment are architecturally available but operationally untouched.
With planning narrowly scoped to finance, the LoB lacks the forward-looking integration between financial targets and operational drivers that SAC Planning is designed to deliver, leaving Mondelez's FP&A function visibility-rich but scenario-poor and unable to stress-test enterprise plans against workforce or capex shifts.
Launch a planning model expansion initiative for SAP Analytics Cloud Planning targeting workforce, capex, and driver-based forecasting to activate the dormant unified planning capability and close the recurring monthly attainment gap.
Conduct a persona and entitlement audit against active SAC Planning users and story consumption to determine whether the $14K–$17K monthly gap reflects under-licensed planning personas or under-utilized seats, then drive targeted enablement on the weaker side.
Establish a Joule and predictive planning enablement track for the FP&A team to move SAC Planning usage from static finance plans into AI-assisted scenario and driver-based modeling.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$821K
YTD Budget$821K
YTD ACV: $821K
Projected Consumed: $2.0M · Projected Budget: $2.0M · Projected ACV: $2.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Supply Chain Management architecture is operating with a severed innovation-to-planning loop — IBP is provisioned to run S&OP and demand-supply planning while Integrated Product Development sits at $0 consumed, meaning new product introductions are not feeding formulation, cost, and feasibility signals into the planning engine that depends on them.
IBP's perfectly flat $164,268/month consumption against an identical budget for five straight months reflects entitlement-based billing rather than usage telemetry, leaving it unverified whether planners are actually executing demand sensing, inventory optimization, and supply response scenarios — the core capabilities the LoB is paying for.
The Nov-Dec IBP step-up to $185,097 and $194,559 signals a contracted capacity expansion that current run-rate adoption does not support, putting $51,121 of projected value and the underlying planning scope expansion (new geographies, BUs, or use cases) at risk of going live without readiness.
With Integrated Product Development dormant, Mondelez's R&D-to-supply-chain bridge is architecturally contracted but operationally absent — recipe, specification, and cost-target alignment between PLM, S/4HANA manufacturing, and procurement is not happening, forcing NPI decisions to occur outside the integrated cost and feasibility framework SAP was meant to provide.
Launch an Integrated Product Development activation with Mondelez R&D leadership to onboard formulation, specification, and recipe workflows and recover the full $4,601 budget before the December consumption window closes.
Instrument IBP usage telemetry — active planner counts, scenario runs, forecast cycles, and S&OP meeting cadence — to confirm the monthly burn reflects genuine planning adoption rather than passive subscription consumption.
Build a Nov-Dec IBP scale-up enablement plan that names the specific business units, geographies, or planning use cases driving the capacity uplift and aligns onboarding to consume the additional $379,656 in budgeted capacity.
Establish an architectural linkage review between Integrated Product Development and IBP so that NPI outputs (BOMs, costs, launch dates) flow into demand and supply planning, closing the innovation-to-plan gap before the back-half capacity expansion.
Primary Products Ingredients Americas
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $54K
YTD Budget: $69K
YTD ACV: $85K
Projected Consumed: $131K
Projected Budget: $168K
Projected ACV: $204K
EA Insights and Actions
With only Finance & Spend Management visibility available for Primary Products Ingredients Americas, the enterprise architecture picture is itself incomplete — there is no observable integration backbone (Integration Suite, BTP, Datasphere) tying T&E into core ERP finance, which means the Concur Expense collapse cannot be triaged as a localized issue versus a broader posting/integration failure into the financial core.
The simultaneous pattern of healthy peripheral modules (Direct Travel, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services) running hot against a collapsed Concur Expense core and a dormant Document & Reporting Compliance layer signals a classic 'edges without a center' architecture — the customer is funding capture, audit, travel, and advisory capacity that cannot land cleanly into a compliant, reconciled financial close, undermining the Americas Ingredients objective of audit-ready, statutory-compliant spend operations.
Document & Reporting Compliance flatlined at ~5% attainment alongside an unreconciled Travel-to-Expense flow exposes the customer to a compounding compliance and working-capital risk in a regulated ingredients business where e-invoicing, VAT, and traceability mandates across LATAM and North America are tightening — the contracted compliance capability exists on paper but provides no architectural protection today.
Preferred Success entitlement is being absorbed across already-healthy modules rather than directed at the failing Expense core and dormant Compliance layer, meaning the customer's advisory contract value is architecturally misallocated and not converting into measurable cross-domain stabilization.
Commission a cross-domain architecture review covering Concur Expense-to-S/4 finance posting, Direct Travel-to-Expense reconciliation, and Document & Reporting Compliance integration points to determine whether the Expense collapse is a transactional, integration, or adoption failure.
Run a Document & Reporting Compliance activation workshop scoped to specific Americas Ingredients country e-invoicing and VAT obligations, with explicit wiring into Concur Invoice and Expense as the compliance system of record.
Redirect Preferred Success Concur hours away from healthy peripheral modules and concentrate them on Expense submission reactivation, Company Bill Statement cadence recovery, and Compliance go-live readiness.
Re-baseline Direct Travel ACV headroom and sequence it behind Expense reactivation so booking growth does not further widen the reconciliation debt against a shrinking expense transaction base.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
78.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$54K
YTD Budget$69K
YTD ACV: $85K
Projected Consumed: $131K · Projected Budget: $168K · Projected ACV: $204K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense architecture is operationally fragmented — ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, and User Support Desk are all accelerating past budget while Concur Expense itself has collapsed to negative consumption, meaning the supporting capture, audit, and reporting layers are running on a core expense engine that has effectively stopped processing reports.
Statutory compliance is contracted but architecturally dormant — Document & Reporting Compliance is flatlined at ~5% attainment, leaving Primary Products Ingredients Americas exposed on e-invoicing and VAT mandates while the upstream Concur Expense and Invoice flows have no live tax-authority integration to centralize against.
Travel demand is outpacing the expense settlement backbone — Direct Travel is running at 113% attainment with bookings accelerating, but the downstream Concur Expense reconciliation path has stalled, creating a growing reconciliation debt between booked trips and reimbursed expenses.
Preferred Success and Intelligent Audit momentum confirms a maturing T&E governance layer, but without restoring core Expense submission cadence the LoB is paying for advisory and audit intelligence on a shrinking transaction base, eroding the ROI of the broader Concur footprint.
Diagnose the Concur Expense ACV reversal and reactivate expense report submission cadence as the prerequisite to stabilizing Company Bill Statement, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services consumption downstream.
Launch a Document & Reporting Compliance activation workshop tied to specific country e-invoicing and VAT scenarios for the Americas Ingredients entities, and connect it to the Concur Invoice/Expense compliance integration points to break the $40/month plateau.
Re-baseline Concur Direct Travel ACV headroom against the trailing overrun trend and align it with Expense reactivation so booking growth flows cleanly into reimbursement and audit.
Convert Preferred Success Concur entitlement into targeted CoE engagements focused on Expense reactivation and Company Bill Statement cadence recovery, rather than spreading advisory hours across already-healthy modules.
S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $600K
YTD Budget: $735K
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.4M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is contractually present but architecturally absent across every L1 domain at SCJ — Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance, and Supply Chain all show zero-to-flat BTP consumption while their LoB products run hot, meaning the entire SAP estate is operating without the extension, integration, and AI fabric it was designed to share, forcing each domain to absorb customization inside its own core and creating compounding technical debt ahead of any S/4HANA evolution.
Integration Suite's three-month decline is the single fault line connecting every domain failure — BDC's $112K over-burn (no offload path), Concur Expense's collapse to 21% (broken booking-to-reimbursement flow), Business Network's May drop (trading partner connectivity contracting), and IPD's dormancy (no design-to-supply handover) all trace back to a regressing integration backbone, blocking SCJ's CPG-critical closed-loop processes from finance close to innovation-to-shelf.
SCJ's architecture is execution-heavy and innovation-light — Business Network, Concur capture, and Blackline reconciliation are live in narrow lanes while the upstream design and AI layers (Integrated Product Development at 5.7%, Joule Studio at 4.3%, Database Cloud at 0%) are dormant, meaning a CPG company whose competitive position depends on rapid product introduction and AI-driven consumer insight is architecturally configured to run yesterday's transactions rather than tomorrow's portfolio.
At enterprise level SCJ is on track to forfeit ~$284K of Supply Chain value, ~$35K of BTP Integration Suite ACV, ~$2.9K of Technology Foundation, and material Concur and BDC envelope misalignment (BDC alone projecting $112K against $71K) — the pattern is not isolated under-consumption but a systemic mismatch between contracted architectural ambition and activated capability, signaling the FY2026 commercial envelope needs structural rebalancing rather than line-item true-up.
Convene an enterprise-wide BTP activation review across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance, and Supply Chain to commit one named production workload per domain — BDC offload to Database Cloud, Concur-to-S/4 integration, Blackline GL enrichment, and IPD-to-Business Network handover — converting dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement entitlements into the shared fabric every LoB depends on.
Run a cross-domain Integration Suite recovery workshop that reframes the contracting iflow footprint into API management, event mesh, and AI-ready APIs anchored to the specific broken flows in Concur Expense, Business Network, and BDC, reversing the three-month decline and unblocking Joule Studio adoption on top.
Stand up an innovation-architecture intervention with R&D, Supply Chain, and Data & AI leadership to reactivate Integrated Product Development and link it through Business Network and BDC, restoring the design-to-supply-to-insight digital thread that SCJ's CPG portfolio strategy requires.
Initiate an enterprise contract restructure that true-forwards BDC toward its $112K run rate, redirects unconsumed BTP, Integration Suite, and Preferred Success ACV into the activation workloads above, and rebuilds the FY2026 consumption ramp so foundational capacity is drawn down progressively rather than concentrated in Q4.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
189.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD Budget$25K
YTD ACV: $154K
Projected Consumed: $112K · Projected Budget: $73K · Projected ACV: $369K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI architecture for S.C. Johnson is lopsided — SAP Business Data Cloud is absorbing the full weight of mission-critical data, analytics, and AI workloads at 276% of plan while the underlying BTP foundation (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement for BDC Public and Database Cloud) sits at zero, meaning the LoB is running advanced data products and agentic AI scenarios without the sanctioned platform services and database substrate intended to extend them.
BDC's $112K projected burn against a $71K envelope signals that SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, and BDC data products have become entrenched in operational use, but without activated BTP capacity the LoB has no architectural runway to offload custom extensions, integrations, or persistence — forcing all growth back into the already over-consumed BDC contract.
The dormant Database Cloud entitlement is the most consequential gap: BDC's business data fabric is designed to anchor on managed database services for governed persistence and AI-ready data, and its absence means S.C. Johnson is likely running unoptimized pipelines or duplicating storage inside BDC compute — a plausible driver of the May spike to $15,246.
Convene a joint workload review across SAP Business Data Cloud and the dormant BTP entitlements to determine which BDC pipelines, replication flows, or AI workloads should be re-architected onto Database Cloud and BTP Public services to relieve BDC over-burn and activate stranded capacity.
Initiate a contract restructure for S.C. Johnson's Data & AI portfolio that true-forwards the BDC commitment toward the $112K projected run rate while repurposing the unconsumed $1,849 of Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement budget into BTP services that directly extend BDC use cases.
Engage S.C. Johnson's technical sponsor to diagnose whether the zero BTP consumption reflects entitlement provisioning gaps, subaccount setup, or absence of architectural guidance — and pair the resolution with a named first workload anchored to a current BDC scenario.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
70.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$19K
YTD Budget$26K
YTD ACV: $21K
Projected Consumed: $45K · Projected Budget: $84K · Projected ACV: $51K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally stalled at the platform layer — three of four BTP entitlements (Joule Studio at 4.3%, Technology Foundation Other at 0%, and Integration Suite trending down 58%) show pre-adoption or contracting usage, meaning S.C. Johnson holds the contractual right to AI, extension, and integration capabilities but is not operationalizing the foundation that every downstream LoB process depends on.
The integration backbone is regressing precisely when it should be scaling — SAP Integration Suite consumption has fallen for three straight months and the BTP Integration Suite allocation is flatlined in a fixed $3,164–$3,550 band, indicating S.C. Johnson is running a static set of legacy iflows rather than expanding into API management, event mesh, or AI-ready APIs, which blocks the catalog's intended outcome of agent- and AI-consumable integrations.
Joule Studio is effectively unactivated with a single $77 January workload followed by near-zero usage, meaning the AI agent design capability that should sit on top of Integration Suite's secure APIs has no runway — without Joule Studio adoption, the integration investment cannot deliver its architectural promise of AI-driven productivity.
The FY2026 budget shape concentrates $35,515 of BTP consumption and a $112 December spike into the back half of the year, signaling the customer is deferring activation rather than ramping — this back-loaded posture compresses all adoption, integration, and AI enablement risk into Q4 and makes the foundation unlikely to support any LoB go-live on schedule.
Convene a unified BTP activation review across all three Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement subaccounts (Joule Studio, Integration Suite allocation, Technology Foundation Other) to diagnose the common blocker behind zero-to-flat consumption and commit to a first production workload per subaccount.
Run an Integration Suite use-case workshop focused on API management, event mesh, and B2B/EDI scenarios to reverse the March-to-May decline and convert the $2,918 unconsumed ACV into AI-ready APIs that unlock Joule Studio adoption.
Pair S.C. Johnson with an SAP solution architect to design at least one Joule Studio agent that consumes Integration Suite APIs, creating the architectural link between the dormant AI layer and the contracting integration layer.
Rebuild the FY2026 BTP consumption ramp so the $505 Technology Foundation Other commitment and the $35,515 Integration Suite shortfall are drawn down progressively against defined workloads rather than concentrated in the back half of the fiscal year.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$300K
YTD Budget$333K
YTD ACV: $427K
Projected Consumed: $721K · Projected Budget: $810K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense stack at S.C. Johnson is architecturally complete — booking (Direct Travel, TripLink), capture (ExpenseIt), processing (Expense), analytics (Analytics, Reporting Services), and success enablement (Preferred Success) are all live — but the May collapse of Concur Expense to 21% attainment breaks the spine of the T&E process, since bookings and receipts continue to flow in while the expense reporting engine that turns them into reimbursable, compliant transactions has stalled.
The Finance close architecture is operating in a narrow lane — Blackline is reconciling core accounts steadily at 92% but not expanding to additional ledger groups, meaning S.C. Johnson is using it as a substitution tool for a fixed account scope rather than as the enterprise close-acceleration platform it is contracted to be, leaving ~$47K of automation capacity unconverted into faster close cycles.
BTP is contractually present but architecturally absent — zero consumption against the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement means there is no extension, integration, or custom analytics fabric underneath the Finance and T&E estate, so any cross-product orchestration (Concur-to-S/4 reconciliation, Blackline-to-GL automation enrichment, custom expense analytics) is being handled outside SAP's intended platform layer.
Preferred Success entitlements are being treated as a line item rather than an adoption lever — the persistent sub-budget pacing across Concur products (Expense, Direct Travel, Preferred Success itself) correlates with $2.3K of unused expert guidance, indicating the very mechanism designed to drive Concur adoption is itself dormant while the products it should be accelerating underperform.
Run an immediate root-cause investigation on the May Concur Expense drop to 21% attainment, focusing on submitter activity, integration health with Direct Travel and ExpenseIt, and any policy or workflow changes that suppressed report volume.
Trigger a BTP activation engagement to identify a first named workload — Concur-to-S/4 integration, Blackline data enrichment, or Finance analytics extension — that converts the dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement into an architectural connector for the Finance and Spend estate.
Expand Blackline Account Substantiation scope to additional ledger account groups in partnership with the controllership team, converting steady-state reconciliation usage into measurable close-cycle acceleration.
Convene a Preferred Success Concur entitlement review to schedule unused expert sessions, release readiness, and adoption workshops directly against the underperforming Concur modules — particularly Expense reactivation and Direct Travel booking compliance.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$234K
YTD Budget$351K
YTD ACV: $417K
Projected Consumed: $562K · Projected Budget: $846K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The collaborative supply chain backbone is architecturally live but losing momentum — Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration is the sole active integration point synchronizing forecasts, POs, and ASNs with S/4HANA and IBP, and its May drop signals that trading partner connectivity, the foundation of SCJ's resiliency and visibility outcomes, is contracting rather than expanding.
The new product introduction architecture is effectively broken at SCJ — with Integrated Product Development at 5.7% YTD attainment and two consecutive zero-consumption months, the bridge between PLM, S/4HANA manufacturing, and procurement is dormant, meaning formulation, cost alignment, and supply feasibility for a CPG portfolio dependent on rapid innovation are happening outside the intended digital thread.
Together these signals show SCJ is operating an execution-only supply chain — collaboration with suppliers is partially active downstream, but the upstream design-to-supply handover is absent, so product decisions cannot be tested against supplier capacity or cost feasibility before they hit manufacturing, eroding the resiliency Business Network is meant to deliver.
At current run-rates SCJ is on track to forfeit ~$284K of contracted Supply Chain value across the two products (~$129K Business Network shortfall plus ~$155K IPD shortfall), concentrating risk in the two capabilities most tied to innovation speed and trading partner reach.
Diagnose the May Business Network consumption drop with supply chain operations to determine whether the regression is in trading partner activity, document flow volume, or an S/4HANA/IBP integration, and re-baseline the onboarding pipeline for forecast, PO, and ASN collaboration.
Escalate Integrated Product Development to R&D and supply chain leadership and anchor reactivation on a defined set of CPG formulation lines, activating recipe development, specification management, and compliance handover as the first live use cases.
Establish the architectural linkage between Integrated Product Development and Business Network so new product specifications and bills of material flow into supplier collaboration scenarios, ensuring design decisions are validated against trading partner capacity and cost.
Stand up a joint Supply Chain adoption scorecard covering trading partner count, collaborative document volume, and active NPI projects so the LoB measures architectural coverage rather than just consumption value.
Stepan Company
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $506K
YTD Budget: $672K
YTD ACV: $650K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M
Projected Budget: $1.7M
Projected ACV: $1.6M
EA Insights and Actions
Stepan has contracted a full SAP stack across Finance, HCM, and Data & AI totaling well over $1M ACV, yet BTP — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement appearing as a $0 zero-start in both Data & AI and Finance, alongside declining Integration Suite (-17%) and dormant Joule Studio in Technology Foundation — is the single shared dependency failing across every domain, meaning the clean-core, AI-extensible, integrated enterprise the portfolio was sized for cannot be assembled regardless of LoB intent.
The decline pattern is identical across domains — Employee Central erodes and drags Performance, Compensation, and Onboarding down with it; Ariba Buying contracts while Sourcing and Catalog sit dormant; BDC has no Database Cloud underneath it — revealing a consistent architectural anti-pattern where Stepan operates each suite as isolated transactional modules rather than as integrated process backbones, blocking the source-to-pay, hire-to-grow, and data-to-insight outcomes the suites were bought to deliver.
Preferred Success entitlements are burning at full ACV in both Ariba (~$9.8K/month) and SuccessFactors while the very modules they were contracted to protect decline in parallel, meaning roughly $200K+ of annual adoption capacity is being consumed as a flat fee rather than as the corrective lever for the suite-wide attainment slide it was designed to be.
With ~7 months remaining in FY2026 and structural zero-starts on BDC (~$8.5K), CPEA across two domains, and Ariba Catalog, combined with double-digit declines in Integration Suite, Ariba Buying, and Employee Central, a material portion of Stepan's annual commitment is mathematically unrecoverable at current burn — signaling the contract was sized to an architectural ambition the customer has not operationally adopted.
Convene a single cross-domain BTP architecture review covering CPEA (Data & AI and Finance), Integration Suite, and Joule Studio to re-anchor the platform layer to a named pipeline of S/4, Ariba, SuccessFactors, and BDC integration and extension scenarios, treating BTP as one enterprise fabric rather than four isolated burn-downs.
Redirect the full Preferred Success entitlement pool across Ariba and SuccessFactors onto the specific declining modules — Employee Central data health, Ariba Sourcing/Catalog activation, and Concur Expense submission recovery — with documented value-realization plans tied to attainment reversal.
Run an enterprise-level commitment rebaseline with Stepan that maps every dormant or declining meter to either a sequenced activation roadmap or a formal at-risk flag, so the FY2026 contract is realigned to the architecture the customer is actually operating.
Sponsor a Chief Architect-level integration coherence workshop that traces the three broken end-to-end chains — hire-to-grow (Recruiting→Onboarding→EC→Payroll), source-to-pay (Sourcing→Catalog→Buying→Invoicing), and data-to-insight (S/4→Datasphere→BDC→SAC) — and assigns single-threaded process owners to each chain rather than module owners.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$3K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $9K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's entire BAIP Data & AI architecture is dormant — Business Data Cloud (the data fabric for AI and analytics) and both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement meters (BDC Public runtime and Database Cloud persistence) all sit at $0, meaning the LoB has contracted a complete data-to-insight stack but activated none of it.
The BDC zero-start is structurally compounded by the CPEA zero-start — even if a Datasphere or SAC use case were scoped, there is no provisioned BTP runtime or database tier underneath it, so the LoB cannot land a data product without first activating the foundational platform layer.
With $0 of a combined ~$8,487 annual commitment consumed and 7 months remaining, the LoB has no agentic AI grounding, no business data fabric, and no analytics surface in production — the 'reliable AI on mission-critical data' outcome BDC was bought to deliver is fully blocked.
The CPEA Database Cloud meter being idle alongside BDC is the most telling architectural signal — persistence is the prerequisite for any Datasphere space or replicated SAP data product, so the data foundation cannot be stood up regardless of analytics intent.
Run a joint BDC + CPEA activation workshop that sequences a single end-to-end use case: provision Database Cloud, connect one SAP source into Datasphere, and surface it in SAC to break $0 across all three meters simultaneously.
Identify the first BDC data product aligned to a Stepan business domain (finance close, supply, or commercial analytics) so BDC consumption is anchored to a named LoB outcome rather than generic onboarding.
Map the committed CPEA spend to specific BTP services (Integration Suite for source connectivity, Database Cloud for Datasphere persistence) so the platform layer is provisioned in direct support of the BDC use case rather than as an unrelated workload.
Formally rebaseline the combined Data & AI commitment against a realistic activation roadmap or flag the full annual value as at-risk, given the burn rate required to recover is now structurally above original pacing.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$23K
YTD Budget$28K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $56K · Projected Budget: $69K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is contracted as a three-pillar platform — Joule Studio for AI extensibility, Integration Suite for process connectivity, and broader BTP services — but all three pillars are simultaneously trending downward, indicating Stepan is treating BTP as a static utility rather than the strategic extension and integration backbone the enterprise agreement was sized for.
Integration Suite is the dominant workload at ~$3.3K/month yet declining 17% over five months, meaning the LoB's primary cross-system integration fabric — the connective tissue between S/4, cloud apps, and third-party systems — is contracting just as it should be scaling to support new interfaces and event-driven scenarios.
Joule Studio consumption is stuck in a $525–$650 band at under 60% attainment, signaling that the AI agent and copilot extensibility capability is provisioned but not being operationalized — Stepan owns the runway for generative AI extensions but is not building on it, leaving the Joule investment as shelfware against the broader BTP entitlement.
The 'Technology Foundation Other' BTP services bucket is also eroding to ~75% attainment, which combined with the Integration Suite decline shows the foundational platform services (extension runtime, data, security) are losing workloads rather than gaining them — a leading indicator that net-new BTP-based innovation is not landing at Stepan.
Convene a BTP architecture review with Stepan's platform owners to map which integration flows, extensions, and data services have gone dormant across Integration Suite and Technology Foundation Other, and re-anchor them to active S/4 and LoB process roadmaps.
Launch a Joule Studio enablement track with a concrete first use case (e.g., a Joule agent on an existing S/4 or Ariba process) to convert the flat $580/month run rate into demonstrable AI extensibility tied to a business owner.
Establish a single quarterly BTP consumption governance forum covering all three sub-areas under LPR868, re-baselining the $13,478 Joule, $49,882 Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation Other ceilings against a named pipeline of net-new services rather than treating each as an isolated burn-down.
Identify the specific Integration Suite artifacts (iFlows, APIs, events) driving the five-month decline and engage their technical owners to determine whether workloads have been retired, migrated off BTP, or simply lost sponsorship.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
69.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$292K
YTD Budget$421K
YTD ACV: $396K
Projected Consumed: $700K · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $950K
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's Source-to-Pay architecture is inverting — Ariba Sourcing (13.9% attainment, declining) and Ariba Catalog (0.5% attainment) are dormant while Ariba Buying and Invoicing is contracting from $54K to $37K/month, meaning purchases are executing without upstream sourcing events or catalog-guided buying, eroding the spend compliance and savings capture the suite was contracted to deliver.
Travel & Expense shows a split-brain pattern — supporting capabilities (Analytics 99%, ExpenseIt 108%, Reporting 115%, User Support 111%, Connectors 111%) are all running hot while the core Concur Expense transaction engine has collapsed to 27.9% attainment, indicating the surrounding ecosystem is mature but the primary expense submission workflow has stalled and is starving the analytics and audit layers of the data they were architected to process.
Compliance and platform foundations are unevenly activated — Document & Reporting Compliance has finally gone live with momentum ($0→$237/month) but is scoped too narrowly to absorb its $3.8K budget, while Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement remains at $0 consumption five months in, leaving the BTP extensibility layer that should underpin Ariba and Concur integrations entirely unrealized.
Preferred Success Ariba is being drawn down as a flat monthly entitlement rather than a value lever — its passive $9,765/month burn coincides with Ariba Sourcing, Catalog, and Buying all declining, signaling the success services contracted to drive Ariba adoption are not being directed at the modules actively losing traction.
Reconstruct the Source-to-Pay flow at Stepan by tying Ariba Sourcing event outcomes into Ariba Catalog content and routing requisitions through guided buying in Ariba Buying and Invoicing, closing the leakage of off-platform PO and invoice volume.
Redirect Preferred Success Ariba entitlements toward targeted enablement on Ariba Catalog supplier onboarding and Ariba Sourcing event creation, converting the passive $70K of unused success capacity into activation of the dormant upstream procurement modules.
Diagnose the Concur Expense submission collapse with Stepan's T&E owners to determine whether user disengagement, policy change, or backlog is the driver, since the entire Concur analytics, audit, and reporting investment depends on restoring transaction throughput at the core.
Activate Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement against a defined first workload that extends Document & Reporting Compliance into additional Stepan legal entities, simultaneously closing the BTP zero-consumption gap and the statutory reporting expansion gap.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$191K
YTD Budget$220K
YTD ACV: $255K
Projected Consumed: $460K · Projected Budget: $533K · Projected ACV: $611K
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's HCM foundation is eroding from the core outward — Employee Central, the system of record that feeds payroll, talent, learning, and onboarding, is in active decline (95.3%→91.8% attainment), which explains why every downstream module (Performance 76.1%, Compensation 74.3%, Career Development 51%, Onboarding 40%) is simultaneously losing ground; the LoB is not facing isolated module problems but a deteriorating HR data backbone that is starving the entire suite.
The talent lifecycle is architecturally contracted but operationally fragmented — Recruiting is capped at a narrow requisition set ($3,938 plateau), Onboarding absorbs only 40% of new-hire flow, and Career Development sits at 51%, meaning candidates are being hired into a hire-to-grow journey that breaks at every handoff and prevents the skills-driven talent framework SuccessFactors is designed to deliver.
Employee Central Payroll is structurally under-scoped rather than under-adopted — the flat $4,759–$4,906 burn against a $7,000 budget signals only a subset of Stepan's legal entities or country payrolls were activated, leaving the unified HR-to-payroll architecture incomplete and forcing parallel payroll operations outside the SuccessFactors cloud.
Preferred Success entitlements are burning at full ACV with zero behavioral variance while every SuccessFactors module declines in parallel — the customer success, release readiness, and adoption guidance designed to prevent exactly this erosion is being paid for but not consumed, which is the proximate cause of the suite-wide attainment slide.
Commission an Employee Central data and process health diagnostic as the root-cause anchor for the suite-wide decline, since restoring the HR system of record is prerequisite to recovering Performance, Compensation, Career Development, and Onboarding adoption.
Activate the Preferred Success engagement model with Stepan — book Customer Success Partner sessions, release readiness reviews, and a documented value-realization plan — and tie its deliverables directly to reversing the attainment slide across Employee Central, Performance and Goals, and Onboarding.
Run an Employee Central Payroll scoping review to identify unactivated legal entities, countries, or employee populations and bring them onto the platform to convert the structural ~32% shortfall into contracted utilization.
Re-sequence the talent lifecycle by tying Recruiting, Onboarding, and Career Development activation to shared hiring-manager and new-hire cohorts so the hire-to-grow journey functions as one integrated process rather than three under-used modules.
Target Corporation
Retail
YTD Consumed: $2.6M
YTD Budget: $2.4M
YTD ACV: $3.1M
Projected Consumed: $6.2M
Projected Budget: $5.8M
Projected ACV: $7.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Target's enterprise architecture is inverted — transactional layers (Ariba P2P at 121%, Concur estate at 106-130%, Integration Suite at 129-158%) are over-consuming while every foundational and forward-looking layer (BDC at $0, SAC Planning plateau, Build at 15%, Joule contracting, Multi Bank Connect at 36%) is dormant, meaning the customer is scaling transaction volume on an architecture that has no governed data backbone, no planning loop, and no AI surface to convert that volume into differentiated retail outcomes.
Integration Suite is the single live platform fabric carrying Target's enterprise, but with its CPEA wrapper at $0, BDC unactivated, and Build/Joule stalled, the iFlows are moving data point-to-point without API governance, event mesh, or a consuming extension layer — blocking the closed-loop story from P2P/T&E execution back into Datasphere-fed planning and agentic decisioning across Finance and Supply Chain.
Target is paying for guided value realization on autopilot — Ariba, Concur, and SCM Preferred Success engagements are all drawing entitlement on flat or over-burn patterns against domains that are either breaching ceilings (P2P/T&E) or architecturally hollow (SCM shows only success hours with no visible execution platform), meaning the success motion is reinforcing consumption inertia rather than steering Target toward the unified data, planning, and AI architecture the contract was sold on.
The contracted enterprise architecture commits Target to a unified data-and-AI retail operating model (BDC + SAC + Joule + Build on BTP), but the YTD picture — ~$27.8K BDC, ~$83K Integration Suite CPEA, ~$92K SAC Planning, and Build/Joule capacity sitting idle while ~$575K of Ariba P2P overage accumulates — signals Target will arrive at renewal having paid for an AI-ready foundation while operating a transactional-only estate, undermining the investment thesis at the negotiation table.
Convene a cross-domain enterprise architecture review with Target's CIO, CFO, and Supply Chain sponsors to re-sequence the BTP foundation (BDC activation, Integration Suite CPEA governance, Build/Joule reactivation) ahead of further transactional scale, framing it as the prerequisite to closing the loop from Ariba/Concur execution into SAC Planning and treasury.
Restructure all three Preferred Success engagements (Ariba, Concur, SCM) into a single outcome-based program tied to activating dormant architecture — BDC data products, SAC Planning use cases, Multi Bank Connect onboarding, and SCM platform discovery — so guided value hours are pulled into architectural uplift rather than flat drawdown.
Open a consolidated commercial conversation with Target that pairs the ~$575K Ariba P2P and Concur ceiling true-ups with reallocation of idle BDC, Integration Suite CPEA, Build, Joule, and SAC Planning entitlement, presenting one enterprise architecture commitment rather than disconnected line-item negotiations at renewal.
Run a Supply Chain architecture discovery with Target to confirm whether the absence of IBP/EWM/TM/Ariba SCM execution products is a reporting gap or a genuine void, and link findings to an integrated planning roadmap that connects SCM to SAC Planning and BDC so success entitlement has a live platform to accelerate.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
87.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$60K
YTD Budget$69K
YTD ACV: $97K
Projected Consumed: $145K · Projected Budget: $173K · Projected ACV: $232K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Target is architecturally fractured — SAP Business Data Cloud, which is contracted to anchor the business data fabric and feed mission-critical data products into downstream analytics and AI, sits at $0 consumption while SAC BI runs independently, meaning the LoB is consuming visualization capacity without the unified data backbone it was designed to sit on top of.
SAC BI is operating as a standalone reporting tool rather than the unified analytics-and-planning surface it was contracted to be, and its four-month attainment decline from 94.4% to 80.3% is consistent with users hitting the ceiling of siloed datasets because Datasphere and BDC data products are not yet activated to deliver the trusted, business-context-rich content that sustains engagement.
Without BDC live, Target has no path to the agentic AI and Joule-driven planning capabilities embedded in this contract — the LoB is paying for an AI-ready architecture but operating in a classic BI-only mode, deferring the differentiated outcomes (trusted AI decisions, unified financial and operational planning) that justified the $27,840 BDC commitment alongside the $145,137 SAC ceiling.
The combined YTD picture — $0 of $27,840 on BDC and a $8,620 shortfall on SAC BI against $68,952 plan — shows the data platform investment is regressing rather than compounding, with the dormant foundation actively dragging consumption on the layer above it.
Stand up a BDC activation workshop with Target's data platform owners to land a first production data product in Datasphere and wire it into SAC BI, converting the dormant foundation into a live source for existing dashboards.
Re-architect the SAC BI adoption motion around BDC-sourced data products and Joule-assisted analytics scenarios rather than net-new standalone dashboards, so renewed consumption reinforces the integrated stack instead of deepening the silo.
Sequence a joint BDC + SAC roadmap with Target's analytics and finance owners that targets at least one unified planning use case, unlocking the planning capabilities currently stranded inside the SAC BI entitlement.
Establish a single consumption governance view across BDC and SAC BI so the $27,840 BDC runway and the $8,125 monthly SAC gap are managed as one architectural commitment rather than two disconnected line items.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
76.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$65K
YTD Budget$86K
YTD ACV: $380K
Projected Consumed: $157K · Projected Budget: $253K · Projected ACV: $911K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is the only live capability in this foundation — SAP Integration Suite is running 129–158% of monthly plan while the Integration Suite CPEA wrapper sits at $0 of $32K YTD, meaning Target is consuming integration runtime without drawing on the contracted BTP services that extend it (event mesh, API management tiers, trading partner management), so the LoB has connectivity but no governed platform around it.
The extensibility and citizen-development layer is architecturally contracted but operationally dormant — SAP Build sits at 15% YTD attainment and Joule Studio's CPEA is contracting from $116 to $102/month, so Target has integrations flowing but no low-code surface or AI-agent tooling consuming them, blocking the 'apps + workflows + agents on top of integrated data' outcome the Technology Foundation is designed to deliver.
The full BAIP foundation is pacing to leave roughly $1.9K (Joule CPEA) + $5.4K (Build) + ~$83K (Integration Suite CPEA) of contracted value unrealized against only ~$26K of Integration Suite overage, indicating Target is over-indexed on point-to-point integration spend while the surrounding platform, extension, and AI capabilities that justify a BTP enterprise agreement remain unactivated.
Reframe the Integration Suite CPEA as the activation vehicle for API management, event mesh, and AI-ready API exposure so the $32K idle YTD budget is tied to the same iFlows already driving Integration Suite overage, converting raw integration spend into governed, agent-consumable services.
Pair the May SAP Build uptick with a Joule Studio reactivation plan by identifying 3–5 process extensions or agent scenarios that consume existing Integration Suite APIs, closing the Build and Joule consumption gaps through a single use-case pipeline rather than two separate adoption motions.
Establish a unified BTP consumption governance review across Integration Suite, Build, and both CPEA lines so Target's Technology Foundation is steered as one architecture — rebalancing the Integration Suite overage against the Joule and CPEA shortfalls before contract renewal conversations.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
109.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.4M
YTD Budget$2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.6M
Projected Consumed: $5.8M · Projected Budget: $5.2M · Projected ACV: $6.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Target's procure-to-pay spine is operationally hot but governance-light — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is burning at 121% of plan ($575K projected overrun) while Ariba Contracts sits at 67% attainment and Ariba Sourcing is flat-lining at ~27% on a single recurring event, meaning purchases and invoices are executing at scale without the upstream sourcing competition or contract guardrails that justify the suite investment.
The Concur T&E estate is uniformly over-consuming across Expense, ExpenseIt, Drive, Direct Travel, Analytics, Company Bill Statement, and extract services (all 106–130% YTD attainment) — adoption has clearly landed, but every contract ceiling is converging on breach simultaneously, making ACV capacity, not user engagement, the binding constraint on T&E operations.
The two Preferred Success engagements are drawing down entitlement on autopilot — Ariba Preferred Success is perfectly flat at $9,831/month matching ACV exactly while Concur Preferred Success runs 112% to plan without a documented outcome map, meaning Target is paying for guided value realization but receiving straight-line consumption rather than targeted adoption acceleration on the very products that are over- or under-running.
The forward-looking finance and treasury layer is dormant relative to transactional spend — SAC Planning is locked in a $13.2K/month plateau ($92K of capacity unused) and Multi Bank Connect is at 36% attainment with a negative May entry, leaving Target with strong transactional throughput in P2P and T&E but limited driver-based planning or unified bank connectivity to close the loop back to S/4HANA treasury and FP&A.
Open a consolidated Ariba commercial conversation with Target that true-ups Buying and Invoicing against the $575K projected overage while pairing it with a Contracts and Sourcing adoption sprint (clause library, repository migration, RFx category expansion) so contract governance and competitive sourcing catch up to invoice volume.
Initiate a single Concur capacity right-sizing review covering Analytics, Company Bill Statement, Direct Travel, Drive, ExpenseIt, Extract Services, and WalkMe Premium to lift ACV ceilings ahead of mid-year throttling, and bundle it with policy and pre-trip approval tuning so adoption growth translates to compliant spend.
Restructure both Preferred Success engagements into outcome-based roadmaps — anchor Ariba Preferred Success to Contracts and Sourcing reactivation use cases and Concur Preferred Success to a documented value story (cycle time, compliance, audit readiness) to convert flat drawdown into measurable LoB outcomes ahead of renewal.
Launch a finance-and-treasury activation play that pairs an SAC Planning use-case workshop (workforce, capex, driver-based revenue) with a Multi Bank Connect onboarding sprint to add bank accounts and payment formats, closing the $92K planning and $12.5K treasury budget gaps and connecting transactional spend back to forward-looking finance.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management

YTD Consumed$48K

YTD ACV: $48K

Solution Insights and Actions
Target's Supply Chain Management adoption surface is architecturally thin — Preferred Success SCM is the only signal present, meaning the LoB is consuming success-enablement services without any visible underlying SCM execution or planning product (IBP, EWM, TM, Ariba) to enable, which suggests success hours are being burned without a core SCM platform to accelerate.
The flat $9,576/month burn against an $8,400 budget is the signature of entitlement accrual, not advisory engagement — Preferred Success is designed to drive release readiness, expert guidance, and adoption acceleration on live SCM workloads, and a fixed-rate consumption pattern indicates Target is paying for a guidance layer that isn't being pulled into active architectural work.
Projected $114,917 annual consumption against a $101,886 budget creates a $13,031 overage with 7 months unreported, meaning the LoB will exhaust its success entitlement before the back-half SCM planning and fiscal close cycles where expert guidance has the highest architectural leverage.
Convene an SCM architecture review with Target to map Preferred Success hours to specific SCM product workstreams (planning, logistics, or procurement) and confirm whether the absence of catalog SCM products reflects a reporting gap or a genuine architectural void that Preferred Success cannot remediate alone.
Reprofile the remaining Preferred Success SCM entitlement toward high-leverage outcomes — release readiness, guided expert sessions, and adoption workstreams tied to upcoming SCM initiatives — to convert passive accrual into measurable LoB capability uplift before the overage trajectory forces a mid-year true-up.
Escalate a coverage-risk conversation with Target's SCM sponsor on the $13,031 projected overage, presenting either an entitlement expansion tied to a concrete SCM adoption roadmap or a consumption deceleration plan to preserve advisory capacity through fiscal year-end.
Tate and Lyle Americas LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $109K
YTD Budget: $135K
YTD ACV: $173K
Projected Consumed: $261K
Projected Budget: $330K
Projected ACV: $416K
EA Insights and Actions
Tate and Lyle has only one L1 domain (Finance and Spend Management) materially represented in its SAP architecture, and even within that domain the T&E stack runs at a 73-85% adoption ceiling while statutory compliance sits dark — the enterprise architecture is single-pillar and sub-scale, meaning SAP is functioning as a point T&E tool rather than a connected finance backbone for the Americas business.
The simultaneous over-consumption of Company Bill Statement (117-129%) and collapse of Concur Expense (116% to 30.6%) exposes a broken integration contract between card ingestion and expense submission — AP is closing the books on reconciled-but-uncoded card spend, which structurally undermines the financial close integrity that a CPG manufacturer like Tate and Lyle needs for SOX and segment reporting.
Document & Reporting Compliance entitlement is provisioned but inactive (<0.5%) while Preferred Success Concur sits 17% under-consumed — the customer is paying for both the compliance engine and the enablement capacity to activate it, yet neither is wired into the Americas tax obligations (1099, GST/HST, CFDI), leaving statutory automation dependent on manual or third-party paths and exposing the S/4HANA tax thesis as architecturally hollow.
Across the entire contracted footprint, the only component pacing healthily is the one that ingests external data (Company Bill Statement), while every component that requires user action, policy enforcement, or compliance discipline is under-adopted — indicating the architecture has no internal change-management or governance layer to convert provisioned capability into operational outcomes.
Commission an enterprise architecture review focused on whether the SAP footprint should be expanded beyond T&E into supply chain, procurement, or manufacturing domains that align to Tate and Lyle's specialty food ingredients operating model, or right-sized to its current single-domain reality.
Run a finance close integration workshop to re-establish the Company Bill Statement to Concur Expense submission chain and activate Document & Reporting Compliance against a named Americas statutory obligation, converting two dormant-but-paid-for capabilities into live close and audit coverage.
Redirect the unused Preferred Success Concur entitlement into a single governance-led adoption program covering Triplink, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services, treating the 80% ceiling as an enterprise architecture problem rather than four independent product adoption problems.
Initiate a contract true-up review across the Concur portfolio to rebalance the Company Bill Statement overage against the systemic under-consumption elsewhere, ensuring the next renewal reflects the customer's actual single-domain architectural posture rather than the aspirational full-stack one.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
80.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$109K
YTD Budget$135K
YTD ACV: $173K
Projected Consumed: $261K · Projected Budget: $330K · Projected ACV: $416K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense stack is architecturally complete end-to-end (Direct Travel for booking, Triplink for capture, Expense + ExpenseIt for submission, Intelligent Audit for compliance, Reporting Services for visibility, User Support Desk for enablement) yet every component except Company Bill Statement is pacing at 73–85% attainment — Tate and Lyle has built a full T&E platform but is running it at three-quarters capacity, meaning policy enforcement, data capture, and audit coverage are systemically leaky rather than failing in any single product.
Concur - Expense is the architectural anchor of the T&E stack and its collapse from 116% in March to 30.6% in May while Company Bill Statement runs 117–129% over budget reveals a structural disconnect — corporate card transactions are flowing in but employees are not submitting expense reports against them, leaving AP with reconciled card spend that lacks the line-item, policy, and GL coding required for clean financial close.
Document & Reporting Compliance at <0.5% attainment ($32 projected vs $8,869 budget) means the statutory e-invoicing and digital reporting backbone scoped for Tate and Lyle's Americas tax obligations is provisioned but architecturally dark — compliance is being handled outside SAP through manual or third-party means, creating audit exposure and undermining the S/4HANA tax automation thesis.
Preferred Success Concur is under-consumed by ~17% ($7,077 projected gap) at the exact moment the Concur stack shows systemic under-adoption — the customer is paying for guided enablement they are not pulling on, which is the direct cause of the flat consumption ceilings across Expense, ExpenseIt, Triplink, and Reporting Services.
Activate Document & Reporting Compliance against Tate and Lyle's highest-volume Americas statutory obligation (US 1099, Canadian GST/HST, or Mexican CFDI) to convert dormant entitlement into live compliance coverage before FY2026 audit cycles.
Reconnect Concur - Expense submission discipline to Company Bill Statement card feeds by enforcing mandatory expense reporting for all corporate card transactions, closing the structural gap between card spend ingestion (over-consuming) and expense report submission (collapsing).
Redirect unused Preferred Success Concur hours into a targeted adoption program covering Triplink supplier enrollment, Intelligent Audit rule expansion, and Reporting Services dashboard activation — using the entitlement to fix the stack-wide 80% attainment ceiling.
Initiate a contract true-up on Concur - Company Bill Statement to right-size the $1,389 projected overage while validating the May softening with the AP owner to confirm sustained transaction volume.
Tenneco Automotive
Automotive
YTD Consumed: $596K
YTD Budget: $780K
YTD ACV: $966K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
Tenneco has contracted BTP/CPEA as the extensibility and integration backbone across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance, yet near-zero activation across all three means the enterprise has no operational platform layer connecting SuccessFactors, IBP, C4C, and finance — every domain is effectively running as an island, blocking the integrated lead-to-cash, hire-to-retire, and plan-to-deliver flows the architecture was designed to enable.
Across a combined annual contract footprint where the front-office (C4C $12.7K), data platform (CPEA Data & AI $71), finance extensibility (CPEA $7), and integration channels all sit at $0 consumption, Tenneco is exhibiting a systemic onboarding-and-governance failure rather than isolated adoption issues — the LoBs are paying for a composable enterprise but operating a fragmented one.
The only domains showing real consumption — SuccessFactors (declining talent modules) and IBP (flat single-use forecasting at 64% attainment with $12.3K unconsumed) — are both regressing because the BTP integration fabric meant to connect them to finance, CX, and analytics is dormant, meaning Tenneco's automotive-critical demand-to-supply and workforce-to-cost loops cannot close end-to-end.
Tenneco's architecture reflects an automotive supplier that has bought a clean-core, AI-ready SAP stack but is operating it as disconnected point solutions — without Integration Suite as a governed fabric and Joule/BTP as the AI extensibility layer, the company cannot deliver the supply resilience, margin transparency, and workforce agility the sector now demands.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with Tenneco's CIO and LoB sponsors to re-baseline the BTP/CPEA entitlements across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance onto a single governed consumption plan anchored to named cross-domain workloads.
Launch an integration backbone activation program that stands up Integration Suite as the connective tissue between SuccessFactors, IBP, C4C, and S/4 — prioritizing the hire-to-onboard, performance-to-pay, and demand-to-supply iFlows that are currently broken.
Establish an enterprise consumption governance forum covering all L1 domains to convert the at-risk annual ACV across C4C, CPEA, IBP, and SuccessFactors talent modules into a sequenced activation backlog tied to measurable business outcomes.
Run a cross-domain architecture workshop pairing IBP demand signals with SuccessFactors workforce planning and finance/SAC scenarios on BTP to demonstrate the closed-loop automotive supply-and-margin story the current fragmented state is blocking.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$41
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $71 · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco's entire Data & AI foundation in BAIP is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the entitlement vehicle for both BDC Public and Database Cloud BTP services, and with $0 consumed against a combined $39 YTD budget, neither the application platform nor the data tier has been activated, meaning no SAP-native data, AI, or extension workloads exist in this LoB.
The dual-stall across BDC Public ($0 of $19 YTD, $35 annual) and Database Cloud ($0 of $20 YTD, $36 annual) indicates this is not a workload-selection problem but an onboarding failure — Tenneco has no landing zone, no provisioned services, and no consuming use cases, which blocks every downstream Data & AI capability the CPEA was meant to underwrite.
With ~100% of the combined $71 annual ACV unconsumed and only 7 months remaining, the architectural risk is forfeiture of the entire Data & AI entitlement, leaving Tenneco without the BTP data platform and database foundation needed to support analytics, integration, or AI initiatives that depend on this contract.
Convene a joint CPEA activation workshop covering both BDC Public and Database Cloud to select an initial production workload — such as a HANA Cloud instance backing a SAC or Datasphere use case — and stand up the BTP landing zone that currently does not exist.
Escalate the zero-consumption pattern on LPR868 to Tenneco's executive sponsor with a recovery roadmap that pairs named BTP services to specific Data & AI business outcomes across the $35 BDC Public and $36 Database Cloud budgets.
Assign a BTP technical onboarding lead to Tenneco to resolve the foundational gaps — subaccount setup, entitlements mapping, and identity provisioning — that are preventing any consumption from materializing against the CPEA.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
37.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$4K
YTD Budget$10K
YTD ACV: $10K
Projected Consumed: $9K · Projected Budget: $24K · Projected ACV: $23K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco's Technology Foundation is architecturally contracted but functionally fragmented — CPEA is being consumed for ad-hoc BTP services and Integration Suite while a parallel CPEA-for-Integration-Suite entitlement sits at $0, meaning the LoB is running integration workloads through one channel while a duplicate contracted channel remains entirely dormant.
Integration Suite is deployed as a project tool, not an integration backbone — the volatile burst pattern (24% YTD attainment with $102→$571→$374 monthly swings) shows iFlows are being built reactively for point projects rather than as a governed API and event fabric, which blocks the AI-ready, agent-consumable integration layer the platform is architected to deliver.
BTP consumption governance is architecturally absent — CPEA shows simultaneous under-run (42.5% on Joule Studio, 0% on Integration Suite entitlement) and over-run (109.5% on Technology Foundation Other with 167%+ spike months), proving workloads are being spun up without capacity planning and leaving the LoB exposed to both shelfware and unbudgeted overage on the same contract.
The Joule Studio footprint is stalling before it scales — May's drop to 34.8% attainment after a March–April uptick indicates AI extensibility pilots are not maturing into production agents, leaving Tenneco without the agent-development capability needed to consume the Integration Suite APIs it is concurrently building.
Consolidate the two CPEA entitlements onto a single governed BTP consumption plan and activate the dormant Integration Suite-linked CPEA against a named first workload to eliminate parallel shelfware.
Stand up an Integration Suite center-of-enablement with a prioritized iFlow backlog and reusable content catalog to convert project-driven bursts into a sustained integration program targeting the $9,166 ACV gap.
Implement a monthly BTP consumption governance cadence covering all CPEA service buckets to cap the Technology Foundation Other overage and redirect unused Joule Studio and Integration Suite budget toward activated use cases.
Pair the Joule Studio footprint with a concrete agent use case grounded on Integration Suite APIs so the AI consumption layer and the integration layer mature together rather than in isolation.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
0.0%

YTD Budget$5K
YTD ACV: $11K
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $13K · Projected ACV: $26K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Customer Experience domain at Tenneco is architecturally provisioned but functionally absent — Cloud for Customer is the sole CX product in scope and it sits at $0 consumption against a $5,217 YTD budget, meaning the LoB has no operational system of engagement for sales pipeline or service case management despite having paid for it.
Without Cloud for Customer activation, the front-office tier of the CX architecture is disconnected from any downstream ERP or service fulfillment processes, leaving sales reps and service agents to work in shadow tools and breaking the lead-to-cash and case-to-resolution traceability the platform is designed to deliver.
The ACV load pattern stopping in May while budget continues through December signals a contract that was provisioned but never operationalized — the full $12,693 annual entitlement is trending toward forfeiture, which means Tenneco is funding CX capability while the LoB continues to operate without it.
Convene a Customer Experience activation workshop with Tenneco's sales and service operations leads to define the initial use case (lead management or service ticketing) and provision the first wave of named users against the $5,217 unconsumed YTD budget.
Escalate the zero-consumption pattern to Tenneco's executive sponsor and co-author a phased CX rollout plan that recovers the remaining $7,476 of at-risk annual budget.
Validate whether Cloud for Customer is intended to integrate with Tenneco's existing ERP or commerce stack and scope the integration design before user onboarding, so the activation delivers end-to-end CX value rather than another isolated front-office tool.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
