Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the architectural foundation for extending Finance and Spend Management capabilities — custom planning models, finance integrations, and spend analytics extensions all depend on BTP, yet zero consumption means Tenneco's FP&A function has no extensibility runtime, forcing any non-standard finance logic back into spreadsheets or core ERP customisation.
A flat-line $0 against a $5 YTD and $7 annual commitment is not slow adoption but architectural dormancy — the LoB has paid for a platform capability it has not connected to any finance or spend workload, signalling a disconnect between the contract and the FP&A roadmap.
Without an activated BTP layer, downstream Finance and Spend Management investments (SAC, Ariba, S/4) lose their integration and extension fabric, capping the LoB at out-of-the-box functionality and blocking composable finance scenarios such as automated close extensions or custom spend approval flows.
Run a BTP activation workshop scoped specifically to FP&A and Spend use cases — SAC data actions, Ariba integration flows, or close automation extensions — to anchor the first production workload to a tangible finance outcome.
Stand up a joint BTP consumption governance forum with Tenneco's finance IT and technical platform owner to track service-level usage against the $7 annual commitment and prevent forfeiture.
Map Tenneco's Finance and Spend Management roadmap against BTP-dependent capabilities (integration suite, build apps, AI services) to convert the dormant agreement into a sequenced backlog of finance extension workloads.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
77.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$583K
YTD Budget$750K
YTD ACV: $927K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M · Projected Budget: $1.7M · Projected ACV: $2.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco's HCM architecture has the foundation laid but the talent layer is hollow — Employee Central (78.7% attainment, declining) is the system of record feeding Compensation, Learning, Performance, and Career Development, yet every downstream talent module is regressing or flatlined, meaning the LoB has a core HRIS without the integrated talent lifecycle it was architected to enable.
The hire-to-productive-employee journey is architecturally broken — Recruiting Management is capped at 83% with a flat $11.4K/month plateau while Onboarding sits at 12% attainment and barely $850/month consumption, so candidates are being sourced through SuccessFactors but onboarding is happening outside the platform, severing the integrated new-hire workflow Employee Central was meant to anchor.
The performance-to-pay loop is collapsing in parallel — Performance and Goals (65.7% and declining) and Compensation (63.1% and declining) are designed to operate as a linked workflow where performance data drives merit and bonus decisions, and their synchronized erosion indicates Tenneco's Total Rewards process is no longer running through the architected SuccessFactors path.
Career and Talent Development is provisioned but architecturally orphaned — flatlined consumption near $1K/month against a $74K budget shows the skills-driven growth layer that should connect Learning completions to Performance outcomes and internal mobility is dormant, leaving Tenneco with training delivery but no talent development framework.
Commission an end-to-end HCM process trace from Employee Central through Recruiting, Onboarding, Performance, Compensation, and Career Development to identify where Tenneco's HR operations have diverged from the integrated SuccessFactors architecture and which workflows are running off-platform.
Prioritize an Onboarding activation intervention tied to Recruiting Management hires to close the largest architectural break in the hire-to-productive-employee journey and convert the capped recruiting funnel into measurable onboarding throughput.
Re-anchor the Performance-to-Compensation cycle by aligning Tenneco's Total Rewards team on a single planning cycle that uses Performance and Goals outputs as the merit and bonus input, reversing the parallel decline in both modules.
Open a Preferred Success scope rebalancing conversation to convert the $57K projected overrun into either a formal expansion or a reallocation of success services toward the dormant talent modules where adoption is structurally failing.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
65.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$14K
YTD ACV: $18K
Projected Consumed: $22K · Projected Budget: $34K · Projected ACV: $43K
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the sole supply chain planning platform contracted for Tenneco, and its flat $1,767–$1,826 monthly draw signals that only a narrow slice of its architecture — likely baseline demand forecasting — is live, leaving S&OP collaboration, inventory optimization, and response/supply planning dormant despite being part of the same unified platform.
With attainment sliding from 66.6% to 64.1% and a projected 36% ($12,281) of annual contract value unconsumed, the LoB is paying for an integrated planning suite but operating it as a single-purpose forecasting tool, which blocks the cross-functional S&OP outcomes IBP is architected to deliver.
The fixed-draw consumption pattern indicates a capped planner population rather than expanding persona coverage, meaning demand, supply, and inventory planners are not yet collaborating in the unified process IBP was designed to orchestrate — a structural gap that limits Tenneco's ability to sense and respond to automotive demand volatility.
Activate IBP's demand sensing and S&OP modules beyond the current baseline forecasting use case to unlock the collaborative planning architecture and close the $1,022 monthly consumption gap.
Run a planning persona audit across demand, supply, and inventory roles to identify which IBP modules are unlicensed-in-practice and drive activation against the $12,281 unconsumed annual value.
Establish an AI-powered demand prediction pilot leveraging IBP's native algorithms to expand value beyond static forecasting and justify the full contracted planning footprint.
The Lubrizol Corporation
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $935K
YTD Budget: $1.0M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.2M
Projected Budget: $2.5M
Projected ACV: $3.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Lubrizol has invested across seven L1 domains but every domain exhibits the same pattern — a foundational system-of-record is live while its surrounding intelligence, talent, planning, or collaboration layers sit dormant (SAC without Datasphere fabric, Commerce without CX, Employee Central without talent, Business Network without sourcing, Process Management without Intelligence conformance), meaning the enterprise is paying for integrated suites but operating them as disconnected transactional silos that block the cross-domain outcomes (specialty chemicals margin intelligence, supplier resilience, talent-driven innovation) the portfolio was assembled to deliver.
BTP is simultaneously the most strategically important and least governed layer in Lubrizol's architecture — Integration Suite at 15x burn, Joule Studio at 510%, and Datasphere flat at 62% reveal that integration and AI workloads are being stood up in production without a tenant-level consumption plan, while SAP Build sits dormant, meaning the platform is being consumed as plumbing rather than as the clean-core extensibility and AI fabric that would unify Finance, HCM, SCM, and CX data into composable business processes.
The design-to-execution governance loop is broken across the entire enterprise — Signavio Process Intelligence covers one process, Ariba Catalog/Sourcing handoffs are weakening while Buying runs 110%+, Onboarding-to-Compensation talent flow is severed, and Document & Reporting Compliance runs at 899% without architectural sequencing — collectively signaling that Lubrizol lacks an enterprise process and master-data governance spine to discipline the transactional volumes its operational systems are now generating at scale.
Contract-to-consumption asymmetry is structural rather than incidental — roughly $200K+ in projected shortfalls across Datasphere, SAP Build, Commerce Cloud, talent SuccessFactors modules, and Business Network coexist with ~$150K+ in overages on Integration Suite, Joule Studio, Concur, and Document Compliance, meaning Lubrizol's commercial envelope is misaligned with its actual architectural center of gravity and a true-forward rebaselining is required before FY27 planning to redirect dormant capacity into the platforms actually driving the business.
Convene a single enterprise architecture review covering BTP, Integration Suite, Datasphere, and Build as one platform stack with Lubrizol's CIO and architecture leadership to establish tenant governance, consumption forecasting, and a clean-core extensibility operating model before further AI and integration workloads are onboarded.
Commission a cross-domain process and data governance workshop that connects Signavio Process Intelligence coverage expansion, Ariba catalog/sourcing/contract conformance, SuccessFactors talent-cycle integration, and Datasphere semantic modeling into a single enterprise design-to-execution backbone.
Run a portfolio-level commercial recalibration with Lubrizol procurement and finance that nets dormant entitlements (Commerce Cloud, Build, talent SuccessFactors, Business Network, Datasphere) against active overages (Integration Suite, Joule Studio, Concur, Document Compliance) to realign the contract envelope to the demonstrated architectural footprint.
Establish a Lubrizol-wide adoption activation roadmap that pairs every underutilized foundational asset with a specific cross-domain use case — Commerce + CDC, Employee Central + talent, Business Network + Sourcing, SAC + Datasphere + Joule — so dormant capacity is converted into measurable business outcomes rather than renewed at status quo.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
89.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$53K
YTD Budget$59K
YTD ACV: $66K
Projected Consumed: $128K · Projected Budget: $166K · Projected ACV: $159K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation is architecturally inverted at Lubrizol — SAC BI is consuming at near-budget levels ($47.6K YTD, 94.5% attainment) while its underlying semantic layer Datasphere sits at 62% attainment with flat $1.1K/month burn, meaning the LoB is visualizing data without the unified business-context fabric Datasphere is designed to provide, leaving analytics dependent on point-to-point sources rather than a governed knowledge graph.
Datasphere is deployed as a static data store rather than an expanding data fabric — the flat $1,113/month run rate against a back-loaded budget ramping to $6,275+/month signals no new source systems, personas, or business data products are being onboarded, blocking the AI-ready data foundation that both SAC and Joule-based analytics depend on architecturally.
The April–May SAC BI step-down of ~$1,100/month coincides with Datasphere's flat consumption profile, indicating a specific analytics cohort has disengaged without a semantic-layer expansion to replace it — the LoB is losing BI surface area at the same time the data foundation isn't growing to fuel new use cases, compounding into a combined ~$38K full-year shortfall across the two products.
Planning and AI capabilities contracted within SAC BI (unified planning, Joule analytics) appear unactivated — consumption patterns reflect traditional dashboard/BI usage only, meaning Lubrizol is paying for a unified analytics-and-planning platform but operating it as a reporting tool, leaving financial and operational planning convergence on the table.
Audit the dormant SAC BI user cohort behind the April–May drop and tie re-onboarding to Datasphere-sourced models so recovered consumption reinforces the semantic layer rather than perpetuating disconnected reporting.
Run a Datasphere use-case expansion workshop to scope new business data products, additional source system integrations, and consumer personas that align to the H2 budget ramp where 64% of annual spend is planned.
Activate the planning and Joule capabilities within SAC BI by scoping a financial-or-operational planning pilot, converting the platform from BI-only usage into the unified analytics-and-planning role it is architected to deliver.
Establish a joint SAC–Datasphere adoption plan that treats the two as one stack, with every new SAC story required to consume a Datasphere-modeled data product to enforce governed business semantics across the analytics estate.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
177.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$108K
YTD Budget$61K
YTD ACV: $90K
Projected Consumed: $259K · Projected Budget: $177K · Projected ACV: $216K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is operationally live but commercially un-modeled — SAP Integration Suite went from dormant to running at 15x planned burn in April while the underlying BTP platform stair-stepped to $18,486/month, meaning Lubrizol has stood up production AI/API integration workloads on a contract sized for a pilot.
The Technology Foundation is being consumed as an integration platform, not an application platform — Integration Suite plus its BTP substrate are tracking to a combined ~$70K overrun while SAP Build sits flat at ~$680/month against a $23.9K budget, leaving the low-code extensibility layer dormant and the LoB unable to surface integrated data into custom business apps or workflows.
BTP consumption governance is structurally absent across the foundation — two separate LPR868 envelopes (Joule Studio at 510% and Integration Suite at 161%) are both breaching ceilings simultaneously, indicating workloads are being onboarded to BTP without a tenant-level consumption plan or chargeback discipline.
Joule Studio is being provisioned without a calibrated AI workload plan — $4,127 consumed against an $809 YTD budget signals agent/AI experimentation is active on BTP, but with no Build-side app surface to operationalize those agents into business processes, the AI investment risks staying in sandbox.
Run a unified BTP consumption review across both LPR868 envelopes and SAP Integration Suite to map which iFlows, AI services, and runtime allocations are driving the combined ~$78K projected overage, and right-size or true-forward the contract to the demonstrated run rate.
Launch an SAP Build activation track tied directly to the live Integration Suite flows, converting the $15,700 projected Build shortfall into citizen-developer apps that consume the new APIs Lubrizol is already paying to run.
Establish a BTP tenant governance model with Lubrizol covering service onboarding approval, consumption forecasting, and Joule Studio AI-workload sizing to prevent further unmanaged sprawl on the Technology Foundation.
Initiate a commercial recalibration conversation covering Integration Suite capacity, Joule Studio AI consumption, and BTP CPEA ceilings as a single architectural package rather than three isolated overage discussions.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
57.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$20K
YTD Budget$35K
YTD ACV: $102K
Projected Consumed: $48K · Projected Budget: $121K · Projected ACV: $245K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Signavio process transformation stack is architecturally split-brain — Process Management (design/modeling) and Process Intelligence (execution mining) are both contracted, but Management is running at 48% YTD attainment with flat modeling activity while Intelligence is locked to a single process, meaning Lubrizol has the design-to-execution feedback loop on paper but is not closing it in practice.
The sharp ACV step-ups in Process Management (April $19,212, May $48,031) without a corresponding consumption ramp indicate entitlements were sold ahead of an operating model — new business units were provisioned but the CoE governance, modeling standards, and publication workflows needed to industrialize process design across the LoB are absent.
Process Intelligence is functioning as a single-process monitoring tool rather than an enterprise process mining capability — the flat $1,227 monthly burn against a stepped budget curve climbing to $2,683 shows the LoB is extracting insight from one value stream while O2C, P2P, and S2P remain architecturally unobserved, blocking the continuous improvement loop the platform was bought to enable.
Without active linkage between Process Management models and Process Intelligence mined reality, Lubrizol's Business Transformation function cannot validate whether designed processes match executed processes — the transformation investment is producing documentation and dashboards in parallel rather than a governed, evidence-based process improvement cycle.
Stand up a Signavio CoE operating model with Lubrizol that defines modeling standards, publication governance, and a backlog of business units to onboard, directly tied to absorbing the April–May ACV uplift in Process Management.
Expand Process Intelligence coverage into at least two additional end-to-end processes (O2C, P2P, or S2P) and connect each mined process back to its Process Management model to activate the design-to-execution conformance loop.
Run a joint value review with Lubrizol's process owners to prioritize the next process analyses for Intelligence and the next modeling workloads for Management, converting flat consumption into a coordinated ramp ahead of the heavier Sep–Dec budget tranche.
Establish conformance checking between Process Management models and Process Intelligence event data as a recurring governance ritual so the CoE can demonstrate measurable process improvement outcomes rather than tool usage.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
16.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$14K
YTD Budget$83K
YTD ACV: $161K
Projected Consumed: $32K · Projected Budget: $204K · Projected ACV: $387K
Solution Insights and Actions
Customer Experience at Lubrizol is operating as a single-product Commerce footprint with no surrounding CX architecture — SAP Commerce Cloud is the only adopted capability, meaning the LoB has a transactional storefront but lacks the connected marketing, sales, service, or customer data layers needed to deliver the 'unified commerce experience' the platform is designed to anchor.
The flatlined $2,556–$2,807 monthly consumption band confirms Commerce Cloud is running a single static workload rather than serving as an enterprise B2B commerce backbone — Lubrizol is using the platform as a narrow-scope storefront, leaving catalog expansion, segment personalization, and AI-driven commerce innovation capabilities dormant.
At a projected $32K full-year run rate against a $203K budget, ~84% of contracted Commerce Cloud capacity is unused — this is not a licensing overspend issue but an architectural under-deployment where the LoB has paid for enterprise-scale commerce but operationalized only a single-site footprint.
The absence of complementary CX products (Sales, Service, Marketing, CDC) means Commerce Cloud is running without identity, personalization, or post-purchase service integration — Lubrizol's B2B buyers are transacting in a silo, blocking the cross-sell, retention, and customer insight outcomes that justify a Commerce Cloud investment.
Run a Commerce Cloud storefront expansion workshop to identify additional B2B catalogs, business units, and regional sites that can consume the ~$171K unused budget ceiling.
Build a CX architecture roadmap that pairs Commerce Cloud with at minimum a customer data and service capability so the storefront is not operating as an isolated transactional node.
Conduct a technical utilization review of the current Commerce Cloud tenant to confirm whether the flat ~$2,800/month pattern reflects a single limited-scope deployment and define the onboarding path for additional Lubrizol business units.
Engage Lubrizol Customer Experience leadership on an AI-enabled commerce use case (product discovery, personalization, or guided selling) to activate the 'future ready with AI' capability that is currently contracted but unused.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
99.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$454K
YTD Budget$455K
YTD ACV: $528K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $1.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay backbone is operationally lopsided — Ariba Buying, Buying & Invoicing, Sourcing, and Contracts are all active, but Ariba Catalog is effectively dormant at 6% attainment, meaning Lubrizol is transacting and invoicing without channeling spend through pre-negotiated catalog content, which leaks the compliance and savings value that Sourcing and Contracts are architecturally meant to lock in.
Ariba Sourcing's 43% May drop combined with Ariba Contracts running as flat ratable burn signals the strategic sourcing-to-contract handoff is weakening — fewer sourcing events upstream means fewer negotiated agreements feeding the contract repository, eroding the governance layer that should discipline the very Buying and Invoicing volumes now running 110%+ of plan.
The Travel & Expense estate is structurally undersized across every Concur component (Expense, ExpenseIt, Reporting Services, Company Bill Statement volatility, Preferred Success, and a 295% overrun on Concur-Other), indicating T&E adoption has matured into steady-state enterprise usage but the commercial envelope was scoped to a pilot-era footprint, putting end-of-year service continuity at risk.
Document & Reporting Compliance consumption at 899% of May budget against a back-loaded plan shows the statutory e-invoicing and VAT reporting capability is already operating as a production compliance backbone, but the planned Aug–Dec ramp implies additional country or entity rollouts that have not yet been architecturally enabled.
Launch a catalog activation program tied to Ariba Sourcing and Contracts outcomes so that negotiated suppliers and pricing flow into Ariba Catalog and are enforced through Ariba Buying, closing the compliance loop across the P2P stack.
Diagnose the May Ariba Sourcing decline and rebuild the RFX/auction pipeline with category managers to restore upstream event volume that feeds Ariba Contracts, protecting the governance layer over the rising Buying & Invoicing transaction base.
Open a consolidated Concur capacity and true-forward conversation covering Expense, ExpenseIt, Reporting Services, Preferred Success, and Concur-Other to right-size the contracted ceiling to the demonstrated enterprise run rate before multiple components breach ACV simultaneously.
Rebaseline the Document & Reporting Compliance plan with Lubrizol's tax and finance teams and confirm the specific countries, document types, and legal entities targeted in the Aug–Dec ramp so enablement is sequenced ahead of statutory go-lives.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
92.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$280K
YTD Budget$301K
YTD ACV: $329K
Projected Consumed: $672K · Projected Budget: $731K · Projected ACV: $790K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lubrizol's HCM suite is architecturally complete on paper but operationally bifurcated — Employee Central is fully licensed as the system of record while the talent layer (Performance, Compensation, Career Development, Onboarding) is consistently underutilized at 66–85% attainment, meaning the core HR data foundation is paid for but the talent processes meant to monetize it are dormant.
The talent-to-pay value chain is broken at both ends — Onboarding at 31.9% attainment means new hires aren't entering the SuccessFactors journey systematically, while Compensation's five-month decline signals merit and bonus cycles are bypassing the integrated worksheets, severing the architectural link from Performance and Goals data to executed pay decisions.
Career and Talent Development's flat 66% consumption against a healthy Learning footprint indicates skills capture and career pathing are disconnected from training delivery — Lubrizol is running compliance learning but not converting completions into skill profiles, mentoring, or development plans, which collapses the skills-driven talent architecture into a transactional LMS.
Preferred Success is running 16% over ceiling while every functional module underperforms — the success service is being consumed at maximum capacity to sustain steady-state operations rather than drive expansion, signaling Lubrizol is using premium advisory bandwidth on run-the-business support instead of adoption acceleration.
Reposition Preferred Success SFSF engagement away from operational triage and toward a structured activation program targeting the dormant talent stack (Career Development, Onboarding, Compensation), using the existing overrun as evidence to expand entitlement with adoption-linked deliverables.
Re-anchor the Onboarding process to SuccessFactors by auditing which hire steps at Lubrizol are happening outside the system and reactivating new hire portal, task workflows, and document e-signature so Employee Central receives a complete hire-to-productive data trail.
Launch an integrated talent cycle review connecting Performance and Goals outputs to Compensation worksheets and Career Development plans, ensuring goal calibration data flows into merit planning and skill profiles rather than ending at the review form.
Validate Employee Central module activation (position management, time off, global assignments) against entitlement to confirm whether the flat $12,626 monthly draw represents full core HR scope or hidden dormant capacity that should be powering the talent layer.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$6K
YTD Budget$6K
YTD ACV: $6K
Projected Consumed: $14K · Projected Budget: $16K · Projected ACV: $14K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lubrizol's Supply Chain Management footprint on SAP is architecturally narrow — Business Network Commerce Automation is the sole active capability, meaning the LoB has electronic document exchange with suppliers but no surrounding sourcing, contract, or supply planning fabric to govern what flows through that network.
The perfectly flat $1,193 monthly draw on Business Network Commerce Automation indicates the network is operating as a static document conduit rather than an expanding supplier collaboration platform — PO, confirmation, ship notice, and invoice automation are running for a fixed supplier set with no evidence of onboarding momentum or transaction growth.
The $1,328 projected shortfall against a back-half budget step-up to ~$1,531/month exposes that the supplier enablement roadmap underpinning the LPR1037 contract was never operationalized, leaving procurement compliance and end-to-end supplier visibility capped at today's transaction baseline.
Audit the supplier population transacting on Business Network Commerce Automation and identify the next wave of suppliers, spend categories, and document types (ship notices, service entry, invoice) to bring onto the network to convert the fixed subscription into usage-driven value.
Position complementary Supply Chain capabilities — sourcing, contract lifecycle, and supply planning — to Lubrizol so the Business Network is fed by governed sourcing events and contracts rather than operating as an isolated transactional channel.
Build a back-half consumption ramp plan tied to specific supplier onboarding waves and automation flows to close the $1,328 gap and align actual adoption with the contracted $15,641 commitment.
Tropicana Products, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $427K
YTD Budget: $515K
YTD ACV: $495K
Projected Consumed: $1.0M
Projected Budget: $1.3M
Projected ACV: $1.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Tropicana's entire SAP architecture rests on a BTP foundation that is functioning as shelfware — Integration Suite eroding to 80% attainment, Joule Studio at 13.6%, Database Cloud at $0, and CPEA at $0 — which simultaneously starves Data & AI activation, breaks Finance extensibility, and leaves Supply Chain and Process Intelligence without the integration fabric to close their respective loops, meaning every domain-level gap traces back to the same unactivated platform spine.
The customer has bought a complete design-to-decision value chain (Integrated Product Development → IBP → Datasphere → SAC BI/Planning → Signavio Process Intelligence) but every handoff point in that chain is dormant or decaying, leaving Tropicana with a juice business that cannot connect innovation pipeline to demand plan, plan to actuals, or actuals to process conformance — collapsing the cross-domain analytics story that justified the enterprise commitment.
Across L1 domains the pattern is identical — semantic/modeling layers (Datasphere, Process Management, SAC Planning, Concur Expense) are live while their activation, mining, and consumption counterparts (DI Cloud, Process Intelligence, CPEA, Reporting Services, ExpenseIt) are flat or reversing, indicating Tropicana has invested in authoring capability without operational throughput, so the architecture produces artifacts but not business outcomes.
Holistic contract posture shows a heavy ratable burn concentrated in IBP (~$490K annual, flat $40,843/month suggesting subscription-only recognition) while discretionary-consumption products across BTP, Signavio, Concur, and FP&A are collectively trending to a multi-tens-of-thousands shortfall, meaning Tropicana's renewal defense will rest almost entirely on an unproven IBP value case with no surrounding ecosystem of demonstrated workloads.
Convene a single enterprise architecture review with Tropicana that maps Integrated Product Development → IBP → Datasphere → SAC → Signavio as one end-to-end juice innovation-to-conformance value stream and identifies the specific Integration Suite interfaces required to wire it together.
Launch a BTP activation program treating Database Cloud, Joule Studio, CPEA, and Integration Suite as one platform commitment with a named Tropicana platform owner and a workload pipeline that ties each dormant entitlement to a concrete LoB use case in Finance, Supply Chain, or Data & AI.
Validate IBP's $204K YTD consumption as active planning versus ratable burn by pulling planner telemetry and S&OP cycle evidence, then anchor the remaining $285,904 to a demand sensing and innovation-integration use case that pulls Integrated Product Development data into the live planning engine.
Run a cross-domain adoption diagnostic across SAC BI, Concur, and Process Intelligence to determine whether the synchronized May disengagement reflects a shared Tropicana organizational change (finance reorg, headcount, or PepsiCo carve-out dynamics) rather than isolated product issues, so remediation targets the root cause not the symptoms.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
96.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$113K
YTD Budget$118K
YTD ACV: $91K
Projected Consumed: $272K · Projected Budget: $294K · Projected ACV: $217K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Data & AI stack is architecturally bifurcated: the semantic data layer (Datasphere) and platform runtime (BTP BDC) are live and pacing at or above plan, but the downstream activation layers — Data Intelligence Cloud for pipelines, SAC BI for visualization, and the Database Cloud BTP tenant — are all decaying or dormant, meaning Tropicana is paying to curate AI-ready data it cannot operationalize into business decisions.
The Database Cloud BTP entitlement sitting at $0 consumed across five months indicates the database/persistence foundation intended to underpin Datasphere and AI workloads has never been activated, leaving the data platform architecturally incomplete and forcing Datasphere to operate without its companion data tier.
SAC BI's 27% three-month consumption decline is a reverse-adoption signal on the only business-facing consumption layer in this stack — the trusted, semantically modeled data Datasphere is producing has no growing audience, breaking the line of sight from data investment to LoB decision-making.
Data Intelligence Cloud's three-month attainment slide (105% → 76%) signals the integration and orchestration capability that feeds Datasphere is losing workloads, which will progressively starve the semantic layer of fresh pipelines and erode the AI-ready data promise the rest of the stack depends on.
Activate the Database Cloud Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement with a kickoff workshop tied to a concrete Datasphere or AI workload so the persistence tier underpinning the data layer comes online rather than expiring unused.
Run a SAC BI persona and dashboard diagnostic to identify which user cohorts disengaged after February and rebuild the consumption path from Datasphere's semantic models into business-facing analytics.
Convene a Data Intelligence Cloud pipeline review with Tropicana's data engineering team to identify the dormant workloads behind the Apr–May drop and onboard at least one new integration use case feeding Datasphere.
Reconcile the BDC Public BTP overage trajectory against the Datasphere, DI Cloud, and SAC BI underconsumption to confirm whether platform growth is being driven by sanctioned Data & AI workloads or by services outside the intended architecture.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
64.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$17K
YTD Budget$27K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $42K · Projected Budget: $66K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Technology Foundation is contracted as a unified BTP platform but is operating as three disconnected, underused slices — Integration Suite is eroding (14% MoM decline, 80% projected attainment), Joule Studio is barely touched (13.6% attainment), and core BTP services are declining three of four months (62.6% attainment) — meaning the LoB has paid for an extension, integration, and AI fabric but is running none of them at production scale.
Integration Suite is the only workload with meaningful consumption yet it is shrinking, which architecturally signals that the iPaaS backbone connecting SAP and non-SAP systems for Tropicana is losing interfaces rather than gaining them — a leading indicator that downstream process automation and data flows are reverting to point-to-point or manual handoffs.
Joule Studio's flat $188/month plateau shows the AI/agent development capability is provisioned but no LoB use cases have been built on it, leaving Tropicana without the generative extensibility layer that justifies the enterprise agreement and blocking any roadmap toward embedded AI in finance, supply chain, or CX processes.
The combined ~$11,900 projected annual shortfall across all three BTP slices indicates the Enterprise Agreement is functioning as shelfware rather than a strategic platform commitment, exposing Tropicana to a renewal conversation where SAP cannot defend consumed value against contracted value.
Convene a BTP architecture review with Tropicana to map Integration Suite's declining interfaces, identify which integrations were decommissioned or migrated off, and re-platform at least two high-volume integrations to halt the erosion toward 80% attainment.
Launch a Joule Studio activation track anchored on one or two concrete LoB agent or extension use cases (e.g., finance close assistant, supply chain exception agent) to convert the dormant $12,960 capacity into a production AI workload.
Assign a named Tropicana platform owner accountable for monthly BTP consumption across Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and foundation services, with a workload pipeline tied to the $11,900 projected gap.
Build a consolidated BTP use-case roadmap mapping Tropicana's stated business initiatives to specific Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and foundation services so the Enterprise Agreement is defended by demonstrable workload coverage rather than aggregate spend.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
52.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$55K
YTD Budget$105K
YTD ACV: $123K
Projected Consumed: $131K · Projected Budget: $257K · Projected ACV: $296K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Business Transformation stack is architecturally half-wired — Process Management (modeling/design) is running above plan while Process Intelligence (data-driven process mining) just had a quarter of consumption reversed, meaning the LoB can document target processes but has lost the closed-loop ability to measure conformance and quantify improvement against them.
The May -$18,530 reversal on Process Intelligence wiped out a Jan–Apr attainment climb from 79.4% to 89.7%, indicating the analyst community and mining workloads were genuinely active — the adoption muscle exists but the consumption signal is now distorted, masking true transformation ROI to the business.
Process Management consumption is projected to overrun the $2,258 annual budget by ~18% with May ACV expanded to $1,489, showing the modeling layer has crossed from pilot into operational use by named process owners — entitlement headroom, not adoption, is now the binding constraint on continued business process design activity.
Reconcile the -$18,530 May entry on SAP Signavio Process Intelligence with billing operations to determine whether it is a credit, correction, or true usage collapse, and restore an accurate consumption baseline before drawing adoption conclusions.
Reconnect Process Intelligence mining outputs to the Process Management models being authored so conformance, bottleneck, and variant findings feed directly into the to-be designs, closing the transformation loop the two products are architected to deliver jointly.
Reforecast Process Management entitlement against the $1,489 May ACV ceiling and decide with the LoB whether to formally expand the contract or throttle modeling activity, so process design work is not capped by commercial limits.
Re-engage the Process Intelligence user base to rebuild toward the 89.7% April attainment trajectory and close the $125,941 remaining annual budget gap by expanding active investigations into additional Tropicana business units.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
63.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$37K
YTD Budget$59K
YTD ACV: $73K
Projected Consumed: $89K · Projected Budget: $152K · Projected ACV: $175K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Finance & Spend Management architecture is fundamentally a Travel & Expense deployment with everything else dormant or decaying — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, and Reporting Services are all contracted as an integrated T&E stack (capture → submission → analytics) but each is in synchronized decline, indicating the entire expense management process is losing operational traction, not just one tool.
The Treasury architecture is contracted but never scaled — Multi Bank Connect is designed as a centralized hub eliminating point-to-point bank integrations, yet flat $71/month consumption and a May reversal show only a single bank feed is live, leaving Tropicana with none of the multi-bank consolidation, SWIFT gpi tracking, or payment centralization value the product was bought to deliver.
The FP&A layer is architecturally incomplete — SAC Planning is live but plateauing on a single use case while CPEA sits at $0 consumption, meaning Tropicana has planning models without the extensibility platform to integrate them into broader finance data flows, capping the planning investment at its initial footprint.
Concur ExpenseIt's flat consumption against a rising budget indicates the mobile receipt-capture front-end is not being rolled out to new employee segments, which architecturally starves the downstream Expense and Reporting Services tools of submission volume and explains their parallel decline.
Treat the Concur T&E stack as a single adoption recovery program rather than three separate products, rebuilding submission volume in Expense and ExpenseIt first so Reporting Services has the data foundation to regain analytical value.
Convene a Treasury architecture review with Tropicana to confirm whether Multi Bank Connect is intended as the enterprise bank connectivity hub, then sequence onboarding of additional bank relationships and SWIFT gpi tracking to realize the centralized integration model.
Pair the CPEA activation workshop with the SAC Planning expansion plan so the first CPEA workload directly extends planning into workforce, sales, or capex models, converting two dormant investments into one integrated FP&A capability.
Audit dormant SAC Planning licenses and Concur user provisioning together to determine whether the parallel disengagement reflects a shared organizational change at Tropicana (e.g., headcount, policy, or finance reorg) rather than isolated product issues.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
99.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$205K
YTD Budget$207K
YTD ACV: $209K
Projected Consumed: $491K · Projected Budget: $496K · Projected ACV: $501K
Solution Insights and Actions
Tropicana's Supply Chain Management architecture has its planning backbone (IBP) contracted but operationally inert — the perfectly flat $40,843/month burn against a $490,119 annual commitment indicates ratable license recognition, not active demand sensing, inventory optimization, or S&OP cycle execution, which means the juice supply chain is paying for a planning engine while likely still running forecasts and supply response outside the system.
The product innovation-to-supply-chain bridge is broken — Integrated Product Development is at 16.4% YTD attainment ($379 of $2,305) with consumption declining month over month, meaning the architectural handoff between beverage formulation, BOM, and manufacturing feasibility is not flowing through SAP, leaving R&D and supply planning disconnected at the design stage.
Together these two dormant capabilities sever the upstream half of the SCM value chain for Tropicana — new product designs are not feeding into IBP's demand and supply plans, so even if planners did stress IBP, they would be planning against a stale portfolio rather than the live innovation pipeline that drives juice category growth.
Validate real IBP adoption by pulling planner login counts, planning area activity, and S&OP cycle telemetry to confirm whether the $204,215 consumed YTD reflects active demand and supply planning or subscription-only burn, and target the remaining $285,904 for a value-realization plan anchored on demand sensing and inventory optimization for the juice portfolio.
Convene Tropicana's R&D and manufacturing leads to activate Integrated Product Development's BOM, compliance, and handover-to-manufacturing capabilities on a live beverage formulation use case, converting the $4,658 unconsumed ACV into a working design-to-supply bridge.
Establish an architectural integration checkpoint between Integrated Product Development and IBP so that new product introductions and recipe changes propagate into demand and supply planning, closing the upstream gap that currently isolates innovation from the planning engine.
VS Service Company, LLC
Professional services
YTD Consumed: $276K
YTD Budget: $299K
YTD ACV: $257K
Projected Consumed: $663K
Projected Budget: $711K
Projected ACV: $616K
EA Insights and Actions
Integration Suite is the de facto connective tissue across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance domains yet is simultaneously running ~133% hot in BTP and sitting dormant in Finance's EA — meaning the same integration backbone that should close the loop from Ariba sourcing to S/4 to BDC data products is both throttling under load and architecturally absent where it is most needed for procurement-to-analytics traceability.
Across all three L1 areas, the consumption-side workloads (BDC data fabric at 76% over budget, Joule Studio at 133%, Ariba Buying at 89.5%) are running ahead of contract while their corresponding BTP extension and platform pools sit at or near $0 activation — a structural pattern showing the customer is operationalizing SAP outputs without the clean-core extensibility layer, forcing customizations into the core and blocking the agentic AI and process-automation outcomes the data and AI investments are meant to deliver.
The customer's architectural ambition spans strategic sourcing, AI-driven decisioning, and a unified data fabric, but Ariba Sourcing at 42% attainment combined with dormant BDC Public capacity means the negotiated-spend signals that should feed BDC analytics and Joule-powered category intelligence never reach the data layer, leaving the enterprise with transactional throughput but no closed-loop savings or AI-driven procurement intelligence.
At the enterprise contract level, ~$49K of BDC Public, the full Finance EA commit, and the Technology Foundation Other allocation are unconsumed while Joule, Integration Suite, and BDC are collectively trending tens of thousands over budget — indicating the customer's EA portfolio is misallocated rather than under-invested, and a portfolio rebalance could fund production growth without net new spend.
Convene a cross-domain integration architecture review anchored on Integration Suite as the shared backbone for Ariba-to-S/4, S/4-to-BDC, and Joule-to-LoB flows, true-sizing the runtime to the combined demand from Finance, Data & AI, and Technology Foundation rather than treating each domain's integration need in isolation.
Launch a unified BTP activation workshop covering the dormant BDC Public, Technology Foundation Other, and Finance EA capacity to identify the first extension workloads — agentic sourcing, Ariba-to-BDC spend analytics, and Joule-embedded process extensions — that convert unused entitlement into clean-core operational outcomes.
Initiate a consolidated enterprise true-up and reallocation across BDC, Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Database Cloud, anchored to a workload inventory that ties each overage to a documented business outcome and redirects dormant EA capacity to fund the rebalance.
Establish a single enterprise consumption governance cadence spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance to manage the customer's SAP estate as one architectural portfolio, ending the pattern of isolated overages alongside stranded contracted capacity.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
86.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$34K
YTD Budget$39K
YTD ACV: $53K
Projected Consumed: $81K · Projected Budget: $96K · Projected ACV: $128K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI architecture is bifurcated: SAP Business Data Cloud is the entrenched analytical backbone running at ~$4,897/month — nearly double its contracted ceiling — while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC Public) that should host complementary integration, extension, and application workloads went dark after January, meaning the LoB is consuming data products and AI insights without the platform layer needed to operationalize them into business processes.
Within BDC, the data fabric is live but commercially under-provisioned — consumption has breached the $10,640 ACV by $13,843 and is tracking to ~76% over annual budget, signaling that mission-critical data products and agentic AI workloads are already in production use and exposed to throttling or punitive billing risk before the LoB realizes full value from SAC and Datasphere.
The Database Cloud footprint under the Enterprise Agreement shows healthy, normalizing adoption (attainment compressing from 124% to 107%), confirming that persistence for BDC workloads is real and sticky — but the $1,107 projected overage against an $8,689 budget indicates the database tier was sized to a pilot rather than to the production BDC consumption pattern now in flight.
The dormant BDC Public capacity ($49,122 remaining of $54,269) represents the missing extension and integration surface for BDC — without it, the LoB cannot build the agentic AI and business process scenarios that justify the Business Data Cloud overage, leaving the data fabric investment trapped in analytics rather than driving operational outcomes.
Initiate a true-up on SAP Business Data Cloud sized to the observed ~$4,897/month run rate and anchored to a workload inventory of the data products, pipelines, and SAC/Datasphere use cases driving the overage, so the renewed ceiling reflects production reality.
Launch a reactivation review on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC Public) to diagnose the post-January stall and identify the next integration, extension, or agentic AI scenarios that connect BDC data products to LoB processes against the $49,122 of unused capacity.
Rightsize the Database Cloud allocation within the Enterprise Agreement to the sustained over-consumption pattern, aligning the persistence tier with the production BDC workloads it is now supporting rather than the original pilot baseline.
Establish a unified Data & AI consumption governance cadence across BDC and the Enterprise Agreement so over-consumption in the data fabric and under-consumption in the platform are managed as one architectural portfolio rather than isolated line items.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
120.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$64K
YTD Budget$53K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $154K · Projected Budget: $127K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the foundational consumption vehicle for VS Service Company's Technology Foundation, and the data shows it is powering two very different realities — Joule Studio and Integration Suite are running hot with sustained 109–140% monthly overshoot, while the broader Technology Foundation pool sits at $0 activation, meaning the LoB has concentrated all its platform value into AI tooling and integration runtime while leaving the general-purpose BTP capacity dormant.
Integration Suite overconsumption ($37,481 vs $33,235 YTD, trending to a ~$10,897 annual overage) signals that integration has become the de facto backbone connecting SAP and non-SAP systems for this LoB — this is architecturally healthy but commercially undersized, and without a true-up the integration layer risks throttling or overage penalties that would cascade into every downstream process depending on it.
Joule Studio's ~133% sustained attainment ($26,644 vs $19,036 YTD, projecting $63,946 vs $48,035) indicates the LoB has moved Joule from pilot into embedded production AI workloads faster than the contract anticipated — the business is realizing generative AI value, but the entitlement envelope was sized for experimentation, not the operational footprint now in flight.
The $107 Technology Foundation Other allocation with 87% of budget landing in Jun–Aug and zero current activation reveals a planned BTP extensibility or platform service that was scoped but never operationalized — leaving the LoB without the general-purpose runtime needed to host custom extensions, side-by-side apps, or governance services that typically complement Joule and Integration Suite investments.
Initiate a consolidated Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement true-up covering Joule Studio (~$15,911 projected gap) and Integration Suite (~$10,897 projected gap) to right-size the contract to demonstrated production consumption before overage exposure compounds across both workloads.
Run a workload optimization review on Joule Studio and Integration Suite to separate genuine business-driven growth from inefficient consumption patterns, ensuring the true-up reflects optimized demand rather than locking in unoptimized usage.
Convene an activation workshop for the dormant Technology Foundation Other allocation to identify the first extensibility or platform workload — likely a custom extension or governance service complementing the active Joule and Integration Suite footprint — before the Jun–Aug peak budget window passes unused.
Establish a quarterly BTP consumption governance cadence with VS Service Company to forecast Joule and Integration Suite growth, monitor activation of the Technology Foundation pool, and prevent the recurring pattern of undersized contracts against accelerating platform adoption.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$179K
YTD Budget$207K
YTD ACV: $203K
Projected Consumed: $428K · Projected Budget: $488K · Projected ACV: $488K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay backbone is architecturally live but strategically ungoverned — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is processing transactions at 89.5% attainment while Ariba Sourcing sits at 42%, meaning VS Service Company is paying invoices and cutting POs against suppliers and prices that were never competitively sourced, eroding the savings thesis of the Ariba investment.
Ariba Sourcing's oscillating 15–62% monthly attainment reveals it is being used as an ad-hoc event tool rather than the strategic sourcing engine it is architected to be, which means category strategies, supplier evaluation, and award flows into contracts and S/4 purchasing are not feeding the downstream Buying and Invoicing process with negotiated agreements.
The downward drift in Ariba Buying and Invoicing attainment from 110.8% in January to 87.8% in May indicates supplier enablement and catalog coverage have plateaued, leaving indirect spend categories to leak off-platform and bypassing the three-way match and compliance controls the AP automation architecture is designed to enforce.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is entirely dormant at $0 against a $35 annual commit, meaning the extensibility and integration foundation that would normally host custom workflows, Joule-based sourcing acceleration, and Ariba-to-S/4 integration scenarios is contracted but architecturally absent from the Finance and Spend landscape.
Convene a sourcing-to-contract-to-buying process review to ensure Ariba Sourcing events feed negotiated agreements into Ariba Buying and Invoicing, closing the governance gap between transactional throughput and strategic savings capture.
Launch a supplier enablement and catalog expansion initiative on Ariba Buying and Invoicing targeting the indirect categories driving off-platform leakage, restoring attainment toward the January benchmark.
Establish a templated sourcing cadence in Ariba Sourcing — category waves, recurring RFx, and supplier onboarding events — to convert event-driven spikes into embedded process adoption across the remaining fiscal year.
Run a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop to identify the first integration or extension workload — ideally an Ariba-to-S/4 or Joule sourcing scenario — that anchors the platform into the Finance and Spend architecture.

Industry Insights
Cargill leads this Agribusiness cohort on platform activation — BTP Data & AI (BDC +36%), Integration Suite (107%), and Joule (118%) are all in production overage, while CHS, Corteva, Land O'Lakes, and Coop Regions each show near-zero activation of foundational BTP/CPEA entitlements, meaning Cargill is the only peer operating SAP as an enterprise platform rather than disconnected transactional systems.
Corteva and Cargill face the most acute commercial exposure in the cohort — Corteva's IBP is projecting a ~$987K overage and Integration Suite a ~$165K overage, while Cargill's BDC, Commerce, IBP, and Concur stack collectively overshoot by ~$400K, yet both customers hold dormant platform agreements (Ariba Cloud Platform EA at $0 for Cargill, three BTP CPEAs at $0 for Corteva) that could absorb workloads if activated, making their contract portfolios structurally misaligned with actual consumption.
Land O'Lakes and Coop Regions are the most architecturally fragmented in the peer group — Land O'Lakes runs Ariba Buying at 108% while Fieldglass sits at 1.1% and Integration Suite is dormant, and Coop operates four planning/analytics islands (IBP, SAC Planning, Datasphere, Integration Suite) with no visible ERP backbone, meaning both cooperatives are paying enterprise-platform prices for point-solution value and cannot deliver the member-trust traceability or producer-to-finance closed-loop planning their business models require.
A shared 'execution without intelligence' anti-pattern spans the entire cohort — CHS runs Employee Central at 96% but SAC Planning at 8%, Corteva runs IBP at 158% but SAC BI at 0.2%, Land O'Lakes runs Ariba Buying at 108% but Spend Analysis is dormant, and Coop's IBP and SAC Planning are each under 45% realized — meaning all five Agribusiness customers are generating transactional volume without the analytical and decision layers that enable margin management, demand sensing, and supply chain resilience.
Cargill's Concur and Travel & Expense stack demonstrates the strongest steady-state adoption in the cohort (+30% consumption with active Preferred Success), a pattern Corteva partially replicates in Concur (paired with 105.6% Preferred Success), while CHS, Land O'Lakes, and Coop show no comparable domain where enablement and consumption are both healthy — making Cargill and Corteva's T&E adoption the reference architecture for scaling success services to dormant domains.
Run a cohort-wide BTP/CPEA activation sprint targeting CHS ($5,960 Tech Foundation at $0), Corteva (three platform entitlements at $0), Land O'Lakes (Integration Suite near-zero), and Coop Regions (Joule Studio and BTP CPEA dormant) to stand up the foundational extensibility layer that all four customers have contracted but none has operationalized.
Convene a joint Cargill-Corteva commercial rebalancing session to consolidate Cargill's ~$400K multi-domain overages and Corteva's ~$1.15M IBP/Integration overage into right-sized FY27 enterprise agreements that align contracted envelopes to documented workload trajectories before mid-year capacity exhaustion.
Launch a cross-cohort 'closed-loop planning' workshop for CHS, Corteva, Coop Regions, and Land O'Lakes that sequences Datasphere semantic models, SAC BI/Planning, and IBP into one connected decisioning fabric, directly addressing the analytics dormancy that blocks harvest-aligned margin management, S&OP scenario modeling, and data-driven crop-portfolio decisions.
Redirect underutilized Preferred Success and advisory capacity at CHS (119% consumed against dormant modules) and Corteva (Ariba advisory surplus) toward activating the intelligence layers — SAC Planning, Signavio, Spend Analysis, SAC BI — that justify each customer's enterprise contract value.
Escalate to Land O'Lakes and Coop Regions executive sponsors a single architectural recovery narrative tying dormant platform investments to cooperative-critical outcomes (member analytics, grower payments traceability, producer-to-finance planning) to secure governance commitment that prevents further fragmentation of their estates.

Industry Insights
Tenneco Automotive is the sole customer in this peer cohort and establishes the baseline as a laggard — with 34% overall consumption ($595,921 of $1.76M budget), near-zero BTP/CPEA activation across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance, and $0 consumption on C4C, the company has purchased a composable, AI-ready SAP stack but operates it as disconnected point solutions, a critical deficiency for an automotive supplier facing margin pressure and supply chain volatility.
Tenneco's integration architecture is the most dormant capability in this automotive portfolio — Integration Suite sits at zero activation, which means the hire-to-retire, demand-to-supply, and lead-to-cash flows essential for automotive supplier agility remain broken end-to-end, leaving SuccessFactors, IBP, C4C, and finance as isolated islands.
Tenneco's only active domains — SuccessFactors (declining talent modules) and IBP (flat at 64% attainment with $12.3K unconsumed) — are regressing because the BTP fabric designed to connect workforce planning to demand signals and margin scenarios was never stood up, blocking the closed-loop supply-and-cost visibility automotive Tier 1 suppliers require.
Tenneco exhibits a systemic onboarding-and-governance failure rather than isolated adoption gaps — the pattern of zero consumption across CPEA Data & AI ($71 contract), CPEA Finance ($7 contract), and C4C ($12.7K contract) signals that no enterprise architecture owner is governing cross-domain activation, a structural risk that will persist without executive intervention.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with Tenneco's CIO and LoB sponsors to consolidate all BTP/CPEA entitlements into a single governed consumption plan tied to named automotive workloads such as supply resilience dashboards, workforce-to-cost integration, and margin transparency scenarios.
Launch a 'backbone first' integration activation program at Tenneco that stands up Integration Suite as the connective fabric between SuccessFactors, IBP, C4C, and S/4 — prioritizing demand-to-supply and hire-to-onboard iFlows that directly impact automotive delivery performance.
Establish a quarterly enterprise consumption governance forum at Tenneco covering all L1 domains to convert at-risk ACV across C4C, CPEA, IBP, and SuccessFactors into a sequenced activation backlog with measurable business outcomes tied to automotive KPIs.
Run a cross-domain architecture workshop at Tenneco demonstrating how IBP demand signals, SuccessFactors workforce planning, and SAC margin scenarios can close the supply-and-labor loop on BTP — using automotive-specific use cases like shift-level labor costing and component shortage impact modeling to build executive urgency.

Industry Insights
BTP platform activation is a universal failure across all three chemicals customers — H B Fuller shows zero consumption on every BTP entitlement, Stepan has $0 zero-starts on CPEA across two domains, and Lubrizol is burning Integration Suite at 15x without tenant governance — meaning the clean-core extensibility and AI fabric required for chemicals-specific outcomes like formulation management, batch traceability, and regulatory compliance automation does not exist in any account despite being contracted in all three.
Lubrizol leads this chemicals cohort on transactional execution volume but trails on architectural governance — Integration Suite at 15x and Joule Studio at 510% demonstrate production workloads running at scale while H B Fuller's Integration Suite is contracting and Stepan's is declining 17%, yet Lubrizol's lack of consumption planning means its integration and AI spend is unmanaged compared to the dormant-but-sized portfolios at H B Fuller and Stepan.
Signavio process intelligence is broken or absent across the entire chemicals cohort — H B Fuller shows 27% attainment with a May reversal, Stepan has no documented process mining activation, and Lubrizol covers only one process — leaving all three companies unable to identify margin leakage, batch variance, or supplier performance bottlenecks in their high-volume specialty and formulated chemicals operations.
H B Fuller has the most comprehensive contracted portfolio (S/4HANA Private + Public, Signavio, Ariba, Concur, BTP) but the lowest effective utilization, with blended attainment under 50% and Preferred Success functioning as passive support rather than adoption lever, placing it behind both Stepan and Lubrizol on realized business value despite higher contract sophistication.
The source-to-pay process chain is fragmented identically across all three customers — H B Fuller runs Ariba Buying at 96% without Contracts, Stepan has dormant Sourcing and Catalog while Buying contracts, and Lubrizol shows Buying at 110%+ while Catalog/Sourcing weakens — meaning none has the integrated procurement visibility required for chemicals industry supplier resilience, raw material cost management, and contract compliance.
Launch a chemicals-industry BTP activation sprint across all three customers, positioning the platform as the required foundation for formulation data management, regulatory reporting automation, and supplier integration — treating H B Fuller's shelfware, Stepan's zero-starts, and Lubrizol's ungoverned burn as three expressions of the same unresolved architectural gap.
Run a cohort-wide Signavio process mining engagement targeting chemicals-specific value streams — batch-to-cash for H B Fuller, source-to-pay for Stepan, and margin-to-insight for Lubrizol — to establish the execution visibility layer that is absent across all three accounts.
Convene Preferred Success entitlement reviews at H B Fuller and Stepan to redirect passive support spend into active adoption interventions on declining modules, using Lubrizol's high-burn integration workloads as a reference pattern for what engaged platform consumption looks like.
Facilitate a cross-customer peer exchange between Lubrizol (demonstrating production-scale integration and AI workloads) and H B Fuller/Stepan (demonstrating contracted-but-dormant platform capacity) to accelerate adoption learning and establish chemicals-industry reference architectures for clean-core extensibility.
Initiate commercial rebaselining conversations with all three customers before FY27 planning — netting H B Fuller's shelfware against its transformation ambitions, aligning Stepan's commitment to its actual operational footprint, and redirecting Lubrizol's dormant entitlements toward its demonstrated integration and AI center of gravity.

Industry Insights
BTP platform dormancy is the defining architectural failure across this Consumer Products cohort — Molson Coors ($15K at 0%), Lancaster Colony (~$3.5K at 0%), Ingredion ($5K at 0% across three separate CPEAs), S.C. Johnson (zero-to-flat across all L1s), and Tropicana (Integration Suite eroding, Joule at 13.6%, Database Cloud at $0) have all contracted the integration, extension, and AI fabric their SAP estates depend on but none has activated it, meaning eleven CPG companies are running disconnected SaaS islands without the governed backbone required for Joule, clean-core extensibility, or cross-domain automation.
Kraft Heinz leads the cohort on AI experimentation velocity with Joule Studio at 433% and BTP Public at 1326% attainment, but this positions them as the highest-risk profile because these workloads are scaling on experiment-sized envelopes with zero Database Cloud or SAC BI activation — Kraft Heinz is building shadow AI while peers like Conagra and Mondelez have not yet started, making Kraft Heinz the urgent governance intervention rather than the adoption exemplar.
Conagra and Mondelez share the strongest integration consumption in the cohort (Integration Suite at 175% and 124% respectively), but both exhibit the identical architectural anti-pattern of hot integration pipes feeding dormant analytics and foundation layers — Conagra's SAC BI sits at 41% with SAC Planning declining four months straight, while Mondelez's Business Data Cloud and BTP EA are flatlined, meaning the two largest CPG players by SAP footprint have built data plumbing without decisioning surfaces, blocking the margin defense and demand sensing outcomes the industry requires.
Domino's Concur T&E stack is the only closed-loop architecture operating at full maturity in the entire cohort — capture, digitize, analyze, and over-consume against contract — proving industrialized SAP adoption is achievable when business process wiring is complete, a pattern conspicuously absent from Domino's own Procurement and Supply Chain domains and from every other customer's BTP and analytics stacks.
The CPG-critical Source-to-Pay-to-Plan value chain is broken at the same architectural seam across Domino's (Contracts at 66%, Sourcing down 34%, IBP flat), Molson Coors (Contracts at 24% vs Sourcing at 73%), and Ingredion (Buying & Invoicing at 25.5% while Spend Analysis runs at 240%) — three major food and beverage manufacturers cannot close the loop from sourcing commitment to supplier collaboration to demand planning, exposing ingredient margin and supply resiliency to uncontracted leakage in a commodity-volatile environment.
Launch a coordinated BTP activation sprint across Molson Coors, Lancaster Colony, Ingredion, S.C. Johnson, and Tropicana, designating one named platform owner per customer and sequencing Integration Suite as the anchor workload that unblocks Joule, Database Cloud, and cross-domain automation for all five dormant estates.
Convene a Kraft Heinz AI governance intervention to right-size the runaway Joule Studio and BTP Public consumption against demonstrated baselines, activate Database Cloud and SAC BI as the sanctioned data layer, and establish the API control plane that prevents shadow AI from touching finance, procurement, and supply chain processes without architectural guardrails.
Codify Domino's Concur closed-loop enablement pattern into a reusable adoption-architecture playbook and deploy it first at Conagra (SAC BI/Planning), Mondelez (BDC-to-SAC-to-Process Management), and Tropicana (Datasphere-to-SAC-to-Process Intelligence) to convert integration throughput into governed decisioning surfaces.
Run a cross-customer Source-to-Pay-to-Plan integration workshop for Domino's, Molson Coors, and Ingredion that treats Ariba Sourcing-to-Contracts, Buying-to-Invoicing, and IBP forecast collaboration as one broken supplier value chain, reconnecting the architectural handoffs that protect food cost and supply continuity for commodity-exposed CPG operators.
Execute portfolio-level commercial true-ups at Kraft Heinz, Conagra, and Mondelez that rebalance the combined ~$1.5M+ of transactional overruns (Ariba, Integration Suite, PaPM, SAC BI) against the comparable value of dormant strategic entitlements (Database Cloud, BTP EA, Supplier Risk, Business Data Cloud), converting line-item remediation into single enterprise rebalancing conversations that fund activation roadmaps.

Industry Insights
BTP Integration Suite is the single most critical shared gap across all six life sciences and healthcare customers — AbbVie ($82K dormant), Abbott (overconsuming at 107% without foundational data layer), Cardinal Health (zero activation), Eli Lilly (declining to 89%), Hill-Rom (no integration backbone), and Medtronic (eroding to 49% with $0 on new SKUs) — meaning the entire $17.8M ACV cohort lacks the connected integration fabric required for GxP compliance automation, supplier traceability, and agentic AI, which are table-stakes capabilities for regulated pharma and MedTech manufacturers.
Abbott Laboratories leads this cohort on integration throughput (107-113% Integration Suite) and incentive execution (99.8% Incentive Management) but ranks last on foundational data activation with HANA Cloud at 65% and SAP Build dormant, while Eli Lilly leads on AI experimentation (Joule Studio at 172%, Database Cloud at 136%) but has the weakest compliance spine with Batch Release Hub at 2.4% and Process Intelligence at 17% — both customers are scaling consumption at capability edges while leaving the data and compliance core architecturally hollow.
Eli Lilly and AbbVie share a pharma-critical GxP compliance failure — Batch Release Hub sits at 2.4% at Lilly and $0 at AbbVie while Traceability Hub plateaus — leaving both companies operating paper-based batch disposition processes that expose manufacturing operations to FDA warning letters, EU MDR audit failures, and product recall risk that the contracted architecture was explicitly sized to eliminate.
Medtronic and Hill-Rom, the two MedTech device manufacturers, show the weakest source-to-pay-to-risk governance in the cohort — Medtronic has $0 consumption on Ariba Supplier Risk and Watchlist Screening while Hill-Rom's Sourcing, Contracts, and Supplier Risk modules are inactive — meaning both regulated device companies are flowing purchase orders and payments against unscreened suppliers, directly violating FDA 21 CFR Part 820 supplier qualification requirements and EU MDR economic operator traceability mandates.
Cardinal Health is the most architecturally fragmented customer in this cohort with 7% Customer Experience attainment ($889K unrealized), 13% Ariba SLP supplier master coverage, and SAP Build dormant — compared to Abbott's 99.8% Incentive Management and Eli Lilly's 172% Joule Studio — revealing a healthcare distributor without the connected commerce-to-supplier-to-spend data backbone required for DSCSA drug traceability compliance and GPO contract management.
Launch a cohort-wide BTP Integration Suite activation program across all six customers, prioritizing AbbVie and Medtronic for GxP and MedTech compliance integration patterns, Abbott for data foundation buildout, and Eli Lilly for reversing declining integration consumption — position Integration Suite as the prerequisite for every customer's agentic AI, clean-core extensibility, and regulatory automation roadmap.
Convene a pharma-specific GxP compliance architecture intervention for AbbVie and Eli Lilly to activate Batch Release Hub against quality release processes and close the loop with Traceability Hub, converting two dormant regulated-pharma capabilities into operating systems of record before year-end FDA and EU inspection cycles.
Run a MedTech supplier governance and risk workshop for Medtronic and Hill-Rom to activate Ariba Supplier Risk, Watchlist Screening, and Contracts as the source-to-pay compliance layer, addressing the immediate regulatory exposure from unscreened third-party flows and positioning supplier traceability as the MDR and FDA audit defense architecture.
Establish a Cardinal Health enterprise architecture reset spanning Commerce Cloud, Ariba SLP, and SAP Build with a single activation backlog and Center of Excellence charter — reallocate the $889K unrealized CX capacity against BTP extensibility use cases that connect supplier qualification to customer fulfillment for DSCSA traceability.
Rebaseline all Preferred Success engagements across the cohort — Abbott, Eli Lilly, Hill-Rom, and Medtronic each show advisory overconsumption against dormant platforms — by tying success entitlements to named platform activation milestones (Commerce Cloud channels, Batch Release Hub sites, Integration Suite connectors, SAP Build apps) rather than ratable time-based accrual.

Industry Insights
Cleveland-Cliffs stands as the sole customer in this mining cohort, establishing the baseline for peer comparison with 30% consumption attainment ($118,904 consumed of $391,921 budget), but this baseline reflects significant underperformance given the strategic four-pillar SAP investment (SAC, Integration Suite, C4C, IBP) that remains operationally dormant across all domains.
The Integration Suite's single-iFlow deployment at Cleveland-Cliffs represents the most critical architectural bottleneck in this mining portfolio — without an activated integration fabric, the company cannot achieve the mill-to-customer digital thread that steel manufacturers require for demand-supply synchronization, margin visibility, and customer responsiveness.
Cleveland-Cliffs exhibits a systemic enterprise architecture governance failure where suite-level procurement delivers only point-solution deployment (SAC=BI only, IBP=demand only, C4C=SFA only), blocking the cross-domain compounding value that justifies SAP standardization and leaving ~$106K of annual contract value at risk of non-consumption.
For a vertically integrated steel producer where competitive advantage depends on tight coordination between raw material sourcing, mill scheduling, and customer delivery commitments, Cleveland-Cliffs' absence of Ariba execution behind IBP and Service/CPQ behind C4C means the SAP estate supports departmental silos rather than integrated operational decision-making.
Convene an enterprise architecture review with Cleveland-Cliffs' CIO positioning Integration Suite activation as the prerequisite unlock for IBP and SAC ROI, mapping explicit data flows from IBP planning through Integration Suite to S/4 and Ariba execution systems.
Establish a quarterly consumption governance forum with Cleveland-Cliffs that tracks YTD attainment across all four L1 domains as a unified architectural health metric, targeting the ~$106K at-risk contract value through coordinated cross-domain activation.
Sponsor a mill-to-customer digital thread workshop for Cleveland-Cliffs that sequences missing adjacent capabilities (Ariba for IBP supply execution, Service Cloud/CPQ for C4C customer engagement, Joule for SAC planning intelligence) into a steel-industry-aligned roadmap prioritized by margin and responsiveness impact.
Mandate Integration Suite as Cleveland-Cliffs' standard integration layer for any new Data & AI, CX, or SCM initiative, with iFlow onboarding KPIs owned by an integration COE to convert the dormant platform into the architectural backbone required for mining industry digital transformation.
